title: no comma space in headers
body:
I expected this to include a space `', '`

```js
fetch('https://httpbin.org/get', {
  headers: [['Foo', 'bar'], ['Foo', 'baz']]
}).then(r => r.json()).then(json => json.headers.Foo.includes(', ')).then(console.log)
```
All browser yields true, deno yields false

What's weird is that 
`new Headers([['foo', 'bar'], ['foo', 'bar']]).get('foo')` includes a `', '` but the request didn't

this works doe:
```js
fetch('https://httpbin.org/get', {
  headers: new Headers([['Foo', 'bar'], ['Foo', 'baz']])
}).then(r => r.json()).then(json => json.headers.Foo.includes(', ')).then(console.log)
```


----

deno 1.16.4 (release, x86_64-apple-darwin)
v8 9.7.106.15
typescript 4.4.2
author: jimmywarting

title: Replace uses of `instanceof` in runtime code with `ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf`
body: The former can be tricked using `Symbol.hasInstance` while the latter can not.
author: lucacasonato

title: `deno repl --eval-file`
body:
an `--eval-file` commandline option may be useful? since `--eval <pasted code>` is little different from `deno repl` then pasting the code in.
plus with longer code and/or code with multiple lines, i think it would quickly become quite unwieldy
author: somebody1234

title: Tweak ICU data build settings to include more locale information
body:
In looking at Deno, I discovered that its `Intl` seems to share Chrome's non-support for Icelandic.

Since both Node and Edge _do_ support Icelandic, it's clearly not a limitation of V8 as such. In Chrome's case, Google appear to specifically disable support for locales that don't have a full interface translation, which seems like a silly thing to do for a browser. Does Deno inherit that butchered ICU for some reason?
author: anarchodin

title: weird caching issue with `deno repl`?
body:
i was getting this weird error previously:
```
$ deno repl --eval 'import {} from \"./cli.ts\"'
error in --eval flag. Uncaught TypeError: Relative import path "cli.ts" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../
```
note the missing `./` in the error message
same happened with this:
```
$ deno repl
> import {a} from './cli.js'
Uncaught TypeError: Module not found "file:///C:/path/to/cli.js".
```
oddly enough, i created a dummy file `cli.js` (empty), tried to import that, and it worked. then i tried to import `cli.ts` without exiting repl, and it worked

unfortunately, i don't think i can repro so feel free to close it i guess?

chances are it was from me ctrl+c-ing when it was typechecking or something.
author: somebody1234

title: bug(ext/crypto) - exportKey JWK in js not base64url
body:
`exportKey(JWK)` still uses JS function `unpaddedBase64` for symmetric keys (`AES-???`, `HMAC`) which is not `base64url` as per [RFC7515](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#page-54).

Failure detected during WPT tests with `wrapKey_unwrapKey`. Strangely, WPT `import_export` cases do not detect this.

author: Im-Beast

title: `deno test` command fails to detect `.mts` test files
body:
The `deno test` command fails to detect test files that have the `.mts` file extension.

For example, with the `foo_test.mts` file present, running:

```
deno test
```

Results in:

```
error: No test modules found
```

The `.mts` file extension needs to be supported for tests so they can properly import `.mjs` ESM containing JSDoc TypeScript types.

author: AnInternetTroll

title: lsp: hover info for a registry URL should include documentation if present
body: The registry v2 allows for providing a documentation endpoint for URL when providing suggestions. If a URL matches one of the enabled registries, the documentation response should be included in the quick info when hovering over the URL.
author: kitsonk

title: docs: Security reporters need a clear reporting policies document such as SECURITY.md
body:
Deno is becoming popular as a secure runtime in the world. However, the procedure for reporting issues leading to vulnerabilities remains unclear. 
Security engineers who discover a vulnerability will first check `SECURITY.md`,  so just having this will help. 
In addition, the limitations on Security Guarantee should be written as clear as possible in security policies. 
There are many discussions whether they are vulnerabilities or not, because many "secure" runtime is not clear at which layer security is guaranteed. 
For example, if all the code is coded by a malicious user, does Deno continue to guarantee the safety of the sandbox? 
rel  #8094  #12058

title: Firebase example not working
body:
I am attempting to get Firebase, specifically Firestore, working with Deno but am running into issues. I've raised this as a [question on StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/70564143/using-firsebase-firestore-with-deno).

These issues seem to be with the compatiblity between the `firebase` module and Deno, as the error even occurs in the [Deno Firebase tutorial](https://deno.com/deploy/docs/tutorial-firebase), and if running the [source code](https://deno.com/examples/firebase.js) directly with `deno run --allow-net https://deno.com/examples/firebase.js`.
author: bicarlsen

title: `deno test --watch` should have a CLI flag for clearing the terminal when testing is restarted
body:
At the moment, if you have a tall terminal window, you can still be peripherally "seeing red" even when your test is passing, because old failures are still visible higher up.

I'm proposing adding a new flag, e.g. `--clear`, making it so that `deno test --watch --clear` will clear the terminal right before the:

>Watcher: File change detected! Restarting!

...message is printed.

There's precedent for providing this feature. I've enjoyed it in a bunch of testing commands over the years (and can try to remember which ones if you want!)
author: frou

title: ffi: Panic on incorrect FFI parameters
body:
# Code
```
const user32 = Deno.dlopen("user32.dll", {
    SystemParametersInfoA: {
        parameters: [
            "u32", 
            "u32",
            "pointer", 
            "u32", 
        ], 
        result: "i32" 
    } 
});

const result = user32.symbols.SystemParametersInfoA(0x0014, null, new TextEncoder().encode("D:/flower1.jpg"), 0x01)
```

# Panic
```
============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: windows x86_64
Version: 1.17.1
Args: ["deno", "run", "--unstable", "--allow-all", "natives.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'Expected ffi arg value to be an unsigned integer', ext\ffi\lib.rs:281:6
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

# Mitigation
In this case, you can replace the `null` in `user32.symbols.Syst...` to 0, to match the symbol's parameters and the panic no longer occurs.

title: Property decorators work differently with `run` and `run --no-check`
author: kt3k

title: cli: when fetching code, use a better accept header
body:
Currently when the CLI attempts to fetch a module it sends an `Accept` header of `*/*`, which isn't actually very accurate, or makes content negotiation for a server very difficult.

The following would be better:

```
Accept: application/javascript, application/typescript, text/javascript, text/typescript, text/jsx, text/tsx, application/json;q=0.9, text/plain;q=0.8, application/octet-stream;q=0.8
```

This roughly follows how we handle it in `MediaType` as well as indicates that we don't just accept anything.  This would allow servers to better negotiate the content being sent to a client.  If we are concerned that the lack of "accept any" might cause regressions, we could go with this instead:

```
Accept: application/javascript, application/typescript, text/javascript, text/typescript, text/jsx, text/tsx, application/json;q=0.9, text/plain;q=0.8, application/octet-stream;q=0.8, */*;q=0.7
```

Which makes it easier for servers who are trying to negotiate content to provide "code" in preference to something like HTML, but still advertises all content is welcome (though it really isn't).

author: ije

title: Emit, source map, and declaration cache in deno dir is racy across multiple processes
body:
I've run into two caching issues while using deno the past two days where I was sure my code was ok, but I was getting type checking errors.

Upon closer inspection, our cache code is racy across multiple processes since the hash is stored in a separate file.

Also, looks like if you kill the process between these two statements then the emit won't be saved correctly and won't ever be reloaded until you change the source text:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/12423e16b7df39b2bf995efd1376ba4b6ef41e02/cli/emit.rs#L458-L459

To solve this, we should store both the version hash along with each artifact in the same file. I've already started working on a fix. Edit: ended up storing an emit hash in the version file.
author: dsherret

title: Deno.serveHttp() & Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br - Feature Request
body:
Most user agents like FireFox, Chrome, etc. send the header `Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br` and `Deno.serveHttp()` does not support any of these compressions, then `Deno.serveHttp()` is only really able to process `Accept-Encoding: identity` which means "without modification or compression".

Since the new server is based on Hyper, could Hyper's ability to `gzip` be implemented in `Deno.serveHttp()`?

Just one little compression mode would be really nice to have. gzip would be ideal.

title: `deno::Flags` should be reference counted
body:
The [`Flags` struct](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1fb5858009f598ce3f917f9f49c466db81f4d9b0/cli/flags.rs#L211-L256) has a lot of data in it and it is cloned multiple times. It makes sense to wrap it in an `Rc`.

For example:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1fb5858009f598ce3f917f9f49c466db81f4d9b0/cli/main.rs#L378

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1fb5858009f598ce3f917f9f49c466db81f4d9b0/cli/main.rs#L572
author: ry

title: cli: console clearing when watching should be an option
body:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/12613 introduced a neat console clear on reload. However this behaviour may be undesirable on multi environments where files are linked (dylib) and watch/reload chained.

Workaround:
```
deno ... 2>&1 | grep -v '\\x1B\[1;1H'
```

author: kt3k

title: improve(ext/ffi) - allow alias for symbol renames to solve Windows DLL import issues.
body:
A number of Microsoft windows DLL's contain two entry points for effectively the same function, with "A" and "W" prefixes to indicate normal (ANSI) or wide strings, for example: [`winscard.h`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/winscard/nf-winscard-scardconnecta)

The windows import headers `.h` automatically select between the two variants. 

Problem: linux/os-x versions of this library do not have these suffixes, thus making the `symbols` hash of `Deno.dlopen` somewhat complicated.

Solution: Add an `alias?: string` to `ForeignFunction`, which if set would indicate the symbol name to be imported from the library. This might also help in other cases, such as imported C++ dlls where the symbol names have been munged by the compiler.

title: `bundle` and `compile` should use options for final output
body:
As of Deno `1.17.x`, `compile` uses `--output` option to _say_ where to place the result from the operation. On the other hand `bundle` makes use of an optional argument.

I know the two _directives_ work different, but since the argument is optional, wouldn't be more aligned with the current behavior of the other commands if `bundle` would just use an option (`--output`) as well, in the same way `compile` does to output the final result?
author: x80486

title: Deno has different permission requirements when using sync import and async import.
body:
For example.ts:

Don't need --allow-read when...
```typescript
import { myLocalFunction } from './my_local_function.ts'
```

Don't need --allow-net when...
```typescript
import { delay } from 'https://deno.land/std/async/delay.ts'
```

Need --allow-read when...
```typescript
const { myLocalFunction } = await import('./my_local_function.ts')
```

Need --allow-net when...
```typescript
const { delay } = await import('https://deno.land/std/async/delay.ts')
```

If someone want to wirte a deno program which need to importing both of above libraries using async import, but don't want to have any other prermission.

They need to run deno with this complicated commend.
```
deno run --allow-read=my_local_function.ts --allow-net=deno.land/std/async/delay.ts example.ts
```

When using sync import, only need...
```
deno run example.ts
```

Is this normal or a bug?

title: node polyfill for vm.runInContext using web workers
body:
<!-- NOTE: for larger scale feature requests like additions to the standard library, consider opening a new discussion under the "Proposals" category over here: https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/discussions/new?category=proposals -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I would like to use JSDom for SSR on Deno. I can't use JSDom directly without its own global context, because I want to run a browser script on it. For that it needs to have an environment for the script where all global browser methods are available, for example window, dom, etc and for security.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

A polyfill for `runInContext` or a Deno native API for running a script file or script string in a sandbox with it's own context and executing it.

I can try to implement this, my approach would be to use the web worker for this, but I would have liked to hear your opinion beforehand. Do you think using web workers for that would be a possible and clean solution?

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

An alternative I tried was to run the script using `Deno.run({ cmd: ['deno', ...] })`, but that had too long loading times and it is difficult to pass the context to it or to communicate with the script. With web workers you could at least use postMessage

author: porsager

title: Proposal: `deno vendor`
body:
## Context

Deno aspires to provide a full featured set of development tools baked into the single `deno` CLI. At the same time, Deno provides an open platform where code can be imported just as easily as a browser can fetch a web page.  Deno stores remote dependencies in a cache commonly referred to as the `DENO_DIR`.  While Deno allows you "relocate" this directory at runtime, and the files in the cache are editable, it does not solve all the use cases in a well supported way.

### Use cases

- As a developer, I want to be able to quickly edit a remote dependency to investigate a coding issue.
- As a developer, I want to be able to store all of my remote dependencies for a given project and check them into my source code to ensure that those dependencies don't change or can't be altered.
- As a developer, I want to be able to develop a workload with the Deno CLI and be able to test it and then deploy it on the edge with Deno Deploy with a fixed set of dependencies, not relying upon availability of other hosts or services for my application.

All of these use cases also are constrained by:

- Without needing to edit or change the code I have authored.
- I want to be able to quickly "revert" if needed.

## Proposed Solution

Add a subcommand to the Deno CLI named `deno vendor`.  The subcommand would work similar to the `deno run` subcommand, in that it would take a target script.  For example:

```
> deno vendor main.ts
```

Would analyse `main.ts` and all of its dependencies.  For those dependencies that are remote dependencies, it would write their source out locally to the `_vendor` path, along with `_vendor/import_map.json`, which would remap all the remote dependencies to local ones.

The concept would then be then that changes could be made to the local "vendored" code and be reflected in the application by simply using the output import map:

```
> deno run --import-map _vendor/import_map.json main.ts
```

(Note: having to be explicit about this import map isn't the _ideal_ design, and suggestions below in other complimentary features would be able to make this just so then all the user would need to do is `deno run main.ts`)

This would also allow specific dependencies to be vendored, without having to vendor everything.  For example if someone wanted to just vendor oak and all of it dependencies, but leave everything else to continue to be remote:

```
> deno vendor https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.1.0/mod.ts
```

Other flags that should be supported:

- `--output` - specify the output path, which defaults to `_vendor` of cwd.
- `--import-map` - specifies an _upstream_ import map that should be used as the base when calculating the vendored import map
- `--config` - specifies a configuration file, which will be considered when vendoring.
- `--info` - output human readable information about the current state of the `_vendor` directory.

Other flags/features that might be supported, or might be added after the "MVP" feature:

- `--ignore` - a glob pattern of dependencies to exclude/ignore
- The ability to remove specific dependencies from `_vendor` (which can be accomplished from editing the `_vendor/import_map.json` directly, but might be unapproachable for some devs)

### Semantics

- Once the remote dependencies are identified, they would be written out in a way to minimise the need of remapping the paths.  For example `https://example.com/pkg/mod.ts` would get written out to `./_vendor/https/example.com/pkg/mod.ts`.  In cases where the remote dependency cannot be written to disk following the simple algorithm, it will be written out in as close to a human readable way as possible.
- Once the "manifest" of the `_vendor` is calculated, the import map will be generated with the smallest set of entries possible.  For example a remote import of `https://example.com/pkg/mod.ts` would get an entry like the following:

  ```json
  {
    "imports": {
      "https://example.com/": "_vendor/example.com/"
    }
  }
  ```

  But if there was something like `https://example.com/pkg/mod.ts` and `https://example.com/pkg/lib?dev` something like the following would be written out:

  ```json
  {
    "imports": {
      "https://example.com/pkg/lib?dev": "_vendor/example.com/pkg/lib_a02vf0.ts",
      "https://example.com/": "_vendor/example.com/"
    }
  }
  ```

- If a `_vendor` or output path already exists, the `_vendor/import_map.json` and output dependencies would be updated.
- A design limitation would be that some dynamic imports that cannot be statically analysable would be missed. A consideration might be to warn on such dependencies being present.
- It is assumed that at this point in time, other state/meta data does not need to be stored, but might need to be. If this is the case, then the state should be stored in the `_vendor/import-map.json` under additional _private_ keys (e.g. `"__deno_vendor": {}`).  The import map specification specifically allows additional keys without invalidating the file, and this would help ensure that the output of `deno vendor` is contained, repeatable and portable.
- When an existing `_vendor` is updated, and for some reason a remote dependency has changed, import map `"scopes"` can be used to allow two different versions of the same dependency to exist in the vendor directory.

### Other features which improve/compliment this feature

- Automatic detection of `deno.json`/`deno.jsonc` - This is already in progress.
- Ability to express an import map in `deno.json`/`deno.jsonc`.  This would allow the feature a) identify an existing import map that should be amended with the vendor mappings as well as "point" the configuration at the vendor import map.
- Add the _Deno: Vendor Dependencies_ to the vscode_deno extension.
- Ability to express and support import maps with Deno Deploy.  This would allow someone who had vendored dependencies to deploy it to Deploy.

### Specific advantages to this proposal

- It aligns to the web platform standards, providing a portable solution that is independent of Deno CLI.
- Integrates well into existing developer workflows.
- Because all the "magic" is done in import maps, it gives the end user fine grained control over the behaviour, allowing them to address edge cases or different workflows without "breaking" the feature or making the feature unusable.
- The feature is open, transparent, portable, easily reversible, non-destructive and totally optional.
- Easily integrate-able into editors, so that a dev would run the command and presto, all the remote dependencies are local and editable.

### Other options considered

- Making access to `DENO_DIR` easier - currently, users can edit files in the `DENO_DIR`. There are significant risks to this, like depending on the dependency type and the state of the cache, files may or may not be invalidated in a way that causes the program to emit properly.  Also, it is a reasonable expectation that we should consider `DENO_DIR` and its schema "private" and while accessible, the layout and format may need to change as Deno evolves, and if people are tightly coupled to this, it becomes problematic and could easily hold back the development of Deno for fear of breaking integrations to non-public behaviours. Another risk is that it would not be (ever) compatible with Deno Deploy.
- Adding specific features to the language server to edit dependencies - this would then require people to use an editor with a language server to take advantage of this feature, which is not realistic or desired.

body:
I'm not sure if this is an open issue (since I can't find anything similar) but make Deno automatically search for the config file (`$PROJECT_ROOT/deno.json` or `$PROJECT_ROOT/deno.jsonc`).

This can be overridden if you pass the `--config` flag.

author: nathanbowang

title: remove window variable
body:
The background for this is that many existing web frameworks incorrectly use `window` as a feature detect for web browsers. This breaks them when server side rendering in Deno, as they think they are running in a browser.

The web frameworks should be feature detecting `document` if they want to know if they have access to the DOM, not `window`.

Nonetheless this practice is so prevalent in the ecosystem, that it would be difficult to change all of these wrong feature detects. This Sourcegraph query illustrates how widespread this is: https://sourcegraph.com/search?q=context:global+typeof+window&patternType=literal.

Because of this, we should consider removing the `window` global from Deno at the earliest possible date (likely Deno 2.0).
author: ry

title: Streams: reject pending reads when releasing reader
body:
Previously, calling `ReadableStreamDefaultReader.releaseLock()` or `ReadableStreamBYOBReader.releaseLock()` while there are pending `read()` requests would throw a `TypeError`.

The specification has been changed to allow this case, and to reject such pending `read()` requests instead.

Standard change: https://github.com/whatwg/streams/pull/1168
Tests: https://github.com/web-platform-tests/wpt/pull/32072
author: MattiasBuelens

title: Feature Request: `Deno.isUnstable`
body:
`Deno.isUnstable` will be set to true or false depending on if `--unstable` is provided or not
The reason why I would like this api in deno is because some modules need `--unstable` for additional functionality which could be enabled or disabled by this flag and not just throw when trying to use a unstable feature when in stable mode
author: MierenManz

title: `cancel_try_future` cancels a network I/O future right after polling it vs cancels a spawned task before it actually runs.
body:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/core/async_cancel.rs#L649-L677

Shouldn't it cancel a network I/O future *before* polling it like it cancels the task before it runs?

If the test is ran in a hermetic environment it fails with `NetworkUnreachable` I'm guessing since it can't get the initial polling done?

```
---- async_cancel::tests::cancel_try_future stdout ----
thread 'async_cancel::tests::cancel_try_future' panicked at 'assertion failed: `(left == right)`
  left: `NetworkUnreachable`,
 right: `Interrupted`', core/async_cancel.rs:674:7
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace


failures:
    async_cancel::tests::cancel_try_future
```

If it must be canceled after the polling then can the test be modified to use loopback or a temporarily hosted service?

author: imaaquibali

title: The deno does not support the socks5h protocol.
body:
```
https_proxy=socks5h://myproxy:myport  deno upgrade
```
Upgrade failure

```
https_proxy=socks5://myproxy:myport  deno upgrade
```
Upgrade success

Differentiate socks5h from socks5. In a proxy string, socks5h:// means that the hostname is resolved by the SOCKS server. socks5:// means that the hostname is resolved locally.
author: pinghe

title: lsp: better error handling with bad or unavailable registry configs
body: As seen in denoland/vscode_deno#609, if a registry configuration is unavailable or corrupt, it sends a client notification instead of just logging the issue to the debug output.  For these type of failures it should just be logged out.  It is also "terminating" the list of origins, instead of just skipping over the "bad" one.
author: kitsonk

title: SIGSEGV when running multiple `MainWorker`s in a single thread
body:
I was trying to reproduce a case of async APIs hanging when an embedder run multiple `JsRuntime`s in a single thread, and I found that I got a segmentation fault, even though the embedding code had no `unsafe`. This doesn't happen every time, but on my machine it happens most times (whether using the `tla` feature or not), although I haven't been able to reproduce it when running under valgrind.

I tested this with both `rustc 1.60.0-nightly (22e491ac7 2022-01-13)` and `rustc 1.58.0 (02072b482 2022-01-11)` (stable), both on `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu`.

https://github.com/andreubotella/deno-multiple-isolates-per-thread
author: andreubotella

title: `deno bundle` fails to resolve react/jsx-runtime
body:
I'm seeing an issue with this when trying to do `deno bundle` with `react/jsx-runtime`, created repro here with description of error: https://github.com/pheuter/hello-deno#blocking-issue.

Currently unable to bundle client-side code using `react/jsx-runtime` despite `deno run` working just fine using the same code and config:

```
» deno bundle --config deno.json client/index.tsx static/client.js
Check file:///Users/mark/src/pheuter/hello-deno/client/index.tsx
Bundle file:///Users/mark/src/pheuter/hello-deno/client/index.tsx
error: Unable to output during bundling.

Caused by:
    0: load_transformed failed
    1: failed to analyze module
    2: failed to resolve https://esm.sh/react@17.0.2/jsx-runtime from file:///Users/mark/src/pheuter/hello-deno/client/index.tsx
    3: Cannot resolve "https://esm.sh/react@17.0.2/jsx-runtime" from "file:///Users/mark/src/pheuter/hello-deno/client/index.tsx".
```

```sh
» deno --version
deno 1.17.3 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 9.7.106.15
typescript 4.5.2
```

Oddly enough, the issue appears to go away when I explicitly do `/** @jsxImportSource https://esm.sh/react@17.0.2 */` in every tsx file, which I'm trying to avoid with this optimization in the first place.
author: pheuter

title: Some global variables are not available on globalThis
body:
For example, `globalThis.close()` raises a type error. (`window.close()` does not raise a type error, but it does raise a lint error.) ```ts globalThis.close(); // deno-ts: Element implicitly has an 'any' type because type 'typeof globalThis' has no index signature. window.close(); // deno-lint: For compatibility between the Window context and the Web Workers, calling Web APIs via `window` is disallowed Instead, call this API via `self`, `globalThis`, or no extra prefix ``` I looked this up in the script below and it seems that I found some similar issues.
check.ts
```ts const denoGlobal = [...inspect(globalThis, "globalThis", new Set())].sort(); for (const l of denoGlobal) { // avoid syntax error... if (l === "globalThis.RegExp.$'") { console.log(`globalThis.RegExp["$'"]`); } else if (l === "globalThis.RegExp.$`") { console.log('globalThis.RegExp["$`"]'); } else if (l === "globalThis.Deno.SeekMode.0") { console.log("globalThis.Deno.SeekMode[0]"); } else if (l === "globalThis.Deno.SeekMode.1") { console.log("globalThis.Deno.SeekMode[1]"); } else if (l === "globalThis.Deno.SeekMode.2") { console.log("globalThis.Deno.SeekMode[2]"); } else { console.log(l); } } function inspect(obj: any, path: string, res: Set) { for (const key of Object.getOwnPropertyNames(obj).sort()) { const keyPath = path ? `${path}.${key}` : key; // avoid circular reference if (keyPath === "globalThis.window") { continue; } if (keyPath === "globalThis.self") { continue; } if (keyPath === "globalThis.globalThis") { continue; } if (typeof obj[key] === "object" && obj[key]) { inspect(obj[key], keyPath, res); getPrototypeKey(obj[key], keyPath, res); } else { res.add(keyPath); if (typeof obj[key] === "function") { Object.getOwnPropertyNames(obj[key]).filter((v) => !["length", "name", "prototype", "arguments", "caller"].includes(v) ).forEach((v) => res.add(`${keyPath}.${v}`)); try { const { prototype } = obj[key]; if (prototype) { const name = prototype.constructor.name || `[[${keyPath}]]`; Object.getOwnPropertyNames(prototype) .filter((v) => v !== "constructor") .forEach((v) => res.add(`${path}.${name}.prototype.${v}`)); getPrototypeKey(prototype, `${keyPath}.prototype`, res); } } catch (error) { console.error(error); } } } } return res; } function getPrototypeKey(obj: any, path: string, res: Set) { if (obj === null || typeof obj !== "object") { return res; } const prototype = Object.getPrototypeOf(obj); if (prototype === null || typeof prototype !== "object") { return res; } if (prototype.constructor === Object) { return res; } if (prototype.constructor === Array) { return res; } Object.getOwnPropertyNames(prototype).forEach((v) => res.add(`${path}.${v}`)); getPrototypeKey(prototype, path, res); return res; } ```
```shell $ deno --version deno 1.17.3 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc) v8 9.7.106.15 typescript 4.5.2 $ deno run --allow-read ./check.ts > ./tmp.ts $ deno cache --unstable ./tmp.ts 2> info.txt # output typescript diagnostics ```

Related: #12484
author: ayame113

title: Some global variables are not available on globalThis
body:
For example, `globalThis.close()` raises a type error. (`window.close()` does not raise a type error, but it does raise a lint error.) ```ts globalThis.close(); // deno-ts: Element implicitly has an 'any' type because type 'typeof globalThis' has no index signature. window.close(); // deno-lint: For compatibility between the Window context and the Web Workers, calling Web APIs via `window` is disallowed Instead, call this API via `self`, `globalThis`, or no extra prefix ``` I looked this up in the script below and it seems that I found some similar issues.
check.ts
```ts const denoGlobal = [...inspect(globalThis, "globalThis", new Set())].sort(); for (const l of denoGlobal) { // avoid syntax error... if (l === "globalThis.RegExp.$'") { console.log(`globalThis.RegExp["$'"]`); } else if (l === "globalThis.RegExp.$`") { console.log('globalThis.RegExp["$`"]'); } else if (l === "globalThis.Deno.SeekMode.0") { console.log("globalThis.Deno.SeekMode[0]"); } else if (l === "globalThis.Deno.SeekMode.1") { console.log("globalThis.Deno.SeekMode[1]"); } else if (l === "globalThis.Deno.SeekMode.2") { console.log("globalThis.Deno.SeekMode[2]"); } else { console.log(l); } } function inspect(obj: any, path: string, res: Set) { for (const key of Object.getOwnPropertyNames(obj).sort()) { const keyPath = path ? `${path}.${key}` : key; // avoid circular reference if (keyPath === "globalThis.window") { continue; } if (keyPath === "globalThis.self") { continue; } if (keyPath === "globalThis.globalThis") { continue; } if (typeof obj[key] === "object" && obj[key]) { inspect(obj[key], keyPath, res); getPrototypeKey(obj[key], keyPath, res); } else { res.add(keyPath); if (typeof obj[key] === "function") { Object.getOwnPropertyNames(obj[key]).filter((v) => !["length", "name", "prototype", "arguments", "caller"].includes(v) ).forEach((v) => res.add(`${keyPath}.${v}`)); try { const { prototype } = obj[key]; if (prototype) { const name = prototype.constructor.name || `[[${keyPath}]]`; Object.getOwnPropertyNames(prototype) .filter((v) => v !== "constructor") .forEach((v) => res.add(`${path}.${name}.prototype.${v}`)); getPrototypeKey(prototype, `${keyPath}.prototype`, res); } } catch (error) { console.error(error); } } } } return res; } function getPrototypeKey(obj: any, path: string, res: Set) { if (obj === null || typeof obj !== "object") { return res; } const prototype = Object.getPrototypeOf(obj); if (prototype === null || typeof prototype !== "object") { return res; } if (prototype.constructor === Object) { return res; } if (prototype.constructor === Array) { return res; } Object.getOwnPropertyNames(prototype).forEach((v) => res.add(`${path}.${v}`)); getPrototypeKey(prototype, path, res); return res; } ```
```shell $ deno run --allow-read ./check.ts > ./tmp.ts $ deno cache --unstable ./tmp.ts 2> info.txt # output typescript diagnostics ```

author: ayame113

title: Introduce a way to tell `deno run` to parse the script as TypeScript
body:
Hey community,

I presume it would be nice to have an option flag to `deno run` to tell that one wants to parse the file as TypeScript even though it doesn't have a `.ts` extension.

Consider this CLI shebang executable script, named `hello.ts` and `hello`:

```ts
#!/usr/bin/env -S deno run

let s: string = "Hello world"
console.log(s)
```

Trying to run it produces the following:

```
$ ./hello.ts
Check file:///tmp/deno-run/hello.ts
Hello world

$ ./hello
error: Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token ':'
let s: string = "Hello world"
     ^
    at <anonymous> (file:///tmp/deno-run/hello:3:6)
```

As there could be multiple reasons to have a CLI executable named without the `.ts` extension, I think it would be great to have something like `deno run --ts` or `deno run --language=ts` in order to force TypeScript processing of the specified script.
author: dadooda

title: URLPattern panic
body:
## Minimal reproduction

```js
new URLPattern({ pathname: '/:a.' })
```

## Expected behaviour

Running the reproduction case in Chrome yields:
```js
URLPattern {
  hash: "*"
  hostname: "*"
  password: "*"
  pathname: "/:a."
  port: "*"
  protocol: "*"
  search: "*"
  username: "*"
}
```

## Cause

This happens because of an `.unwrap()` in our `rust-urlpattern` crate: https://github.com/denoland/rust-urlpattern/blob/fdb0bbb45613b346cb1bfef57c16fc8af97fa1ff/src/component.rs#L195
author: AaronO

title: Proposal: dotenv + computed env types in std
body:
Part of almost every node project I've done is validating the env variables, both their existence and content (type, formatting, defaults and such). Joi is a great way to do that, I've also created my own module as a simpler version of what Joi does (https://www.npmjs.com/package/dotenv-typed) and for Deno there is the computed_types third party (https://deno.land/x/computed_types@v1.1.0).

My proposal is to merge computed_types (not my project though) into std. And, for that matter, I feel dotenv should be embedded in std because the .env has become such a mainstream way to solve the development environment env problem.

In summary: extend std with dotenv with rich type and value checking to be added to Deno.env

What do you think? Thank you.
author: GerbenRampaart

title: JsRuntime::new in docker container panics on "No such file or directory"
body:
I am having the same issue described [here](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13067), I will add some logs hoping they can help somebody.

The issue is that running this code will panic with the error 
```rust
let opts = RuntimeOptions::default();
let mut runtime = JsRuntime::new(opts); # this gives the error
```

```
thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: No such file or directory (os error 2)', /usr/local/cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/deno_core-0.107.0/runtime.rs:399:38
```


The problem only appears when I try to run the executable inside a docker container on `debian:bullseye-slim`. I tried installing `libv8-dev` and `nodejs` on the container, hoping they could provide some dependencies, but nothing changed. 

I tried both the following:
```
deno_core = "0.107"
serde_v8 = "0.18"
```
and
```
deno_core = "0.114"
serde_v8 = "0.25"
```

title: Implement test step metadata
body:
We are stabilizing test steps in #13055 without the metadata described in #10771.

We still need to implement metadata sometime and it probably should be done under unstable for now.
author: dsherret

title: lsp: Autocompletion and error highlighting too slow while working with some files in VSCode
body:
Working on some files in VSCode using Deno plugin turns really slow on some scenarios. Overall, almost all files become slow to work on, but some are way slower than others. Seems like importing some external dependency worsens the problem.

I have other projects in Deno, and none of them have this behavior. Maybe the only difference is the amount of dependencies, but anyway, doesn't explain completely the slowness for me, seems like too much.

Here's a video to show how slow it can become, working on the following folder: https://github.com/sirikon/bilbostack-app/tree/2ddb90f4cb7c7392b2083858f0b585f1fcc04f26/back opening file `src/web/server.ts`.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1157382/149842369-aae47ab5-a312-48d8-9d7b-ee462746477d.mp4

Here is the output that can be shown below when enabled using `"deno.internalDebug": true`: [Download](https://github.com/denoland/deno/files/7884904/output.txt)

`deno --version`
```
deno 1.17.3 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 9.7.106.15
typescript 4.5.2
```
VSCode deno plugin: `v3.10.1`
[VSCodium](https://vscodium.com/) (It's just VSCode without Microsoft telemetry):

```

author: zuisong

title: "Fatal javascript OOM in Reached heap limit" when logging too many `Uint8Array`s
body:
While I had a relatively complicated async loop with several concurrent tasks running, deno crashed on me with this:

```
<--- Last few GCs --->

[7944:000001FC318434B0]   468530 ms: Scavenge 1276.5 (1281.4) -> 1272.6 (1281.4) MB, 0.0 / 0.0 ms  (average mu = 0.364, current mu = 0.349) allocation failure
[7944:000001FC318434B0]   468532 ms: Scavenge 1276.5 (1281.4) -> 1272.6 (1281.4) MB, 0.0 / 0.0 ms  (average mu = 0.364, current mu = 0.349) allocation failure
[7944:000001FC318434B0]   469084 ms: Mark-sweep 1402.2 (1409.5) -> 1400.7 (1409.5) MB, 278.0 / 0.0 ms  (average mu = 0.448, current mu = 0.513) allocation failure scavenge might not succeed


<--- JS stacktrace --->


#
# Fatal javascript OOM in Reached heap limit
#
```

After googling the error I found #7081 which I think is not related to this issue at all, but brought me on the right track because I was using several instances of `TextDecoder` as well. I only used them on small slices of `Uint8Array`s though, so my next best guess was to remove all my `console.log`s I put in my code while debugging and voilà, it stopped crashing. My assumption is that logging long `TypedArray`s (or probably normal `Array`s as well) "concurrently" causes the internal console formatter to "clog up" somehow. I was very surprised Deno even crashed, because I worked with huge Arrays before without any problems, but I guess never in such an async, "concurrent" manner.

Specs:

deno 1.17.3 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 9.7.106.15
typescript 4.5.2
author: nnmrts

title: Deno.isatty throws Busy error intermittently
body: Using this example: ```ts const isTTY = Deno.isatty(Deno.stderr.rid) ``` I see intermittent throws from the `Deno.isatty` function. While I am sure it's possible that `stderr` is busy, I don't see a way to use `isatty` with `rid`s that won't sometimes throw if they happen to busy at the time. If there is a way to handle this case in my code, then I apologize and this can be closed.
author: myobie

title: Improve time to lint diagnostics in LSP
body:
This can be measured using the following benchmark:
```
# cargo bench --bench lsp_bench_standalone
   Compiling deno v1.17.3 (/Users/ry/src/deno/cli)
    Finished bench [optimized] target(s) in 7.03s
     Running unittests (target/release/deps/lsp_bench_standalone-50969af938e69a9b)

running 1 test
test incremental_change_wait ... bench: 288,701,433 ns/iter (+/- 6,529,414)
```

Currently it takes well over 200ms to receive lint diagnostics due to the debouncing mechanism.
author: ry

title: Deno not recognising module import url updates
body:
After playing around with various import URLs (trying to find compatible modules) I reached a point where my import url updates were no longer being recognised by Deno.

When running $ deno run --allow-all --reload example.js

I receive: error: Expected a JavaScript or TypeScript module, but identified a Unknown module. Importing these types of modules is currently not supported.
  Specifier: https://jspm.dev/preact@10.6.4/
    at /lib/preact.js:2:57


/lib/preact.js contents:
`
    import htm from "https://jspm.dev/htm@3.1.0/preact";  
`
`
    import { createElement, hydrate as preactHydrate } from "https://jspm.dev/preact@10.6.4";  
`
`
    export { useState, useEffect } from "https://jspm.dev/preact@10.6.4/hooks";  
`

As you can see I have removed the trailing / from the import statement on line 2 but Deno still thinks it is there. Even if I completely replace the import URL with a unpkg url I still get the same error. I am using Deno version 1.17.3. Using --reload flag on deno run doesn't resolve the issue on my 2015 MacBook Pro, nor does a system restart.
author: sejori

title: Fail to upgrade to 1.17.3
body: On Windows 11 (but it also failed on Windows 10) from 1.17.2: ``` $ deno upgrade Looking up latest version Found latest version 1.17.3 Checking https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v1.17.3/deno-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip 24.4 MiB / 24.4 MiB (100.0%) Deno is upgrading to version 1.17.3 ============================================================ Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new. If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env var set and include the backtrace in your report. Platform: windows x86_64 Version: 1.17.2 Args: ["C:\\woot\\usr\\bin\\deno.exe", "upgrade"] thread 'main' panicked at 'assertion failed: output.status.success()', cli\tools\upgrade.rs:306:3 stack backtrace: 0: 0x7ff7bd5ee704 - 1: 0x7ff7bcd645fa - 2: 0x7ff7bd5ec6c8 - 3: 0x7ff7bd5ed7b2 - 4: 0x7ff7bd5eccc0 - 5: 0x7ff7bcd14c83 - 6: 0x7ff7bd5ec4f7 - 7: 0x7ff7bd5f4be3 - 8: 0x7ff7bd5f4b79 - 9: 0x7ff7bd5f4b34 - 10: 0x7ff7be6ef520 - CrashForExceptionInNonABICompliantCodeRange 11: 0x7ff7be6ef67c - CrashForExceptionInNonABICompliantCodeRange 12: 0x7ff7bc91e5b5 - 13: 0x7ff7bc91aa27 - 14: 0x7ff7bc905da2 - 15: 0x7ff7bc8f10bf - 16: 0x7ff7bc8eb391 - 17: 0x7ff7bc8eb437 - 18: 0x7ff7be6a8dcc - CrashForExceptionInNonABICompliantCodeRange 19: 0x7ffdd4cf54e0 - BaseThreadInitThunk 20: 0x7ffdd5d6485b - RtlUserThreadStart ``` Copy the binary from `Releases` (Assets - artifact `deno-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip`): ``` deno --version C:/woot/usr/bin/deno.exe: error while loading shared libraries: api-ms-win-crt-multibyte-l1-1-0.dll: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory ``` I didn't change any runtime lib installed: Any hint?

title: [suggest]Is there any plan to drop import extension？
body:
I use deno in a Monorepo project witch nodejs and browser code.

And for now,webstorm only support one typescript language server. Actually, I did some work in babel,and now I can deliver code between node、deno and brower.

And I use vscode for deno,webstorm for node and brower.

What annoying me is, if I careless touch a deno file in webstrom,it alart me '.ts' error as you know.

Use deno and node in mix mode is the reality.

Actually,the reason I use deno is because,it is good to write TypeScript script. And I can obtain that ability via esbuild.

I think the incompatible in typescript import syntax,is a big mistake you should change.

deno is good,because if we make a huge program,we can use deno to isolate something.In that scope,what is important is we can tansfer any code from deno to node,and any code from node to deno.

In order to achieve this goal, my design(and I think is the common one) is use node develop code.and generate deno code.(because if a problem can solve in deno,it certainly can be solved in node,but  not the other way around).

And I think it's good for deno to generate language syntax compatible design.

Meanwhile,I think npm discompatible is receivable,because this is higher than lanuage syntax,And we can do a lot to compat that layer.

title: deno compile should use eszip instead of bundling
body:
Once new version of [`eszip`](https://github.com/denoland/eszip) is available we should use it for `deno compile` instead of relying on bundler. 

`eszip` provides a loss-less way to store a whole module graph and doesn't alter source code in any way. Given numerous problems with bundler we experienced over the last year it seems to be desirable to avoid bundling if possible.

title: lsp: does not use $DENO_CERT when fetching registry configurations
body:
**Describe the bug**

When opening VS Code to a Deno project, this extension errors when attempting to request an external URL.  I am behind a corporate proxy with a self signed cert.

**To Reproduce**

1. Have a self signed cert in your certificate chain.
2. Open a Deno project.
3. Error occurs.

**Expected behavior**

When VS Code opens, I do not expect to see this error.  Instead, I would expect this extension to use the $DENO_CERT environment variable to access any external resources.

Here is a curl example that shows the same behavior.

```shell
$ curl https://x.nest.land/.well-known/deno-import-intellisense.json
curl: (60) SSL certificate problem: self signed certificate in certificate chain
More details here: https://curl.se/docs/sslcerts.html

curl failed to verify the legitimacy of the server and therefore could not
establish a secure connection to it. To learn more about this situation and
how to fix it, please visit the web page mentioned above.
```

Here is a curl example where $DENO_CERT is used and the request is successful.

```shell
$ curl --cacert $DENO_CERT https://x.nest.land/.well-known/deno-import-intellisense.json
Moved Permanently. Redirecting to https://intellisense.nest.land/deno-import-intellisense.json
```

**Screenshots**
![vscode-deno-error](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12434812/150331382-02414b64-0612-4ed4-9b03-b121e40675f6.png)

**Versions**

- vscode: 1.63.2
- deno: 1.17.3
- extension: 3.10.1
author: tdillon

title: Generate lock file from import maps
body:
I tend to place all dependencies in a file `import-map.json`. This works usually well, until you have to generate the lock file based on your actual project's dependencies. For instance, nothing used in the tests would be included by `deno cache ... --lock-write main.ts`, and there could be other scenarios that I'm not aware of.

I think an alternative solution could be to generate the lock file based on what's in the `import-map.json` and that could be used project wise. Another thing I can think of is that `deno cache`, when used with `--lock-write`, could accept a directory instead, but that would trigger some other number of scenarios and inconsistencies.

author: kitsonk

title: `deno cache` directory support
body:
`deno cache src` should walk `src` similar to `deno test`/`deno fmt`/`deno lint`, matching `deno lint`'s pattern matching. This has been implicitly requested a couple of times, usually `deno test --no-run` is good enough but after all it's strange for `deno cache` to not support this.

<details>
<summary>Implementation note</summary>

The short-circuiting check within `prepare_module_load()` is too rigorous to call it for each collected module. Add an additional check around the [`prepare_module_load()` call](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/56d0ca7f52d9bff7326bb2b9406ea1e0282a5fbe/cli/main.rs#L557) to see if each module is at all present in `ProcState::graph_data`. That will also result in a minimal amount of roots being reported as `Check <specifier>` compared to adding them all as roots in a single call.
</details>

title: Source code could not be parsed (Unexpected UTF-8 character)
body:
## Description
I have a language detection library [tinyld](https://github.com/komodojp/tinyld) which works in node (CJS) and browser (ESM) without trouble.

But I run into a strange issue when I try to use it in deno.
> error: The module's source code could not be parsed: Unexpected character 'ำ'

The issue come from a big object where I store n-gram for different languages (as utf-8 strings)
I have thousands of other keys in there which do not cause any problem (japanese, chinese, arabic, ...)
```js
 //...
 อ: 9,
 ฮ: 9,
 ะ: 9,
 า: 9,
  ำ: 9,
 //...
```

But I don't now why, there is one specific thai character, ` ำ` which cause that `Unexpected character error` 
If I remove that line, it works fine. 

So my question, is there a reason why this specific utf-8 character cause trouble?

**P.S.** I noticed that the same problem seem to appear in VSCode.
It kinda feel like this character is detected as a single quote, maybe more a typescript issue than deno.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4903529/150635693-adb60a47-3de6-4a67-83e2-458e1d3b256b.png)

title: Uncaught `TypeError` in `ext/console`
body:
## Bug Report

`console.debug` throws an error when it is used by the `debug` package that is skypack'ed from npm.

```
error: Uncaught TypeError: (intermediate value)(intermediate value)(intermediate value) is not iterable
      logFn.apply(self2, args);
            ^
    at cssToAnsi (deno:ext/console/02_console.js:1591:27)
    at inspectArgs (deno:ext/console/02_console.js:1703:32)
    at console.debug (deno:ext/console/02_console.js:1803:9)
    at debug (https://cdn.skypack.dev/-/debug@v4.3.3-0XITH0hRj2rH5NQ5xJ30/dist=es2019,mode=imports/optimized/debug.js:270:13)
    at file:///tmp/test.ts:5:1
```

### Reproduction Steps

Copy the following code to a file.

```ts
import d from "https://cdn.skypack.dev/debug@^4.3.3";
d.useColors = () => !Deno.noColor;
d.enable("demo");
const debug = d("demo");
debug("test");
```

Make sure your deno version is `1.17.2` or higher.

Run the created file with `deno run`. This will lead to the above stack trace.

You can verify that `1.17.1` is able to run the above code correctly. The ouput is:
```
demo test +0ms
```

### Offending Pull Request

The PR #13175 introduces this bug.
author: KnorpelSenf

title: Since v1.17.3, statically linking VC runtime is not working on Windows
body:
Hi!

It seems like Deno v1.18.0 and v1.17.3 unintentionally use dynamic linking on Windows 10.
Since v1.17.3, I get `vcruntime140_1.dll was not found` error message box.
(Probably related to #4468, #13412)

The results of using https://github.com/lucasg/Dependencies are as follows:

(deno-v1.17.2) -- no external dependencies
![dep-1 17 2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/98225171/150647050-b8fc6685-e351-4661-9495-e2151b22807f.png)

(deno-v1.17.3 and v1.18.0) -- VCRUNTIME140.dll/VCRUNTIME140_1.dll  and external dependencies
![dep-1 18 0](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/98225171/150647075-b9154ba5-7cab-4ab3-931c-0d78f629f1f4.png)

I would guess the `rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+crt-static"]` in `.cargo/config` is ignored or overridden by another config(e.g. [ci env config](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/a96b294a8a421570f6b29b5df6fbeaf2d9658047/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L56), etc.).

Thank you for taking the time to look over this issue.
I'm so sorry if I'm wrong!

Would it make sense for Deno to implement this API?

title: Missing JSDoc imports shouldn't be a hard failure
body:
When I updated deno to v1.18.0, I meet an error.
For example, I cited a third-party package. Here is [a piece of code](https://deno.land/x/kafkasaur@v0.0.7/src/broker/index.ts#L392):
```ts
/**
   * @public
   * @param {object} request
   * @param {string} request.groupId The unique group id
   * @param {import("../protocol/coordinatorTypes").CoordinatorType} request.coordinatorType The type of coordinator to find
   * @returns {Promise}
   */
  async findGroupCoordinator({ groupId, coordinatorType }: 
    {groupId: string, coordinatorType: number}) {
    // TODO: validate groupId, mandatory
    const findCoordinator = this.lookupRequest(
      apiKeys.GroupCoordinator,
      requests.GroupCoordinator
    );
    return await this[PRIVATE.SEND_REQUEST](
      findCoordinator({ groupId, coordinatorType })
    );
  }
```
Then it leads to an error in my code:
```
Download https://deno.land/x/kafkasaur@v0.0.7/types
Download https://deno.land/x/kafkasaur@v0.0.7/src/instrumentation/emitter

    at https://deno.land/x/kafkasaur@v0.0.7/src/broker/index.ts:392:22

author: peterwangfeng

title: Support for passing flags in `Deno.dlopen`
body:
### Feature request

Currently, we cannot pass any flags to the `Deno.dlopen` function. On unix based systems, this maps to `dlopen` itself and it can accept flags such as `RTLD_GLOBAL`. I think winapi also accepts some flags in `LoadLibrary`.

### Why

I have a project that interfaces with Python Interpreter's C API to allow using Python modules in Deno. However, loading any python module that depends on native libraries does not work at the moment since it tries to look for symbols in host process. This makes such modules not work via [`deno_python`](https://github.com/denosaurs/deno_python) on unix-based systems. For coming over this problem, I need to pass `RTLD_LAZY | RTLD_GLOBAL` flags to unix `dlopen` syscall, which is currently not possible with Deno FFI API.

Ref: https://github.com/denosaurs/deno_python/issues/7

### Solution

Maybe a third parameter in `dlopen` that accepts a `flags: number` (defaulting to 0). If this API is okay, I will PR

author: thomasweitzel

title: [suggestion] - Rework permissions feature
body:
Hi 👋🏻 

I have tried Deno several times, before version 1.0, and with the latest version. One obvious problem I see, and which I think is huge, is the permission system. Currently, when we run a script with Deno, we have to specify the different permissions we want using arguments like ``--allow-run``, ``--allow-read``.... or ``--all`` or ``-A`` in order to accept all permissions.
The point is that by allowing all permissions on a program, we expose ourselves to serious risk to the machine or the user, but out of "developer laziness", we don't want to have an execution line like:
```
deno run --allow-read --allow-net --allow-write --allow-env mod.ts
```
That's why I propose a new way of working. Arguments are still important, especially for everything CI. At the same time, for a better user experience, I can see something like:
```shell
deno run mod.ts
mod.ts needs the net permission. Do you allow it?
 - Yes
 - No
> Yes
mod.ts needs the write permission. Do you allow it?
 - Yes
 - No
> Yes
```
Once the developer has entered his preferences, they are cached, so that he doesn't have to specify them again.

An alternative would be to have a configuration file for this, specifically, add this possibility to the current configuration system.

I think a better developer experience at this level would bring more developers into the project, or at least interest a part of the community.

This is an open suggestion, and I would love to hear from the developers and the Deno team about this one.

title: [AES-GCM] 'additionalData' field is required on crypto.subtle.encrypt()
body:
Hello! How are you doing? Hope everyone's okay!

Today I was trying to use AES-GCM encryption on a project and realized that the optional field 'additionalData' in [AesGcmParams](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/AesGcmParams) wasn't that optional, after all. It seems that the new implementation on Deno 1.18.1 requires an ArrayBuffer to be passed with the function, even if it is an empty one. Setting 'additionalData' to `undefined` doesn't work.

Script:
```ts
await crypto.subtle.encrypt({
    name: 'AES-GCM',
    iv: new Uint8Array(12),
    tagLength: 96,
    additionalData: new ArrayBuffer(0) // setting to undefined doesn't work, ommiting doesn't work either
}, key, data);
```

Output error:
```js
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Error parsing args: missing field `additionalData`
```
Deno version:
```
deno 1.18.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 9.8.177.6
typescript 4.5.2
```

title: TypeScript: variables in `catch` should be `unknown` on default settings
body:
With the following code:

```typescript
try {
  throw 'not an Error object'
} catch (error) {
  console.error(error.message)
}
```

The Deno language server shows the type of the `error` variable as `any`, which should be `unknown` and a compiler error. Below is a screenshot of the Deno language server running in Visual Studio Code.

![catch_variable_any](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36610621/151671283-9278ff13-9fe9-40b0-a69b-027f69817f02.png)

Version information:
```
deno 1.18.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 9.8.177.6
typescript 4.5.2
```

Since TypeScript v4.4, [useUnknownInCatchVariables](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#useUnknownInCatchVariables) is activated when `strict` is `true`. According to the [documentation on deno.land](https://deno.land/manual/typescript/configuration), the default value of `strict` is `true` in Deno's default tsconfig, so `useUnknownInCatchVariables` would be implicitly `true` by default also.

body:
Hello! Hope everybody is okay!

Today I was testing decryption using the recently added Galois Counter Mode for AES in Deno and found out that it panics on decrypting if the tagLength is anything but 128 bits length.

If one is encrypting, there is no issue or panic message, but on decrypting, it is present. If the tagLength is unsupported, then it should fail on encryption too, or at least throw an JS runtime error instead of a Rust panic.

Code:
```ts
const key = await crypto.subtle.importKey("raw", new Uint8Array(32), 'AES-GCM', false, ["encrypt", "decrypt"]);

const iv = crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(12));

const encrypted = await crypto.subtle.encrypt({ name: 'AES-GCM', iv, tagLength: 96, additionalData: new ArrayBuffer(0)}, key, new Uint8Array(32));

const decrypted = await crypto.subtle.decrypt({ name: 'AES-GCM', iv, tagLength: 96, additionalData: new ArrayBuffer(0)}, key, encrypted );
```

Panic message:
```
==================================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this at
<https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new>.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the reproduction
steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env var set and include
the backtrace in your report.

Platform: linux x86_64
Version: 1.18.1
Args: ["deno", "run", "mod.ts"]

Thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at 'assertion failed: `(left == right)`
  left: `12`,
 right: `16`', /home/runner/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/generic-array-0.14.5/src/lib.rs:559:9
Note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace.
==================================================================
```

body:
Hey,

Node is considering adding a fetch implementation and I was wondering if the list of things Deno doesn't implement in `fetch` (presumably stuff like the GC requirements, relative paths and CORS) is documented somewhere?

author: benjamingr

title: Feature request: Keep-alive for TCP
body:
From [RFC1122 4.2.3.6](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122#page-101), 

```
Implementors MAY include "keep-alives" in their TCP
implementations, although this practice is not universally
accepted.  If keep-alives are included, the application MUST
be able to turn them on or off for each TCP connection, and
they MUST default to off.
```

Currently, `Deno.listen` and `Deno.connect` occasionally seem to become stale when kept open for a while without any packets (a waiting connection). It doesn't close the connection, and doesn't error out, just becomes unresponsive on both sides until you try to write a packet. This causes me to be unable to free up resources from zombie connections.

As far as I understand, this is due to not sending keep-alive probes. Node has the [`socket.setKeepAlive`](https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#socketsetkeepaliveenable-initialdelay) API that I'm missing on Deno.

Implementing this might simply be setting the `SO_KEEPALIVE`, `TCP_KEEPIDLE`, `TCP_KEEPCNT`, `TCP_KEEPINTVL` options on the socket [`Linux`](https://linux.die.net/man/3/setsockopt) [`Windows`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/winsock/nf-winsock-setsockopt).

A possible Deno API may look like `conn.setKeepAlive(interval)`

### Further reading:

I found this article that seems to describe the exact behaviour I'm facing, and the solution (keep-alive): https://holmeshe.me/network-essentials-setsockopt-SO_KEEPALIVE/#To-detect-an-absent-peer
author: MKRhere

title: allow bundle to ignore choice bare modules
body:
I have a set of deeply nested es6 libraries all of which I compile with Babel.  The next stage in the pipeline is to bundle each deeply nested, modular library into a single bundle.  I would like to use the Deno `bundle` command.

I provide an import map which causes each bare module to be pulled in and defeats the purpose of bundling.  I would like to bundle only the relative paths.  Each of the libraries being bundled results in one of the shared bare modules.  I tried setting the import map to use remote paths and use the `--no-remote` flag to prevent the dependencies from being pulled into each module.  The point is for each bundle to stand alone as its own separate module.

In Rollup.js, there is an option to name bare modules which are external.  The parser recognizes such imports as external, standalone modules and does not pull them in.  Couldn't Deno do the same?  This way it can be used in one stage to create bundles. Then at runtime, when provided an import map, it can resolve the bare modules.
author: mlanza

title: Spki CryptoKey export not working for ECDSA
body:
Hello,

I found yesterday a bug when exporting ECDSA keys to SPKI, deno doesn't accept properly the input CryptoKey as a Public key.
Here is how you can reproduce it 

```ts
const keys = await crypto.subtle.generateKey({ name: "ECDSA", namedCurve: "P-256" }, true, ["sign","verify"]);
const spki = await crypto.subtle.exportKey("raw", keys.publicKey);
```
This should work but deno returns `expected private key`
note that this error also shows up here [wpt.fyi](https://wpt.fyi/results/WebCryptoAPI/wrapKey_unwrapKey/wrapKey_unwrapKey.https.any.html?label=master&label=experimental&product=chrome&product=deno&product=firefox&product=safari&aligned) (link from the last Deno blogpost!)

The SPKI export key method should only take public key, and PKCS8 should take a privatekey. and it seems to be the other way around here.

title: lsp: notify client when config file is auto-discovered
body:
In order to be able to provide "auto-enablement" of Deno projects with the language server when a config file is auto-discovered, the language server would need to search the workspace and notify the client when a `deno.jsonc` file is auto-discovered as well as have semantics for being "enabled" when that occurs (but respecting explicit configuration of not-being enabled).

The biggest challenge is the explicit enablement/disablement, as we can't really disambiguate that at the moment.  The specific case which I don't think we can currently handle is that there is a `deno.jsonc` in the workspace root, but the user has for whatever reason, explicitly set it as `"deno.enable": false` versus that being the default value.

Ref: denoland/vscode_deno#617

author: theoparis

title: Feature request: Support global files require
body:
**Example use case:**
I have to write tests for a deno app. I don't want to use the Deno API directly. Instead I want to use a custom Mocha pollyfill, which itself uses the Deno API. The file declares `globalThis.it = ...`, `globalThis.describe = ....` etc.

I want to be able to tell Deno to require this file and to provide the global variables/functions to every test file I have, so that I don't manually write import statements in all of my files.

**How to support it?**
What I envision as a support is to use the `files` key of the `tsconfig` which is supplied to Deno or some special Deno only key.

**Motivation**
This could save a lot of manual work in certain use cases.

author: pauljoey

title: Configurable option for isolated type-checking of modules
body:
> If it's helpful, you might think of this like the [`isolatedModules`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#isolatedModules) compiler option, but for types.

In order to allow for the modules in a type-checked program's graph to use incompatible and exclusive ambient types, it is necessary to offer the ability to type-check modules in isolation. That way, when different modules use triple-slash references (or—even better—separate deno configs!), those types don't leak into other module code (causing type-checking conflicts and/or the potential for uncaught runtime errors).

This primarily addresses the use of Deno as a compiler (and/or bundler) of code which is then executed in another runtime environment (like a browser with puppeteer, or for evaluation by Node.js in a subprocess, etc.).

Refs:
- https://github.com/lucacasonato/deno-puppeteer/issues/46
- https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/37053
- https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/issues/501
- https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/issues/488
author: jsejcksn

title: Deno FFI permissions model
body:
Hello. I've found some possible improvements with the current Deno FFI permissions model.

At present, a binary toggle between "FFI allowed" and "FFI forbidden" is offered. This has a couple of problem points. For one, it means that once FFI is in play, then all Deno safety guarantees are off. A malicious piece of code can then just open up system libraries and f.ex. build their own unsafe Node.js APIs to work on familiar, unsafe ground.

Second, the current model does not make safe exceptions to the FFI rule possible. Let me illustrate.

For my use case it would be useful, at least in short term, to load a given library from one of three possible paths. As such, a comma separated list of paths for `--allow-ffi` would allow me to trust my given library while avoiding the possibility of a malicious actor gaining access to any and all system libraries they please. Alternatively, a library name (perhaps with glob pattern, eg. `libpython-3.*`) could be considered though this is of course somewhat problematic with naming, aliasing etc. 

As an additional point of security, I think that calling FFI symbols and loading new libraries should be separated in the permissions. Once a library is loaded, the symbols contained in it are free of Deno's permission model to begin with. At present the calls do check for FFI permissions, though. This essentially means that once a library is loaded, Deno cannot relinquish its right to open more libraries and still keep calling the one library it loaded (and presumably thinks safe to call).

A further security option, though mostly irrelevant given permissions are constant for a single process, would be to allow the `dlOpen` symbol definitions to define custom permissions on a per-symbol basis. eg. Given a symbol named `readFile` the symbol definition could then define `"permissions": "read"`. I'm not sure if this would be terribly useful, though.

author: Redstoneguy129

title: lsp: incremental lint and dependency diagnostic generation
body:
Right now, we create lint diagnostics for every open file each time:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/ed3086e4b129843c13a009112cf21dfd05745905/cli/lsp/diagnostics.rs#L451

This means that after you open enough files (say 50), you will start to get a noticable delay when changing only one file.

To improve this, we could store lint diagnostics and only recreate them when the file version changes. This should be ok because lint diagnostics are not dependent on other files. I don't think this would work for things like TypeScript or dependency diagnostics unfortunately.
author: dsherret

title: lsp: investigate prioritizing the generation of diagnostics to the last modified or opened file
body:
Instead of (1) collecting diagnostics for all open documents and (2) publishing diagnostics. We might want to consider (1) collecting diagnostics for the last modified/opened file, (2) publish these diagnostics, (3) collect diagnostics for the other files, (4) publish these other diagnostics.

This would take longer overall, but my guess would be that it would give the impression that the lsp is faster. To implement, the diagnostic update sent from the `LanguageServer` to `DiagnosticsServer` would include a collection of specifiers to prioritize.

One downside to this, is that it would make the LSP tests more complicated so this would be a largish task to implement. If/when implementing we should create a way to make testing the LSP easier and not as verbose.

title: Deno.test: shorter stack traces
body:
`Deno.test` currently prints the entire stack for errors in failures but this is typically unnecessary and is noise (unless you're troubleshooting `deno test` itself).

I suggest omitting deno-cli-specific stack information and, if needed, provide an opt-in flag for printing the entire stack but to not print it by default.

This should make it easier for developers to understand why their tests failed and not be concerned about Deno's internal runtime details, etc.

e.g.

```ts
import { fail } from "https://deno.land/std@0.123.0/testing/asserts.ts";

Deno.test("example", () => fail());

```

current output:

```console
% deno test test.ts
running 1 test from file:///path/to/test.ts
test example ... FAILED (8ms)

failures:

example
AssertionError: Failed assertion.
    at assert (https://deno.land/std@0.123.0/testing/asserts.ts:222:11)
    at fail (https://deno.land/std@0.123.0/testing/asserts.ts:597:3)
    at file:///path/to/test.ts:3:28
    at testStepSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:186:13)
    at asyncOpSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:67:15)
    at resourceSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:137:13)
    at Object.exitSanitizer [as fn] (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:169:15)
    at runTest (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:427:18)
    at Object.runTests (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:540:28)
    at [deno:cli/tools/test.rs:505:6]:1:21

failures:

        example

test result: FAILED. 0 passed; 1 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (24ms)

error: Test failed
```

desired output:

```console
% deno test test.ts
running 1 test from file:///path/to/test.ts
test example ... FAILED (8ms)

failures:

example
AssertionError: Failed assertion.
    at assert (https://deno.land/std@0.123.0/testing/asserts.ts:222:11)
    at fail (https://deno.land/std@0.123.0/testing/asserts.ts:597:3)
    at file:///path/to/test.ts:3:28

failures:

        example

test result: FAILED. 0 passed; 1 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (24ms)

error: Test failed
```

diff:

```patch
--- a.txt	2022-02-03 07:45:54.000000000 -0600
+++ b.txt	2022-02-03 07:46:04.000000000 -0600
@@ -9,13 +9,6 @@
     at assert (https://deno.land/std@0.123.0/testing/asserts.ts:222:11)
     at fail (https://deno.land/std@0.123.0/testing/asserts.ts:597:3)
     at file:///path/to/test.ts:3:28
-    at testStepSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:186:13)
-    at asyncOpSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:67:15)
-    at resourceSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:137:13)
-    at Object.exitSanitizer [as fn] (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:169:15)
-    at runTest (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:427:18)
-    at Object.runTests (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:540:28)
-    at [deno:cli/tools/test.rs:505:6]:1:21
 
 failures:
 
```

title: `baseUrl` and `paths` in tsconfig.json compatibility
body:
Hi, this issue comes for the reason of a limitation which I found when creating my projects in deno. I usually like to create a folder where I incorporate all the routes to third party modules and from there I export everything to include it from those files

```bash
project
├─deno_deps
│  ├─djwt.ts
│  ├─mongo.ts
│  └─oak.ts
├─src
│  ├─app.ts
...  ...
```
ej: oak.ts
```typescript
export { oakCors } from "https://deno.land/x/cors@v1.2.1/mod.ts";
export { Application, Router } from "https://deno.land/x/oak@v6.5.0/mod.ts";
export { getQuery } from "https://deno.land/x/oak@v6.5.0/helpers.ts";
export type { RouterContext, Context, RouteParams } from "https://deno.land/x/oak@v6.5.0/mod.ts";
```

And I think that I am not the only one who does this, and that in a certain way it is a good practice to be able to visualize in the same point all the modules on which the project depends

However, this causes that, if I want to import what I have in the folder, there are relative paths in the project which will mess up readability a bit

```typescript
import { RouterContext } from "../../../_dependencies/oak.ts";
import { Query } from "../../../database/queries/mongo.queries.ts";
import { UserCollection } from "../models/user.model.ts"
import { JwtHelper } from "../../helpers/jwt.helper.ts";
```

For this reason, I consider that if it does include the possibility of establishing the `baseUrl`, and even the paths, in the tsconfig, it would greatly improve this aspect. Something that the typescript cli already brings with it.

```typescript
"baseUrl": "src", 
...
"paths": {

author: devalexqt

title: Add the ability to timeout synchronous tests
body:
# Summary

Deno should be able to timeout synchronous tests which timeout, marking those tests as if they had failed.

Even if a test times out, all other tests in the same file should still run.

> ☝️ This issue complements #11133, for which #13600 has been implemented.


# Implementation details

We could implement the behaviour above in two ways. We could:
1. Track the index of the executed test and re-run the test file skipping the N tests which have already run.
2. We could have separate workers for each test, rather than having separate workers for each test file (as we do now)

Personally, I'd prefer to go for option 2. It allows us to have more control and flexibility when it comes to scheduling tests and would lead to a simpler execution flow.

Skipping the first N tests, in addition to being more complicated and less flexible, could also lead to undesirable side effects.

# Alternative behaviour

⭐  Alternatively, we could do [as @caspervonb suggested on Discord](https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/684911491035430919/929396770222510160), where we'd simply alert the reporter that a synchronous test has been running for longer than the configured timeout. **I also quite like this suggestion.**

If we go for the approach above, however, should we print a warning and allow the test to keep running indefinitely? Or should we abort running all of the tests in that file and proceed with running other test files?

title: [fmt] Option to remove semicolons
body:
Hi!

A lot of JavaScript and TypeScript projects don't use semicolons. Some famous style conventions such as [StandardJS](https://standardjs.com/) prohibit their use (except when needed).

I suggest to add an option `useSemicolon` that defaults to `true` and can be turned off in order to remove automatically insertable semicolons. When turned off this option set [dprint's semicolon option](https://dprint.dev/plugins/typescript/config/) to `asi`.

I think it is an option in the same vein as `useTabs` and `indentWidth`: for some people this improves code readability.

title: Proposal: Remove Deno.umask (with no argument call)
body:
`Deno.umask()` (no argument) has [a race codition bug](https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/1893#issuecomment-1032897779). `Deno.umask()` (no argument) uses 2 syscalls and between these 2, there's small amount of time when the `umask` value is incorrect, and `mkdir` can create a directory with wrong permission on that timing.

If we try to address the above issue properly, we probably need mutex lock for fs op calls. But that introduces much complexity, and also causes negative performance impact.

~~Also `umask` is a global state, and not something we should encourage using in general situations i.e. it can be considered as an antipattern. So it doesn't make much sense to introduce new complexity to support this feature. So I propose we should remove `Deno.umask`.~~

I propose we should remove `Deno.umask` with no argument
