title: APIs to facilitate connection pools
body:
I am unsure if [`Deno.connect`](https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/Deno.connect) requires clients to implement connection pooling, because the current API surface that `Deno.Conn` exposes makes it tricky to implement one (though, [folks indeed have impl poolers atop it using `try..catch` semantics](https://github.com/denodrivers/postgres/blob/b68d3ebfd1a5805050f12e74ddaa4df3185bb86f/client.ts#L486)).

In comparing with NodeJS `net`, I believe equivalent of these two APIs are needed in [`Deno.Conn`](https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/Deno.Conn) (TCP):

1. [`socket.readyState`](https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#socketreadystate) tells if conn is open or half-open.
2. [`socket.destroyed`](https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#socketdestroyed) tells if conn is open at all.
3. An adjustable [`socket.keepalive`](https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#socketsetkeepaliveenable-initialdelay).

([Here's how we use those APIs to determine health of a tcp-conn](https://github.com/serverless-dns/serverless-dns/blob/688c3110f6/src/core/dns/conns.js#L20-L22)).

NodeJS `net` also exposes [`socket.resume`](https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#socketresume) and [`socket.pause`](https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#socketpause), though those are not really required to build a pooler.

From NodeJS `dgram`, for _connected_ UDP sockets, [`Event.close`](https://nodejs.org/api/dgram.html#event-close) and `Event.error` come in handy to remove failed conns preemptively from the pool.

([Here's how we use those events to evict udp-conns from the pool](https://github.com/serverless-dns/serverless-dns/blob/688c3110f6/src/core/dns/conns.js#L152-L153)).

Of course, if Deno doesn't require client-code to pool (TCP/UDP) conns at all (like for HTTP), then that's even more awesome. [From this github discussion](https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/issues/1079), it is confirmed that `tokio` doesn't pool conns, but that [`tower`](https://github.com/tower-rs/tower) does... but I am not fluent in Rust to deduce anything from Deno's use of `tower`.

cc: @bartlomieju
author: ignoramous

title: Proposal: Increase max number of threads Deno can spawn if user decide to
body:
Network related elements makes Rust side to launch new threads in order to make it "non blocking", right? We can start new TCP server or going higher - WS/HTTP and those can work on single thread on small usage but once our service gets heavily used - it starts to spawn new threads to manage that.

The thing is that big apps working on Deno seems to be hitting max number of threads that Deno allows to use. While testing - I've ran a simple TCP server: `deno run --allow-net https://deno.land/x/websocket@v0.1.3/example/server.ts` and made it connect 1024 shards that were sending random payloads (client attacker made in Julia). Server was working fine for a few seconds - then dramatically slow down, it started to receiving like 8~12 payloads, then was idle for like 0.2s then another ~8 payloads. During such heavy usage - Deno's TCP server started to have issues with memory - it simply grows infinitely cause it couldn't handle such pressure at once.

My suggestion is adding a flag like `--max-threads` to the deno run/compile commands that would allow user to define how many threads can Deno use on their machine. Of course perfect solution would be optimizing Deno's TCP/WebSocket API but increasing number of max threads sounds good enough.
author: amatsagu

title: Console.log CSS should support initial (or unset/revert)
body: When pretty-printing output, it's common to apply a style for a span, before reverting back to the terminal's default style. This is used in terminal prompts and in log output, for example. As a specific motivating example, consider this output (this program does not output color to non-tty stdouts). ```sh ; deno run --allow-env --allow-net --allow-hrtime src/index.ts | cat Check file:///[snipped]/src/index.ts Listening on port 8000 status=200 route="POST /" duration=1.3ms status=200 route="GET /" duration=0.3ms status=200 route="PUT /" duration=0.2ms status=200 route="DELETE /" duration=0.2ms status=200 route="HEAD /" duration=0.1ms ``` With color, it looks like this: But what if we want to use background-color instead? In this case, I simply forgot to change the `color: white` used as resets to `background-color: black`. That's an easy fix, but it shows the limitations of the current level of support. Furthermore, this color reset is *fragile*. What if the user uses a green terminal background? This breaks in an ugly way. A quick test reveals that support exists in Firefox: ```js console.log('%cRED%cUNSET', 'color: red', 'color: unset') ``` Thus, implementing at least one of `initial`/`unset`/`revert` is needed to make it easier to write good programs with well-formatted console output, in addition to improving parity with web platforms.

title: Indicate source of leaking async ops
body:
We recently started using Deno as the primary development runtime for a biggish pre-existing codebase. In the process of migrating tests I've become incredibly aware of how many `setTimeout` / `setInterval` operations were being called and not being cleaned up thanks to error messages like this:

```
AssertionError: Test case is leaking async ops.
Before:
  - dispatched: 0
  - completed: 0
After:
  - dispatched: 4043
  - completed: 4041
Ops:
  op_sleep:
    Before:
      - dispatched: 0
      - completed: 0
    After:
      - dispatched: 501
      - completed: 499
 ```

The only issue is that in a large codebase with dependencies this is really cryptic: there's no way to tell what the source of the `op_sleep`. So you have to spray and pray.

Would it be possible to show more information about leaking async ops so that it's easier to address them?

body:

```

```
is not readable:
```
$ ls /mnt/System\ Volume\ Information/
ls: reading directory '/mnt/System Volume Information/': Permission denied
```
Deno, or at least `Deno.readDir()`, does not appear to appreciate this:
```
============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.

reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env

stack backtrace:
```

body:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/38158676/153713708-695bbcca-bb25-434f-9e08-b5f6b2b46754.png)

```shell
[INFO] [stderr] error: Unrecognized option: 'extern-html-root-url'
``` 
And because of this, no docs are shown in crates.io

title: Rename Deno.File to Deno.FsFile
body:
`Deno.File` is a bit unfortunate name because it's easy to confuse it with global `File` Web API (https://developer.mozilla.org/pl/docs/Web/API/File).

We should rename `Deno.File` to `Deno.FsFile` to avoid confusion. `Deno.File` will be marked as deprecated and scheduled for removal in Deno 2.0.
author: bartlomieju

title: [Feature Request]: import assertions support another type 'raw' or 'text' or 'byte'
author: axetroy

title: When using `--inspect` wait a little bit for inspector connection at program start
body:
See https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/issues/557 and past issue #12417

> This is a extremely annoying issue as mostly beginners (me) will encounter this when running very simple scripts that run very fast. In practice once one gets a website running this isn't a issue anymore.

When debugging a small program it might execute and close before vscode connects. This leads to a poor user experience where vscode just keeps waiting to connect, but deno has already finished. There's no indication in the UI about what's going on and it's confusing.

To mitigate (not entirely prevent) this scenario, we could introduce a small delay at program start that delays for X milliseconds or until a connection is established. This delay should be long enough to eliminate this issue happening on most machines, but short enough that nobody would really notice it (especially people who are attaching a debugger after launching). We could perhaps even make this delay take into account the amount of time that's elapsed since deno started so it would likely only be hit for small programs.

author: dsherret

title: vendor: handle truncating directory and file names when they're near the 260 windows path length limit
body:
Windows limits paths to be 260 characters total. When paths get really long in deno vendor, we should change the file system output to keep the path length low.

Possible implementations:

1. We could start truncating paths from the bottom of the file system tree up. This is what dnt does and is the simplest solution: https://github.com/denoland/dnt/pull/125 (we could base the implementation off that)
2. We could take sections deep down in the tree and move them up to be a child of the vendor folder. This is a bit complex and would still need to truncate super long file names. The import map would get a bit complex.

When this occurs, we should warn about it and perhaps introduce a way to disable this behaviour for people who don't care about Windows as it could potentially introduce a lot of new entries in the import map. Most people won't ever run into though.
author: dsherret

title: vendor: handle module specifiers with different text in the same module that resolve to the same module
body:
Requires: https://github.com/denoland/deno_graph/issues/132

This is a known bug with the initial implementation, but should be rare in practice for people to hit.

Example:

```ts
export { foo } from "https://example.com/mod";
export type { foo } from "https://example.com/redirect_to_mod";
```

The above code would only create an import map entry for the last specifier.
author: dsherret

title: vendor: handle remote specifiers with different folder casing for case insensitive file systems
body:
Say you have two separate modules with different folder casing:

```
https://localhost/FOLDER/mod.ts
https://localhost/folder/mod.ts
```

I believe deno vendor will merge the folders together on case insensitive file systems somewhere along these lines:

```
// upper case sorts before lowercase
localhost/FOLDER/mod.ts
localhost/FOLDER/mod_2.ts
```

It should probably create the following though and not merge the folders together (which could potentially cause issues with the import map):

```
// again, upper case sorts before lowercase so FOLDER won
localhost/FOLDER/mod.ts
localhost/folder_2/mod.ts
```

This would be fixed if we used the windows 260 char path truncation code I wrote in dnt that's outlined in #13692.

This is a super edge case that most users won't run into though.

title: `DecompressionStream` type error in 1.19 example
body:
I'm trying to run the DecompressionStream example from the 1.19 release notes:

```ts
// mod.ts
const input = await Deno.open("./file.txt.gz");
const output = await Deno.create("./file.txt");
await input.readable
  .pipeTo(new DecompressionStream("gzip"))
  .pipeTo(output.writable);
```

but my editor is showing a typeError at `.pipeTo(new DecompressionStream("gzip"))`:

```
Argument of type 'DecompressionStream' is not assignable to parameter of type 'WritableStream<Uint8Array>'.
  Type 'DecompressionStream' is missing the following properties from type 'WritableStream<Uint8Array>': locked, abort, getWriter deno-ts(2345)
```

Also the next line `.pipeTo(output.writable);` shows the following error:

```
Property 'pipeTo' does not exist on type 'Promise<void>'.deno-ts(2339)
```

Because `pipeTo` takes a WritableStream I thought maybe I could use this instead:

```ts
await input.readable.pipeTo(new DecompressionStream("gzip").writable);
```

This fixes the TypeError in my editor IntelliSense (VS Code) but when running the code I get this error:

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) Error: deflate decompression error
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:168:12)
    at Object.flush (deno:ext/web/14_compression.js:99:31)
    at Object.invokeCallbackFunction (deno:ext/webidl/00_webidl.js:848:18)
```

body:
Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno_lint/issues/1017

https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13580 added a new diagnostic that warns us when a module specifier is redirected to another specifier. This really works fine for most cases, but unfortunately modules published from nest.land are supposed to have redirection, therefore getting the redirect diagnostic can be annoying in this case.

title: Skip deno upgrade if user lacks write permission to deno executable
body:
Currently `deno upgrade` downloads the approximately 32mb archive first then informs the user they lack permission to write to /bin.
Instead, it should should do a quick check for write permission to the `deno` executable location prior to downloading to save bandwidth.

title: Failure in test asyncOpsSanitizer when no hint is available for op
body:
I don't have a clear reproduction path for this issue but I dug into the 40_testing.js code and I believe I found the cause of the issue. A user of my test_suite module opened [an issue](https://github.com/udibo/test_suite/issues/16#issuecomment-1046193210) saying they were getting an error when upgrading to the newest version that uses the test step API.  The user reported they are using Deno 1.19. Looking at the error message, it appeared to actually be an issue with Deno's test runner.

The Deno.metrics().ops object has 281 keys. The OP_DETAILS object in [40_testing.js](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/v1.19.0/runtime/js/40_testing.js#L64) only has 67 of those keys.

In the asyncOpSanitizer, if there is a difference in ops dispatched and completed, it will try getting hint information from the OP_DETAILS [here](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/v1.19.0/runtime/js/40_testing.js#L176) using destructuring assignment (shown below). If there isn't a hint in OP_DETAILS for the key, then the value returned from `OP_DETAILS[key]` is going to return undefined and you can't destructure undefined.

```js
const [name, hint] = OP_DETAILS[key];
```

If the key happens to be one that is undefined, this will throw the following error.

```js
TypeError: undefined is not iterable (cannot read property Symbol(Symbol.iterator))
        at asyncOpSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:176:32)
```
author: KyleJune

title: bad UX with prompt by default
body:
In 1.19, dynamic imports are prompted even if they are never executed, which causes a bad UX/DX.

For example given something like this:

```js
const TextEncoder = globalThis.TextEncoder ??
  await import("./polyfill.js").TextEncoder;

console.log(TextEncoder);
```

When running under Deno, the dynamic import will never get executed, but:

```
> deno run example.js
⚠️  ️Deno requests read access to "polyfill.js". Run again with --allow-read to bypass this prompt.
   Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)]  n
[Function: TextEncoder]
```

Whether permission is granted or denied, the code runs as expected.

With no prompt (and versions prior to 1.19):

```
> deno run --no-promot example.js
[Function: TextEncoder]
```

The real world usage is in adding support for Node.js for oak, I needed to dynamically import a couple modules if running under Node.js, so they never get loaded under Deno, but if people haven't granted permissions, they get prompted to load `node_shims.ts` and `http_server_node.ts`, which is very confusing user experience to say the least.

```ts
function nextdir() {
```

author: kitsonk

title: Proposal: disable auto-discovery of config file for `deno run <remote entrypoint>`
body:
If the users have custom typescript settings which are incompatible with default settings (e.g. frontend typings), they will see type errors when they execute the remote Deno scripts.

For example, a user develops frontend lib. `deno.json` has frontend types (e.g. `"lib": ["dom", "esnext"]`, etc). When they want to run, for example, `udd` command in the CI, the command throws type error because of the incompatible type settings of the auto-discovered `deno.json`. (This happens, for example, in https://github.com/hayd/deno-udd/issues/59, and they avoid the error by removing deno.json in CI https://github.com/UltiRequiem/timero/commit/56dc4ba0b64f7deb19607d4b476be88279fcd6ac . This doesn't look great.)

This situation doesn't make sense because `deno.json` is usually only for local scripts. The config is not intended to be applied to all deno programs.

So I suggest we should disable auto-discovery of config file when the command is `deno run <remote entrypoint>`, which fixes the above situation.
author: kt3k

title: use urlpattern instead of path-to-regex for registry completions
body: Currently we use a custom implementation of path-to-regex just for registry completions; ideally we'd use the rust-urlpattern crate instead by exposing some of its internal methods. However, a blocker for this is https://github.com/WICG/urlpattern/issues/73, and also the fact that this change would be breaking, but releasing a v3 currently would make very little sense, as a v2 was released fairly recently and only deno.land has upgraded, whereas crux.land & nest.land still use v1.

author: jaydenseric

title: `deno compile` ignores ` --import-map` in 1.19.0
body:
Using 1.18.2 I can run this:

```
deno compile -A --no-check --unstable --import-map ./modules.json --output test test.js
```

and it works.

In 1.19.0 the same thing doesn't work. Well, the compilation works, but running the executable will throw an error like this one, which is familiar to anyone who forgot using the `--import-map` flag once:

```
error: Relative import path "std/path" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ from "file:///path/to/test.js"
```

This might be related to https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13563. Seems like import map support was forgotten in the change. Also, `deno bundle` seems unaffected by this, but it's obviously not the same feature.

title: Panic parsing JSX opening element with exclamation point before name (ex. `<!doctype html>`)
body:
**Noob Alert**
This my first deno project, I started using deno and deno deploy maybe about 3 weeks ago (and I'm loving it for the most part) - I am a bit in over my head submitting this issue, but wanted to give back.

**Developer's Intention**:
- build out compliant HTML using JSX as a templating/component library - using SSR / JSX implementation from   'https://deno.land/x/sift@0.4.3'

**Complication**:
- Attempted to use `/** @jsxFrag Fragment */` to accomplish an overarching page structure that includes a jsx fragment and a `<!doctype html>` element 

Something like 

```
/** @jsx h */
/** @jsxFrag Fragment */

import { h, jsx, Fragment} from 'https://deno.land/x/sift@0.4.3/mod.ts';

export const pageLayout = ()=>jsx(
		<>
			< !doctype html >
			<html lang='en-US' charSet='UTF-8'>
				<Head/>
				<Body/>
			</html>,
		</>
	);

```

**Project Pinpoint for Context**
- First noticed the panic [here](https://github.com/ericdmoore/feedBarber/commit/d07b213d1838923a5e23df94fce88d3006875642#diff-d8b6106916edb81d3cc29a91f9bc2b3c68c130e3641af1cc3fb6851dd5d5959bR46)

- Can get around this by buffering the whole response into memory and prepending a string - note [here](https://github.com/ericdmoore/feedBarber/commit/2c70e49c8f2f8c6c0e4cc1d8e3ea08745350bf35)

title: deno bundleForNode {filename.js/ts} -- --output o.js command
body:
## suggestion

author: elycheikhsmail

title: Support `deno run --no-cache` flag or `{ cache: false }` import option to prevent caching of imports
body:
# Feature Request

I would like to be able to prevent Deno from caching imports on certain runs.

# Use Case

I want to build a tool that supports importing many different kinds of files, such as `.vue` and `.svelte` files, in a server-side dev environment (similar to the [`vite.ssrLoadModule`](https://vitejs.dev/guide/ssr.html#setting-up-the-dev-server) function). I know of no good way to transform/compile a local import on the fly, so my solution is to use a local server and `import` the file using the localhost url. This works, except Deno detects those imports as remote, not local, so it caches them. I don't want those files cached, but I can't find any way to prevent it.

# Proposed API

I think ideally I would like to be able to disable caching on a per-import basis. This could be an extra flag on `import` options, maybe in a special `deno` section:

```js
import('http://localhost:8000/index.svelte', { deno: { cache: false } });
```

An alternative would be to support a `--no-cache` flag:

```
deno run --no-cache ...
```

title: Octal literals and decimals with leading zeroes wrongly allowed in REPL
body:
When first trying out Deno's REPL my first thought was to check if it was properly running on strict mode, my go-to test for this is as simple as: `077`, an octal literal of decimal value `63`.

The test is simple, if the REPL returns `63`, it's not on strict mode, if it throws a strict mode SyntaxError, it's on strict mode.

Disappointingly, Deno REPL returned me `63`:
![SmartSelect_20220225-052234_Termux](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32803471/155680706-55bf6a26-2100-40e7-817a-8243911cd21c.jpg)
As a double check, in the image above I also attempted a decimal with leading zeroes: `099`, which should also throw a strict mode SyntaxError, you can see Deno REPL yet again disappoints.

The [expected results](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Errors/Deprecated_octal) seen in Node for comparison:
![SmartSelect_20220225-052901_Termux](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32803471/155681608-8f3d0659-1b7e-4d3b-9760-5664854891e6.jpg)

After these disappointing results, I went looking for information on how to enable strict mode on Deno's REPL, similar to Node's `--use_strict` REPL flag. To my surprise what I found was several references here on the GitHub issues claiming Deno REPL *is* strict mode by default, and found no such flag, so my guess is, Deno either incorrectly implements strict mode (bug) or it doesn't run in strict mode by default anymore and the info I found is outdated or was never right, since I could not find said info in any official capacity, as https://dotland-fresh.deno.dev/manual@v1.19.1/tools/repl doesn't mention anything regarding this for instance, which seems like a flaw of incomplete documentation that should also be made clearer.

title: file_server.ts uses ReadableStream
body:
https://deno.land/std@0.127.0/http/file_server.ts#L341

Uses `ReadableStream`, but if I understand correctly that has been deprecated:

https://deno.com/blog/v1.16#improvements-to-web-streams-api

> `ReadableStream.getIterator` has been removed. This method has been deprecated since Deno 1.7, and was never implemented in any browser. The `ReadableStream` itself has always implemented the `AsyncIterable` protocol, so use that instead when iterating over a stream.

I get the following error when trying to compile `file_server.ts` under deno 1.19.1:

title: Add fast path in TextDecoder for non streaming decodes
body:
See https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/web/08_text_encoding.js#L98.

Currently for a non streaming decode we perform 3 native calls:

- `op_encoding_new_decoder` to create the decoder
- `op_encoding_decode` to actually perform the decoding
- `core.close` to destroy the decoder

For cases where `options.stream === false` this can be reduced to a single decode op.

To do this, add a new `op_encoding_decode_single` op that performs a single decode on a fresh decoder. Then add a JS fast path to the decode function.

author: kitsonk

title: Improve `AggregateError` console output
body:
A standard [`AggregateError`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/AggregateError) instance has an `errors` property containing an array of the aggregated errors.

It would be really useful if Deno displayed these errors when printing an `AggregateError` instance in the console, much like how it displays the `cause` property.

For example, with `demo.mjs`:

```js
const aggregateError = new AggregateError([
  new Error("Error message 1."),
  new Error("Error message 2."),
], "Multiple errors.");

console.error(aggregateError);

throw aggregateError;
```

Run with Deno:

```
deno run demo.mjs
```

Currently results in:

```
AggregateError: Multiple errors.
    at file://[redacted]/demo.mjs:1:24
error: Uncaught AggregateError: Multiple errors.
const aggregateError = new AggregateError([
                       ^
    at file://[redacted]/demo.mjs:1:24
```

As you can see, it doesn't surface the messages of the aggregated errors.

Unfortunately all sorts of LSP or VS Code Deno extension bugs seem to happen with this workflow; apparently it trips up over the port being encoded in the module URLs? It's the only thing I can tell that's different when comparing modules that work unless headers somehow play a role. Here is an example of an error in VS Code that happens when command clicking on the name of an import from a locally hosted module: ``` Unable to open 'useCss.mjs': Unable to resolve text model content for resource deno:/http/localhost%3A3002/useCss.mjs. ``` Also, when hovering the import name and holding command for other modules it shows source in the tooltip, but for these ones only the regular hover tooltip appears and in the VS Code Deno Language Server output tab a log like this appears: ``` The source was not found: deno:/http/localhost%3A3002/useCss.mjs ```

title: `Deno.ListenOptions` should have `port` as an optional property

title: compat: tracking issue for starting a remix project
body:
This issue tracks the problems which blocks starting a new remix project with node.js compat mode of deno.

**Issues with `npx create-remix`** (wizard for creating a new remix project)
- [ ] `TypeError: child_process.execSync is not a function` (used for calling `npm install` in the wizard)

**Issues with `npm install` in remix project**
- [ ] Can't perform `postinstall` script of `core-js-pure` module https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/2013
  - It has `node -e "try{require('./postinstall')}catch(e){}"` in `postinstall`
  - node compat mode doesn't understand `-e` option

**Issues with `npm run dev` in remix project**
- [x] `TypeError: Invalid signal: SIGBREAK` https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/pull/2014
- [x] Error: Not implemented: `ChildProcess.kill()` with the `signal` parameter denoland/deno_std#2083
- [x] `handle.unref()` is not implemented https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/b81ff8e06a134d4ec8940b0fad1440629b0161e7/node/internal_binding/handle_wrap.ts#L46
  - remix uses [get-port](https://www.npmjs.com/package/get-port) module for getting free port, and that module calls [net.createServer().unref()](https://github.com/sindresorhus/get-port/blob/0760c987c17581395d4e30432881dcb0ca6ca94a/index.js#L45)
  - Need unref'd TCP Listener
  - prerequisite work https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13886
- [x] `Not implemented: ChildProcess.unref()`
- [x] `child_process.execFileSync is not a function` used in [esbuild](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/blob/351eb5b49b0a35139387110bb6bf9d10bbcf2292/lib/npm/node.ts#L368)
- [ ] `Cannot read properties of undefined` at `req.connection.remoteAddress` (req.connection is missing) in [forwarded](https://npm.im/forwarded)
- [ ] `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'encrypted')` at `this.connection.encrypted` in [express](https://npm.im/express)
- [ ] `TypeError: this._implicitHeader is not a function` at `compression/index.js:103` in [express](https://npm.im/express)

author: codehz

title: Feature Request: `ref`/`unref` methods for `Deno.Listener`
body:
`net.Server` class in Node.js API has `ref`/`unref` methods which controls `ref` status of tcp server (unref'd server allows the program to exit when the server is still running). https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#serverunref

We need to have similar methods in Deno.Listener to simulate the above Node.js feature in compat mode.
author: kt3k

title: Better testing for Amazon Linux on the CI
body:
Currently tests run in ubuntu 16 sysroot along with a simple "check":
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L418-L421

Ideally, unit tests should run in an actual environment to ensure GLIBC [issues](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13516) don't happen again. A 1gig self-hosted ec2 runner would work.

body:

WPTs have already been written, and are available at https://github.com/web-platform-tests/wpt/pull/32825.
author: lucacasonato

title: Consider making the `Deno` namespace not read only.
body:
I wanted to mock some Deno functions for unit tests, but I was surprised to see that `Deno = {}` or `globalThis.Deno = {}` results in:
```
Uncaught TypeError: Cannot assign to read only property 'Deno' of object '#<Window>'
    at <anonymous>:2:17
```
Since #11033 we are already able to modify the `Deno` object if we want to mock or polyfill things. So tbh, I'd be fine if the `Deno` object stayed read only. I was just a bit surprised since none of the other globals are read only. For instance:
```js
globalThis.WebSocket = {}
globalThis.Symbol = {}
globalThis = {}
```
author: jespertheend

title: "module not found" for binaries compiled with 1.19
body:
The following example worked fine in 🦖 Deno 1.18, but fails in Deno 1.19

```
// example.ts
import Yargs from 'https://deno.land/x/yargs@v17.3.0-deno/deno.ts';
Yargs();
```
Compile:
```
deno compile -A --unstable example.ts 
```

then run `./example`
This fails with:
```
error: Module not found
```
This worked with Deno 1.18 and earlier.
author: irbull

title: Crypto ImportKey: unknown/unsupported ASN.1 DER tag: 0x2d at DER byte 1
body:
### Description

I am trying to use the Crypto API to import a PKS8 private key for use in a PS256 JWS, however, I receive the following error when running the code below: `DataError: unknown/unsupported ASN.1 DER tag: 0x2d at DER byte 1`

### Reproduction

```ts
import { crypto } from "https://deno.land/std@0.129.0/crypto/mod.ts";

// Generate WebKey from private key (for use in PS256 token)
const key = await crypto.subtle.importKey(
    "pkcs8",
    await Deno.readFile("./private.key"),
    { name: "RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5", hash: "SHA-256" },
    false,
    ["sign"]
); // DataError: unknown/unsupported ASN.1 DER tag: 0x2d at DER byte 1
```

title: Debugging broken
body:
Sorry for the vague title, not sure how to make it more specific.

I was following the tutorial - https://deno.land/manual@v1.19.3/getting_started/debugging_your_code  
...and none of what that page describes actually happens.

`deno v1.19.3`  
`Chrome v99`  
`Win 10 21H2`

What you get from the debugger is this:
![DenoDebug](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8262590/157863647-1270d682-8154-41f1-8869-9f5bbbd4587c.png)

title: SubtleCrypto.importKey issue with JWK and "use"
body:
The following import should not fail (does not in Chrome, does not in Node)

```js
const jwk = {
  "kty": "EC",
  "use": "sig",
  "kid": "MFZeG102dQiqbANoaMlW_Jmf7fOZmtRsHt77JFhTpF0",
  "alg": "ES256",
  "crv": "P-256",
  "x": "FWZ9rSkLt6Dx9E3pxLybhdM6xgR5obGsj5_pqmnz5J4",
  "y": "_n8G69C-A2Xl4xUW2lF0i8ZGZnk_KPYrhv4GbTGu5G4"
}

const algorithm = { name: 'ECDSA', namedCurve: 'P-256' }

await crypto.subtle.importKey('jwk', jwk, algorithm, true, ['verify'])
```

That being said, in Deno this fails with

```
DOMException: 'use' property of JsonWebKey must be 'undefined'
    at new DOMException (deno:ext/web/01_dom_exception.js:100:21)
    at importKeyEC (deno:ext/crypto/00_crypto.js:2547:17)
    at SubtleCrypto.importKey (deno:ext/crypto/00_crypto.js:905:18)
    at <anonymous>:2:15
```


```
$ deno --version
deno 1.19.2 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 9.9.115.7
typescript 4.5.2
```

author: dsherret

title: Add `deno check` subcommand
body:
I couldn't find an issue for this. This sub command would only do type checking. I believe @kitsonk had most of this work done before. We could get this work done before 2.0.

I think this could just essentially be an alias for `deno cache` at the moment and in the future `deno cache` wouldn't type check.
author: dsherret

title: Determine correct `moduleDetection` compiler option (TypeScript 4.7)
body:
Ref: https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/pull/47495

The next minor release of TypeScript (4.7) should include a new `moduleDetection` option, which has to deal with how modules are detected by TypeScript when type checking.  The default is `"auto"`, which might be ok for us, but also there is `"force"` which is more the behaviour that we would expect for code running under Deno.  We need to do a bit of experiment and testing.

The issue mostly manifested itself around issues in the lsp when having a file open with no top level import or export statement being treated a global script for type checking purposes.  There were also some issues with emitting `@jsxImportSource` with modules that appeared to be scripts.
author: kitsonk

title: ops panic in net/ext with a tls operation
author: riimalaj

title: Structured clone serialization errors have the wrong message
body:
The `v8::ValueSerializerImpl` trait has a `throw_data_clone_error` method that must be implemented, which provides embedders with an error message indicating why some object couldn't be serialized. Deno's implementation of that method throws an exception with that error message, which is then supposed to be caught in ext/web and turned into a `DataCloneError` `DOMException` as required by the HTML spec.

However, the resulting exception does not have the V8-provided message – it instead has the message "Failed to serialize response". This can be seen when running the code in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13848#issuecomment-1059979657.

This seems to happen because the check for serialization errors in https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/core/bindings.rs#L1193 does not check if an exception has been previously thrown.
author: andreubotella

title: Deno coverage panics on unicode symbols
body:
# Problem description
Running the deno coverage command against the coverage report of code that printed any unicode character will lead to a panic.

# Steps to reproduce

Create a sample file that prints any unicode character, e.g. `app.ts`:
```ts
export function cheers() {
  console.log('🍻');
}

cheers();
```

Create a simple test that calls the given function, e.g. `app.test.ts`:
```ts
import { cheers } from './app.ts'

Deno.test("It should print an emoji", () => {
    cheers();
})
```

Now try to create a coverage report and watch Deno panic.

# Output without backtrace

```log
[tmp]➡ deno run app.ts
🍻
[tmp]➡ deno test app.test.ts --coverage=cov
🍻
running 1 test from file:///tmp/app.test.ts
test It should print an emoji ...🍻

 ok (6ms)

test result: ok. 1 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (64ms)

[tmp]➡ deno coverage cov

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: linux x86_64
Version: 1.19.3
Args: ["deno", "coverage", "cov"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: BadJson(Error("invalid unicode code point", line: 1, column: 280))', cli/tools/coverage/mod.rs:173:57
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
[tmp]➡ 
```

author: marc2332

title: Exception for missing ops in `OP_DETAILS`
body:
If an async op is not listed in the `OP_DETAILS` map in `40_testing.js`, you can get the following exception:

```
TypeError: undefined is not iterable (cannot read property Symbol(Symbol.iterator))
    at asyncOpSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:177:32)
    at async resourceSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:369:7)
    at async Object.exitSanitizer [as fn] (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:426:9)
    at async runTest (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:786:7)
    at async Object.runTests (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:984:22)
```

Presumably some fallback message should show up in these cases, in case a new op is mistakenly left out, and for ease of development.

Additionally, some async ops related to resources (`op_read`, `op_write`, `op_shutdown`) and workers (`op_host_recv_ctrl`, `op_host_recv_message`) are currently missing.
author: andreubotella

title: Cleanup core examples once macros can inspect source file
body:
Following https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13861, some of `core/examples/*` have a "hackish" `use deno_core::*;` statement to workaround the fact that the `#[op]` macro can't detect that they should use `deno_core::` instead of `crate::`.

This is blocked on https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/54725
author: AaronO

title: `deno_ops` macro tracking issue
body:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13861 Introduces the `deno_op` proc macro crate. The goal is to produce highly optimized v8 Function callbacks from existing deno ops & provide base for future v8-fast-api work.

Here we tracking down a few things to follow-up after the PR lands:

- [x] Cleanup OpDecl (@AaronO) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13952
- [x] Variable op arity (@AaronO) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13949
- [x] ~~Optimize away unused args (i.e: _foo vs only _)~~ (@AaronO) (not relevant now that all args are optional)
- [x] Allow ops without OpState argument. (@AaronO) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13954
- [x] Infallible / Result-free ops (@AaronO)
- [ ] Less verbose bindings init (@AaronO)
- [ ] Less opcall indirection (@AaronO)
- [ ] Move promise ring to Rust https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13906
- [ ] Move unwrapOpResult to Rust (@AaronO)
- [ ] Make [macro generated code](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13861/files#diff-edfa881474570f1c987c98cafcb22b4a45f8861d5f91a642cb7431f51ea42a5cR63-R86) implement a common trait. (@littledivy)
- [ ] Better source file inspection (blocked on rustc) https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13939 (@littledivy)
- [ ] Inbuilt permission handling with proc macro attributes. `#[op(unstable)]` (@littledivy)
- [x] Allow sync ops to directly accept `Rc` opstate instead of a mutable borrow across it's lifetime. (@littledivy)

```

        If you tried to supply `--max-old-space-size` as a value rather than a flag, use `-- --max-old-space-size`

USAGE:
    deno run --compat --unstable --no-check=<NO_CHECK_TYPE>... --allow-all <SCRIPT_ARG>...

For more information try --help
```
author: hinogi

title: Disable snapshots when cross compiling
body:
When cross-compiling, snapshots are built for the host architecture instead of the target. There is no easy workaround for this.

Unblocks our CI to produce the following targets: 
- `aarch64-android-linux`
- `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu`
    - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/1846
    - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/4862

title: Is there any plan to add support on mongoose library for Deno?
body:
I like Deno and I am working on a project using it. But it seems it's MongoDB driver doesn't support Mongoose library and I don't know how can I use schemas in my Deno application?

May I ask either if there is any other alternatives for Mongoose in Deno or a plan to support Mongoose or schemas in Deno soon?

author: ry

title: `handleWasmStreaming` should use fetch's "extract a MIME type"
body: [The spec for `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming()` and `WebAssembly.compileStreaming()`](https://webassembly.github.io/spec/web-api/#compile-a-potential-webassembly-response) specifies that the MIME type has to be extracted with the Fetch spec's [extract a MIME type](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-header-extract-mime-type) algorithm. In Deno currently we are instead getting the `Content-Type` header from the response and comparing it case-insensitively to `"application/wasm"`. However, `ext/fetch` does have an implementation for "extract a MIME type" in `response[_mimeType]()`. We should use that instead.
author: andreubotella

title: Duplicated implementation of "extract a MIME type" in `23_request.js` and `23_response.js`
body:
Both `Request.prototype[_mimeType]` and `Response.prototype[_mimeType]` implement the [extract a MIME type](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-header-extract-mime-type) algorithm. These should be deduplicated.

In fact, it would make sense to implement this algorithm in `ext/web/01_mimesniff.js`, taking `values` as its argument rather than a header list.
author: andreubotella

title: Don't spawn a thread when terminating a worker
body:
#12658, which is a still unfixed V8 bug, was worked around by first signaling to the worker that its event loop should stop whenever possible, and then starting a new thread that would wait for two seconds and then kill the worker's isolate, in case the worker was stuck in a JS endless loop. I felt it necessary to perform this waiting in a new thread rather than in a task on the current thread's event loop because the current event loop might finish well before the process finished, and it was not acceptable to leave the worker unkilled in that case.

I've since realized that, since child workers don't survive termination of their parent, it's fine to kill any not-yet-killed workers when their parent's event loop is finishing. In order to do this, there should be a set in the current `OpState` to store the isolate handles (and `has_terminated` flags?) for scheduled-but-not-yet-killed workers, with a `Drop` impl that kills all workers still in the set. Then, `WebWorkerHandle::terminate` should:
- kill the worker immediately if it's being called when the current thread's event loop is terminating
- otherwise, schedule termination, add the worker's isolate handle to the set, and spawn a tokio task that waits for two seconds and then kills the worker and removes it from the set.

#13941 is a precondition for this.
author: andreubotella

title: Prompt changes and testing
body: With prompt by default, allowing a permission in one module's test doesn't carry over to another test.  For example if module `a.test.ts` requires net access, and it is granted, it will not carry over to tests in `b.test.ts` and you will get re-prompted again.

title: How to Deno.listen on existing file descriptor?
body:
Hello,

I apologize if this has already been answered, I looked everywhere I could - I promise!

How does one use Deno.listen() to listen on an existing file descriptor / resource?
**OR**
Please allow Deno.listen() to listen on existing file descriptors / resources.

Similarly Deno.read(), Deno.write(), etc. allows one to read/write/etc. from a given resource id (which is obviously an essential function as files are a widely adopted system concept). Using the Deno documentation I was able to successfully utilize Deno.listen() to simultaneously create a socket() resource and to bind() to it, which is neat, but in some particular instances one would prefer not to (or can not) do that first step as there already exists a socket (that can still be bound to). 

For example, the popular software `spawn-fcgi` creates a socket, executes a specified process, and assigns the socket to fd 0. 

If this feature isn't yet available, may I suggest this syntax: `Deno.listen(0)` where 0 represents Deno's first resource. This makes it similar to Deno's read(), write(), etc functions and also virtually every other popular definition of listen() (see both posix and win32). I'd greatly prefer that syntax, but if that isn't acceptable then the next obvious form would be `Deno.listen({rid: 0})`. Additionally, in my opinion `Deno.listen({transport: "rid", rid: 0})` or similar is **not** preferable.

Also if this feature isn't yet available, could someone direct me towards some literature that illustrates how to utilize either op calls or Deno's newer FFI API to just use the system's listen() functionality in an async manner? With the instance I have in mind FFI and "unstable" is fine and listen() is available, but Deno.run() is not (nor is any foreign subprocessing functions). 

Thank you!

Update: I just discovered [op_net_accept](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/net/ops.rs#L162) which I'll attempt to experiment with, but some usage hints would still be appreciated.

When I run `cargo test` on my machine, I get a message about `integration::run::issue9750` timing out, and `cargo test` never finishes. When I run that test specifically, it also never finishes running, but if you print its stdout (with `-- --nocapture`), you see that the test in fact fails:

```
OUTPUT Enter \'yy\':\r\n
INPUT yy\n
ECHO: yy\r\n
OUTPUT ⚠️  ️Deno requests env access. Run again with --allow-env to bypass this prompt.\r\n   Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)]  
INPUT n\n
ECHO: n\r\n
OUTPUT ⚠️  ️Deno requests env access to \"SECRET\". Run again with --allow-env to bypass this prompt.\r\n   Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)]  
thread 'integration::run::issue9750' panicked at 'assertion failed: `(left == right)`

Diff < left / right > :
<⚠️  ️Deno requests env access to "SECRET". Run again with --allow-env to bypass this prompt.
>⚠️  ️Deno requests env access to "SECRET". Run again with --allow-run to bypass this prompt.
    Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)]

', test_util/src/lib.rs:1910:11
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

The actual permissions prompt correctly asks for `--allow-env`, but [the test expects it to ask for `--allow-run`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/tests/integration/run_tests.rs#L2337).

I don't know why the test keeps running, though, or why this hasn't been observed in CI.

title: Specifying `permissions: {}` seems to inherit all permissions
body:
**Summary:**

[Deno 1.20 Release Notes](https://deno.com/blog/v1.20#breaking-stricter-defaults-in-programatic-permissions-for-tests-and-workers) say that the deafult behavior of the `permissions` option has changed. However, it does not appear to have actually changed :thinking:

**Steps to reproduce:**

```ts
Deno.test({
```

author: harrysolovay

title: bench: compare implementations
body:
I'd like a way to use `Deno.bench` to define performance "tests" for competing implementations of a solution so that I can have confidence that one implementation remains faster than others when things change.

e g. A new Deno/V8 runtime could cause generators to out perform an array-based solution of an algorithm. A new library dependency version could speed up or slow down a function. etc.

This way I can write multiple implementations of a solution, declare one as the current winner and have it serve as the baseline implementation going forward, and have `deno bench` fail if an implementation performs better than it going forward.

The alternate implementations also serve as references as faster implementations of some algorithms are often harder to read and understand than naive implementations that may be easier to read but do not perform as well.

author: tim-palmer

title: [feature request] Support header hints for newer versions and known vulnerabilities
body:
I thought a long time for the best way to allow yarn like "update all", listing of outdated dependencies and showing known vulnerabilities in deno without introducing central entities or being non idiomatic for deno and this is what i came up with:

support two  new http headers on code download:

given i reference a code file similar to: 

`import { green } from 'https://deno.land/std@0.126.0/fmt/colors.ts'`

when deno would fetch this file, whenever the following http headers are present log a realtime warning:

for hinting to a later version:
`Link: <https://deno.land/std@0.128.0/fmt/colors.ts>; rel="latest-version"`

for warning about known vulnerabilities:
`Link: <https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2021-1472>; rel="vulnerability", <https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2022-1474>; rel="vulnerability"`


additionally deno would do a  cheap http HEAD request upon using  a locally cached resources to warn about outdated or vulnerable dependencies, with cached result to not do a check more than once x days. this also has the nice effect that the head request would discover availability issues of external referenced dependencies so we could warn that a rebuild/refetch would fail.

additionally we can introduce a 'deno scan' or 'deno outdated' command that does an ad hoc refetch of the headers for all referenced files.

Considered alternatives:
- well known path of json file with versions and vulnerabilities, pros: no need for server support to add the headers, cons: we would have to find a way to know at what point of the path we need to check for this file and this would put a strict structure expectation of what a "package" is but this is much more flexible in deno than nodejs, also this would mean noise when trying to load the version and vulnerability files from servers that don't have those, similar to annoying search engine requests to robots.txt on all webservers

- checking in a reposity/service, pros: would have a central configurable source of truth similar to how artifactory etc. are setup, cons: this would bring back a lot of annoyances from npm  that we tried to get rid of such as having to configure a package server and complex setups for multiple sources of code

title: WebSocketStream reading not reliable
body:
### Issue Details

* **Deno Version:**
  * deno 1.19.3 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
  * v8 9.9.115.8
  * typescript 4.5.2
* **Operating System:**
  * Windows 10 21H1

### Expected Behavior
While using async/await there should never be an error claiming module eval is still pending.

### Actual Behavior

While using async/await, it will return a module eval error after a while `error: Module evaluation is still pending but there are no pending ops or dynamic imports. This situation is often caused by unresolved promises.`

### To Reproduce

 - Copy the [Async/await](https://gist.github.com/Officialstrike/1fb2ece6c011b82ed123c5c42cc13603)  code
 - run the file using `$ deno run --unstable --allow-net  <file name here> `
- Wait a couple of seconds, and then it will produce an error.

author: kitsonk

title: Automatic Body Compression Clarification
body:
It should be made clear in the manual how Deno internally handles automatic body compression:

1. Does Deno internally maintain a cache of compressed bodies?
2. If so, how long do those cache entries last?

If Deno does not maintain a cache, it could be inefficient for servers with high traffic to compress bodies upon every request, especially during a denial-of-service attack.

If Deno does maintain a cache, it is not specified how long cache entries last, and long-lived servers may end up keeping cache entries that are no longer needed.

A potential solution would be to specify that Deno does indeed utilize a cache, and to provide a Deno API to inspect/remove entries from such a cache.

title: Import maps don't always work with `deno run`
body:
I have the following files:

```ts
// hello-world.ts

console.log("hello world 1");
```

```ts
// hello-world-2.ts

console.log("hello world 2");
```

And this import map file:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "./hello-world.ts": "./hello-world-2.ts",
    "hello-world": "./hello-world-2.ts"
  }
}
```

Now I run the first file:

```
deno run hello-world.ts                             
> hello world 1
```

If I run the same file including the import map (to resolve `hello-world.ts` to `hello-world-2.ts`), it works as expected:

```
deno run --import-map=import_map.json hello-world.ts
> hello world 2
```

The import map has two imports: `./hello-world.ts` and `hello-worlds`. If I want to use the second import, it doesn't work:

```
deno run --import-map=import_map.json hello-world   
error: Module not found "file:///Users/oscarotero/test/hello-world".
```
author: oscarotero

title: feat(cli): `deno shell` subcommand
body:
Since there is `deno task` and `deno_task_shell`, it would be neat if there was also a `deno shell` subcommand that works like a real shell. I was thinking of the following features:

- Run deno shell commands on commandline: `deno shell -c "ls | wc -l"` and `echo "ls | wc -l" | deno shell`
- Start a deno shell/repl with: `deno shell`
- Execute scripts as deno shell scripts if it starts with a deno shell shebang: `#!/usr/bin/env -S deno shell`

    This would enable support of moving large deno tasks into files:

    > ./deno.json

    ```json
    {
      "tasks": {
        "build": "./my_task"
      }
    }
    ```

    > ./my_task

    ```shell
    #!/usr/bin/env -S deno shell

    ls | wc -l
    # ...
    ```

author: ry

title: deno vendor with importMap fails to resolve modules
body:
# Problem

`deno run --import-map=vendor/import_map.json mod.ts` fails to resolve dependencies after dependencies vendor'd via `deno vendor --import_map=...`.

Error: `# error: Relative import path "oak/mod.ts" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../`

# Discussion

**works**:

```
$ deno run --import-map import_map.json  -A --unstable /app/src/bin.ts
```

**doesn't work**:

```
# vendor the deps, using the ~same flags as above
$ deno vendor --import-map=import_map.json /app/src/bin.ts

# run with vendored
$ deno run --import-map=/app/vendor/import_map.json --unstable --allow-env --allow-net /app/src/bin.ts
# yields:
# error: Relative import path "oak/mod.ts" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../
```

# Reproduction

Easy!

- `git clone https://github.com/cdaringe/deno-emit-esm-server.git`
- `cd deno-emit-esm-server`
- `git checkout b23071be397a2d2e7e7ca53b9df0832de3241bb9`
- (optional) Prove that the server works
    - run it:`deno run --import-map=import_map.json --unstable --allow-env --allow-net ./src/bin.ts` (no output)
    - access it: `curl http://localhost:7777/github/denoland/deno_std/main/uuid/mod.ts` (observe JS emitted to stdout)
- `deno vendor --import-map=import_map.json ./src/bin.ts`
- `deno run --import-map=vendor/import_map.json --unstable --allow-env --allow-net ./src/bin.ts`
  - Observe the module resolution failure

title: test issue - compile_windows_ext
body:
In #14078 a previously ignored test was unignored (`integration::compile:: compile_windows_ext`), and CI passed, but it appears the reason why it was ignored is because when testing a release build the test attempts to download a binary that doesn't exist yet, so CI passes _except_ when it is a release PR, when the test fails.

We need to figure out a way to have the test download the previous build.

author: tru9

title: Remove `fetch(input: URL, ...)` signature from lib.deno_fetch for 2.0
body: We deprecated `fetch(input: URL, ...)` signature in #14113. We should remove this in 2.0.
author: kt3k

title: Proposal: resolve executable paths in Deno.run for more fine grained allow-run permissions
author: andykais

title: Support vendoring modules which read "static" files, like `.wasm`
body:
I would like to `deno vendor` a module that uses WebAssembly (WASM). It is compiled using `wasm-bindgen` so there is a directory structure like this:

```
pkg/
├── file.js
├── file_bg.wasm
├── file.d.ts
```

The `.js` file contains glue code that calls into the `.wasm` file. It is vendored successfully but tries to fetch the WASM from a URL like this:

```js
input = new URL('file_bg.wasm', import.meta.url);
```

Unfortunately, after vendoring the URL points to a path in the local filesystem, but nothing is there because the `.wasm` file isn't brought over. So it is not possible to run the module locally after vendoring.
author: GJZwiers

title: Suggestion: Supporting Native AOT compilers for Denojs like Hermes, NectarJS or ChowJS
body: `deno compile` is good but compiling to purely native binary is better, by using an AOT compiler like NecatrJS or ChowJS
author: yassCan

title: REPL: parse error for bare `module` identifier
body:
I would expect `module` to work like any other identifier, but that doesn't appear to be the case.

> $ deno

```
Deno 1.20.3
exit using ctrl+d or close()

> const foo = 42
undefined
> foo
42

> const module = 'left-pad'
undefined
> module
parse error: Unexpected eof at 1:7
> module.length
8
> module;
"left-pad"
> (module)
"left-pad"
```

It works fine in scripts:

> $ cat ./script.js

```javascript
const module = 'left-pad'
console.log(module)
module
```

> $ deno run ./script.js

```
left-pad
```

I'm guessing this is ASI-related (it's mentioned [here](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Lexical_grammar#automatic_semicolon_insertion) (section 3) as some kind of statement, though I can't find any other references to it), but if it is interpreted as a built-in statement, I would expect it to be prohibited as an identifier, e.g.:

> $ deno

```
Deno 1.20.3
exit using ctrl+d or close()
> const continue = 42
parse error: Expected ident at 1:7
```

---

> $ deno --version

- deno: 1.20.3 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
- v8: 10.0.139.6
- typescript: 4.6.2
author: chocolateboy

title: Proposal: import permissions
body:
## Why
Recent controversy about `node-ipc` showcased node's security weakness. People would think that deno is bulletproof for this kind of issues, but it isn't.

## Let me elaborate:
Even if you'd import non-malicious module with semver – you're fine until its time to update. You're mostly probably just updating those numbers in imports and maybe refactoring code, you don't check whether module has became dangerous.

## Idea
Here comes my idea – import permissions.
By default you could take two directions:
 - Inherit permissions from the entry module, fully backwards compatible
 - Allow none of the permissions by default, breaking change
That way, we would be 100% safe that modules imported by us wouldn't do what we need them to do.

## Syntax
Syntax is debatable, however I see this coming closely to import assertions, e.g.:
```ts
// static imports
import * as malicious from "https://deno.land/x/some-random-module/mod.ts" allow { read: true } // when allowing none by default
import * as malicious from "https://deno.land/x/some-random-module/mod.ts" allow { read: true, write: false, run: false } // when inheriting permissions
import * as malicious "https://deno.land/x/some-random-module/mod.ts" allow { read: "/tmp" } // allow reading only specific directory

// dynamic imports
const malicious = await import("https://deno.land/x/some-random-module/mod.ts", {
  allow: {
    read: true,
  },
})
```
author: Im-Beast

author: sigmaSd

title: Consider keeping the scripts in `/tools` from importing network modules
body:
The `/tools` directory in the Deno repo is home to a number of scripts used for Deno development, cutting releases and other things. For security, it would seem preferable to have only things vendored within the repo as dependencies, to avoid loading modules from the network. This is already done for the standard library, which is vendored in `/test_util/std`, but here are other instances:

- `/tools/upload_wptfyi.js` imports [`compress`](https://deno.land/x/compress@v0.4.1) version 0.4.1 from deno.land/x
- `/tools/wpt/runner.ts` imports [`deno_dom`](https://deno.land/x/deno_dom@v0.1.3-alpha2) version 0.1.3 alpha 2 from deno.land/x
- `/tools/release/deps.ts` imports the [`denoland/automation`](https://github.com/denoland/automation) github, from tag 0.5.0.
- `/tools/bench/rebootstrap.js` imports `path/mod.ts` and `fs/mod.ts` from the latest version of the standard library at deno.land/std.

author: jespertheend

title: Make help clearer about log level
body:
People new to Deno might assume that the `-L` and `--log-level` flags affect _all_ logging including `console.*` (see: #14143).  Current help is certainly ambiguous:

```
    -L, --log-level <log-level>
            Set log level
            
            [possible values: debug, info]

```

We should make it clear that it only applies to internal bugging.  Something like:

> Set log level for internal logging. Does not impact logging via `console`.
author: kitsonk

title: Default Deno.bench warmups may not be high enough for some CI runners
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions/14147
Originally posted by **GJZwiers** March 29, 2022 Recently I have been benchmarking with `Deno.bench` and having fun, although it seems that on some CI runners the default 1000 warmups is not sufficient. On the Ubuntu runner in Github Actions in particular there is a major difference in the measured time of whatever benchmark is run first, compared to other runners and my local machine. For example, I am benching a JS and WASM implementation of a string distance algorithm. The results are very consistent on my local setup and the MacOS/Windows runners: JS is faster on relatively short strings while WASM is faster on long ones: ``` bench Myers 32bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 1,035 ns/iter (700..59,100 ns/iter) ok (17ms) bench Myers 32bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 2,726 ns/iter (2,400..8,900 ns/iter) ok (12ms) bench Myers 1024bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 425,071 ns/iter (401,300..565,900 ns/iter) ok (884ms) bench Myers 1024bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 80,577 ns/iter (78,500..122,900 ns/iter) ok (166ms) ``` On the Ubuntu runner though, there is a time anomaly in the first bench, showing a much higher average `ns/iter`: ``` bench Myers 32bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 10,783 ns/iter (9,300..88,499 ns/iter) ok (35ms) bench Myers 32bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 6,350 ns/iter (3,400..60,500 ns/iter) ok (24ms) bench Myers 1024bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 673,224 ns/iter (556,596..3,331,975 ns/iter) ok (1s) bench Myers 1024bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 152,748 ns/iter (118,299..4,364,268 ns/iter) ok (294ms) ``` I can actually shift the extra time in the CI to a different benchmark by having it run first: ``` bench test bench JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 9,218 ns/iter (1,400..96,701 ns/iter) ok (59ms) bench Myers 32bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 1,322 ns/iter (1,000..5,600 ns/iter) ok (16ms) bench Myers 32bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 4,417 ns/iter (4,300..22,500 ns/iter) ok (33ms) bench Myers 1024bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 750,559 ns/iter (736,909..3,943,152 ns/iter) ok (1s) bench Myers 1024bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 141,181 ns/iter (140,301..225,303 ns/iter) ok (290ms) ``` Using more warmups solves this problem, somewhere around 3000-5000 giving results that are more in line with my expectation: ``` bench Myers 32bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 1,470 ns/iter (1,100..64,300 ns/iter) ok (47ms) bench Myers 32bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 4,473 ns/iter (4,100..129,601 ns/iter) ok (17ms) bench Myers 1024bp string JavaScript ... 1000 iterations 739,221 ns/iter (731,106..1,865,416 ns/iter) ok (1s) bench Myers 1024bp string WebAssembly ... 1000 iterations 144,435 ns/iter (143,501..224,102 ns/iter) ok (298ms) ``` Don't know if anyone else has encountered something similar, but this made me wonder if the default warmups for `Deno.bench` should be higher, assuming more people will be using this in CI environments in the future.
author: bartlomieju

title: lsp: support test discovery
body:
A current limitation of the support for the testing API, is that it will only discover tests in modules that are open in the editor, or a dependency of a module open in the editor.

We should support the following options:

- `deno.testing.discover` - a boolean value which defaults to `true`.  This would look from the workspace root (or workspace folder root) for any tests that match the internal logic of `deno test`.
- `deno.testing.discoverIgnore` - an array of strings which defaults to undefined.  This would be similar to the command line option `deno test --ignore=<ignore>`
author: kitsonk

title: lsp: allow lint enablement to be configurable per resource
body: We currently allow `deno.enable` to be configured "per resource" which allows us to support multi-root workspaces in VSCode for example.  We currently only allow a workspace setting for `deno.lint`, but we could make it apply per resource, like we do with enablement.

author: fabiancook

title: LSP should support organising imports
body:
# Feature request

It would be nice to be able to sort imports automatically.

It probably needs to be decided how they should be organised.

## Alphabetically

Simply sort everything alphabetically.

## By import type

First remote URLs, then imports from an import map, then absolute file paths, then relative paths. Imports of the same type are sorted alphabetically.

## By import type and domain

Sort by import type, but also parse URLs and sort by domain. That way, imports from `sub.domain.com` and `domain.com` could be next to each other.

body:
When a test fails, there's two things I want to know first:
1. **Which** test failed
2. **Why** the test failed

So I think it makes sense to put some extra emphasis on the error message.

from https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions/14156:

title: test: Better headers for the three `deno test` sections
body:
Right now `deno test` gets printed with three sections, but it's not immediately clear where one ends and the other starts. And the last two sections both have `failures:` as header.

I think it could be nice to make them a bit more distinguishable and name them `results`, `errors` and `failures` respectively.

Giving them a separate color would also help I think.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2737650/160925198-9b11111b-465b-4574-b4b4-ecf17d31bbd2.png)
author: jespertheend

title: test: `console.log` calls get printed in between the test results
body:
Consider the following test:
```js
Deno.test({
  name: "logs a message",
  fn() {
    console.log({bool: true, num: 5})
    console.log("hello");
  }
})
```

When you run it, it shows up with the console messages in between `test logs a message ...` and `ok (8ms)`:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2737650/161397622-ac214aa9-5bfe-42a9-acce-1b94a2f881b7.png)

And also, multiple `console.log` calls have two new lines in between them. If you were to do this with `deno run`, they are printed with only a single new line in between:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2737650/161397703-e5a4ed0f-0066-420c-8fbf-377b043a941b.png)

Not sure what the right behaviour is here, but some things I can think of:
- keep it almost like it is right now, except add a new line before the first `console.log()` call, and then only a single new line between every two calls, rather than the double new lines it currently prints.
- print the `console.log()` messages above/below the test result, and then print `ok (8ms)` retroactively in the right position once the test finishes. Not sure how difficult this would be, or if it even would be possible at all.
- Disable printing `console.log()` messages during tests altogether.

All in all I don't think this issue needs to have a very high priority imo, tests generally shouldn't run code that call `console.log` without mocking it first. But I do have a lot of tests that dynamically import blob urls, which looks kind of weird:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2737650/160927670-d88a76d2-c298-4094-a301-80a5ac80a09c.png)

title: lsp: codelens does not render when using backticks for test name
body:
**Describe the bug**

Using backtick strings prevents codelens from popping up:

![codelens_not_respecting_backticks mov](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1003261/161442115-30792479-bc55-458c-8e6d-bd19e84a7a9a.gif)

Using normal quotes works


**To Reproduce**

1. author a test
2. use backticks to render the fn name

4. use normal quotes

codelens should render with all valid strings

See GIF above

author: cdaringe

title: Large file byte ops position references don't work?
body:
A user of oak (@mdkq oakserver/oak#503) has reported an issue when uploading large file via oak. The user is getting a JavaScript stack trace like this:

```
[uncaught application error]: TypeError - out of range integral type conversion attempted

request: { url: "http://localhost/api/fragment", method: "POST", hasBody: true }
response: { status: 404, type: undefined, hasBody: false, writable: true }

    at async ftruncate (deno:runtime/js/30_fs.js:258:5)
    at async parts (https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.5.1/multipart.ts:253:15)
    at async FormDataReader.read (https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.5.1/multipart.ts:415:15)
    at async post (file:///Users/marcus/Desktop/rose/server/controllers/fragment.js:22:18)
    at async dispatch (https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.5.1/middleware.ts:41:7)
    at async dispatch (https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.5.1/middleware.ts:41:7)
    at async restrict (file:///Users/marcus/Desktop/rose/server/middleware/auth.js:20:25)
    at async dispatch (https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.5.1/middleware.ts:41:7)
    at async dispatch (https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.5.1/middleware.ts:41:7)
    at async dispatch (https://deno.land/x/oak@v10.5.1/middleware.ts:41:7)
```

The oak code is attempting a [`.truncate` on a `Deno.FsFile`](https://github.com/oakserver/oak/blob/c4fe8b35c0362b9b153e1cf8af4081c7a3905834/multipart.ts#L253) and the op side of the code is:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1f7dd5eda9b5e17ff102abb4dce1f19a4ce76688/runtime/js/30_fs.js#L258

The error appears to be from the Rust side though, when trying to coerce the len.  For JavaScript it shouldn't be a problem handing file lengths of around 28_000_000_000 bytes, but I noticed the op arguments are:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1f7dd5eda9b5e17ff102abb4dce1f19a4ce76688/runtime/ops/fs.rs#L1519-L1524

Which seems like a problem for file lengths, as a) it is signed in a situation where signed isn't import and b) would limit file operations to 2_147_483_647, quite a lot smaller than a lot of files you find in the wild these days.

Is there any issue with using an `i64`, as that is the closest to what is supported natively in JavaScript (obviously JSON only supports i32, but we aren't using JSON in our ops anymore)?
author: kitsonk

title: Out of memory issue with large amount of parallel `setTimeout`
body:
Hey guys. First of all: This issue is not critical by any means because the steps to produce the OOM are not really realistic in real world applications.

While I was playing around with the new `bench` functionality, I encountered an OOM when there are a lot (~5'000'000) of parallel `setTimeout`. I tested the same code in node to see if it behaves the same, which wasn't the case.

Is this behavior expected, or shouldn't it be the case? 

## Reproduction steps
<details>
  <summary>Full code</summary>

```js
const a = []
for (let i = 0; i < 5000; i++) {
  a.push(Math.floor(Math.random() * 10000))
}
const s = []
for (let i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
  a.forEach((e) => { setTimeout(() => s.push(e), e) })
}
```  
</details>

1. Create a js file `script.js`
2. Create an array with 5000 random, 4 digit numbers
```js
const a = []
for (let i = 0; i < 5000; i++) {
  a.push(Math.floor(Math.random() * 10000))
}
```
3. Initialize timeouts for every elements in array a thousand times with the number as time amount.
```js
const s = []
for (let i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
  a.forEach((e) => { setTimeout(() => s.push(e), e) })
}
```
4. Run the script
`deno run ./script.js` - *fails*
`node ./script.js` - *works*

<details>
  <summary>Exception</summary>

```
<--- Last few GCs --->

[11596:0x148200000]     7180 ms: Scavenge 1368.1 (1417.3) -> 1367.8 (1429.8) MB, 4.6 / 0.0 ms  (average mu = 0.390, current mu = 0.272) allocation failure; 
[11596:0x148200000]     7212 ms: Scavenge 1377.3 (1430.5) -> 1377.6 (1432.3) MB, 4.1 / 0.0 ms  (average mu = 0.390, current mu = 0.272) allocation failure; 
[11596:0x148200000]     7604 ms: Scavenge 1377.6 (1432.3) -> 1376.9 (1454.3) MB, 392.1 / 0.0 ms  (average mu = 0.390, current mu = 0.272) allocation failure; 


<--- JS stacktrace --->


#
# Fatal javascript OOM in Reached heap limit
#

[1]    11596 trace trap  deno run sort.js
```  
</details>

## Environment
**Computer:** Macbook Pro M1 Late 2020, 16 GB Ram
**deno:** 1.20.4 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
**V8:** 10.0.139.6
author: jwanner83

title: `--watch` can corrupt cache
body:
Recently, I've been using watch mode increasingly, i.e. `deno run --watch`. This corrupted something with the cache.

In particular, `deno cache` would always check the types, even if no files were changed in between. Terminating all other programs (no LSP running and so on), this happened:
```bash
$ for i in {1..5}; do deno cache src/main.ts; done
Check file:///home/steffen/git/dtapp/backend/src/main.ts
Check file:///home/steffen/git/dtapp/backend/src/main.ts
Check file:///home/steffen/git/dtapp/backend/src/main.ts
Check file:///home/steffen/git/dtapp/backend/src/main.ts
Check file:///home/steffen/git/dtapp/backend/src/main.ts
```

The same applied for `deno run`. I could run first `deno cache` and then `deno run` and both operations would type-check the project. I expect only the former one to do so.

The problem was local for the directory. When I `cp -r`ed the directory to `/tmp`, everything worked fine there. In other words, it was remembered that type-checking had been performed, so it was only done once. Subsequent `deno cache` commands completed instantly, exactly as expected.

The problem could be solved by `rm -rf ~/.cache`. (I'm reporting the bug anyway here so it can be fixed.)

I do not know how to reproduce this reliably. However, my suspicion is that the watch mode with its frequent recompilations broke something, perhaps due to partial file writes or other things caused by race conditions and starting and stopping a process repeatedly.

I'm using Deno 1.20.4.
author: KnorpelSenf

title: lsp - Caching dependencies requires restarting language server to reset diagnostics
body:
Recently I've noticed I have to reset the language server when caching dependencies. I investigated this a bit and seems to be an issue in the CLI. It seems to occur in deno all the way back to 1.17 (min version I can use vscode with).

To reproduce:

1. Delete your DENO_DIR
1. Open the tswhy repo.
1. Open the main.ts and deps.ts file.
1. Run the "Cache dependencies" command on the deps.ts file.
1. Type errors still occur in the main.ts file. Restart the language server to fix.

This doesn't seem to be a diagnostics invalidation issue... probably some state elsewhere that's not being cleared.

vscode: 1.66.0 deno: 1.20.4 extension: 3.12.0
author: dsherret

title: Closing an already closed file results in a `BadResource` error
body:
With a opened file:

```js
const file = await Deno.open(fileUrl);
```

Closing it twice in a row like this:

```js
file.close();
file.close();
```

Or this:

```js
Deno.close(file.rid);
Deno.close(file.rid);
```

Results in this error:

```
BadResource: Bad resource ID
```

This is problematic because as far as I can tell, there is no way to tell if a given file has already been closed before attempting to close it.

My situation is a try catch around code that's using an opened file, that closes the file in the catch. Trouble is there is a chance the file has already been closed at that point so it's not 100% safe to attempt to close it.

We need a way to tell if a file is closed (perhaps via a boolean property on the `Deno.FsFile` class, or a `Deno.isClosed(rid)` utility?), or it could just be made to be safe to close an already closed file; successive calls could be a no-op.

Here is a link to a question about this I asked in the Deno Discord `#help` channel:

https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/689420767620104201/960710988334760046
author: jaydenseric

title: Aborting a CompressionStream/DecompressionStream leaks a resource
author: Macil

title: Deno panics when trying to listen on a Unix Domain Socket where the path already exists, but is not writable by the user
body:
I'm not quite sure if this is something we need to catch in the runtime, but since I ran into that I'll just leave it here.

Both `Deno.listen()` and `Deno.listenDatagram()` allow binding to a Unix Domain Socket as an unstable feature. If the specified filepath already exists, they first remove the file, then initialize the socket at that location.
If the user specifies a filepath that is not writable by the user, the OS returns a OS Error 13 Permission Denied. If the path does not exist, so we only fail when trying to create the path, the error is properly propagated to allow Deno to exit regularly. If however the path does already exist, the Rust code panics because of the `unwrap()`:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/e33329b47ea1586bd1fbc686cae3048a080039ca/ext/net/ops_unix.rs#L147

This is the case for both `Deno.listen()` and `Deno.listenDatagram()`

Is there a reason why we just call `unwrap()` here?
author: egfx-notifications

title: Connect to AF_UNIX Socket in SOCK_DGRAM mode
body:
I have been trying a lot to find the correct way of doing this, but didn't come up with a solution. I hope you can either point me to the correct resources or verify my current understanding that this is currently not possibly with Deno.

I'd like to connect to an existing socket of type `AF_UNIX` and `SOCK_DGRAM` and let my application talk to it. Example use cases are logging to syslog via UDP messages to `/dev/log` or letting a Deno application running as systemd service communicate with systemd, for example to utilize the built-in watchdog mode.

`Deno.connect()` allows to connect to a `AF_UNIX` socket as unstable feature, but it does so in `SOCK_STREAM` mode. `Deno.listenDatagram()` is not an option because it tries to remove an already existing socket and register a new one at the location.

I suppose we would need something like `Deno.connectDatagram()` for this to work. I spent some time looking into the current implementations of `Deno.listen()`, `Deno.connect()` and `Deno.listenDatagram()` and from what I can see the underlying libraries should already support doing so - I may be horribly wrong, though, this is my first investigation into Deno source code.

Is there any reason why we do not yet have a `Deno.connectDatagram()` besides it not being a priority feature so far? If one were to implement that feature and make a PR, is `Deno.connectDatagram()` a proper way to do this or would you prefer to have this integrated into `Deno.connect`?
author: egfx-notifications
