author: dsherret

title: Deno.stdout.write does not write more than 16384 chars of a Uint8Array
body:
## latest deno version in use

output `deno --version`:
```
deno 1.20.4 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.0.139.6
typescript 4.6.2
```

---

## truncated output (THE BUG)

```typescript
// test-deno-stdout-write.ts
const test = "a".repeat(33000);
Deno.stdout.write(new TextEncoder().encode(test));
```
output `deno run test-deno-stdout-write.ts | wc -c`:
```
   16384
```

---

## TextEncoder.encode holds the full value

```typescript
// test-text-encoder.ts
const test = "a".repeat(33000);
console.log(new TextEncoder().encode(test));
```
output `deno run test-text-encoder.ts`:
```
Uint8Array(33000) [
  97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97,
  97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97,
  97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97,
  97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97,
  97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97,
  97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97,
  97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97, 97,
  ... 32900 more items
]
```

---

## console.log has no problem

```typescript
# test-console-log.ts
const test = "a".repeat(33000);
console.log(test)
```
output `deno run test-console-log.ts | wc -c`:
```
   33001
```

---

## node lts attempt

output `node --version`:
```
v16.14.0
```

---

## node process.stdout.write doesn't truncate

```javascript
// node-test.js
const test = "a".repeat(33000);
process.stdout.write(new TextEncoder().encode(test))
```
output `node node-test.js | wc -c`:
```
   33000
```

---

## Deno.writeAll(...) is fine (WORKAROUND)

```typescript
# test-deno-writeall.ts
const test = "a".repeat(33000);
await Deno.writeAll(Deno.stdout, new TextEncoder().encode(test))
```
output `deno run test-deno-writeall.ts | wc -c`:
```
   33000
```
author: dshick-nydig

title: Ability to inline specifiers when using `deno vendor`
body: I would like to vendor our dependencies in deno_automation, but it's a big task because I would have to run `deno vendor ...` then remove the import map and manually import the specifiers. This is a lot of work and it would be nice if `deno vendor` had an `--inline` option or something that instead of using an import map would update the specifiers (since import maps aren't composable).

body:

Caused by: 0: load_transformed failed 1: failed to analyze module 2: failed to resolve https://esm.sh/preact/jsx-runtime from file:///........................./test.jsx 3: Cannot resolve "https://esm.sh/preact/jsx-runtime" from "file:///........................./test.jsx". ```

title: [feature request] stream stdin to deno eval
body:
Streaming data into `deno eval` would be nice.

```
echo "console.log('hi')" | deno eval
```

The code argument works for small files but if you're trying to pass in a whole project you can run into limitations with argument size limits. Streaming this data into `deno eval` could solve that.

title: --watch fails in Docker container running on ARM Mac
body:
When I run Deno in a Docker container on an Apple M1 Mac and add the `--watch` flag, I get a “Function not implemented” error. Without the `--watch` flag, everything works fine.

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Install [Docker](https://www.docker.com/get-started/) on an ARM Mac
2. In Terminal.app, execute command `docker run -it --rm denoland/deno:1.20.5 run --watch https://deno.land/std@0.134.0/examples/welcome.ts` (For the purpose of this bug report I chose a URL, but the issue is the same for watching local files.)

**Expected output:**
```
Welcome to Deno!
```

**Actual output:**
```
Watcher Process started.
error: Function not implemented (os error 38)
```

The same error is returned when executing
```
docker run -it --rm denoland/deno:1.20.5 fmt --watch
docker run -it --rm denoland/deno:1.20.5 lint --watch
```

Environment:
- M1 Mac, macOS 12.3.1 (21E258)
- Docker version 20.10.14, build a224086
- deno 1.20.5 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu), v8 10.0.139.6, typescript 4.6.2
author: ChristianSiegert

title: lsp: support per workspace folder deno.jsonc
body:
There are several scenarios where being able to have one workspace in the editor, but a different deno.jsonc apply is becoming increasingly necessary. When combining front-end and back end code as part of the same project for example.

The challenge is a couple fold, mostly with resolving config files on a workspace folder perspective and managing those config files depending on what specifier is being queried. In addition the TS language server has the plumbing for type checking on a "project" basis, but our communication with it does not currently account for that, which means we would need to re-plumb that.

It us a non-trivial piece of work but I have ideas on an approach.

Ref: https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/issues/787
author: kitsonk

title: deno compile ignores import_map.json specified in deno.json
body:
Hey! I have a smallish project that I'm working on, and when I compile the command to make a binary, it seems to ignore the `import_map.json`. I have an `"importMap"` key in my `deno.json`, but it doesn't pick it up. When I use `--import-map ./import_map.json` it works, but it then gives me a warning that the `"importMap"` key is being ignored. It also works completely fine when I run with `deno run`, and I don't have to use the `--import-map` option.

Here are the commands I used:

Compile: `deno compile --allow-env --allow-net --allow-read ./src/main.ts`
The compilation succeeds, but when I try to run the binary with `./main` I get this result: `error: Relative import path "dotenv/mod.ts" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ from "file:///home/redxtech/Code/project/src/main.ts"`

Compile with args: `deno compile --allow-env --allow-net --allow-read --import-map ./import_map.json ./src/main.ts`
Warning: `Warning the configuration file "file:///home/redxtech/Code/project/deno.json" contains an entry for "importMap" that is being ignored.`

Run: `deno run --allow-env --allow-net --allow-read ./src/main.ts`
Result: works as expected.

Output of `deno --version`:
```
deno 1.20.5 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.0.139.6
typescript 4.6.2
```

Here's my `./deno.json` (`lint` & `fmt` fields removed):
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "allowJs": false,
    "lib": ["deno.window", "es2021"],
    "noUnusedLocals": true,
    "noUnusedParameters": true,
    "strict": true
  },
  "importMap": "./import_map.json"
}
```
and my `./import_map.json`:
```json
{
   "imports": {
      "dotenv/": "https://deno.land/x/dotenv@v1.0.1/",
      "kall/": "https://denopkg.com/olaven/kall@v1.0.8/",
      "std/": "https://deno.land/std@0.133.0/"
   }
}

```

Here's the relevant part of `./src/main.ts` file where the imports are used:
```ts
import { config as dotEnvConfig } from 'dotenv/mod.ts';
```

If you need any more info I would be happy to provide it.
author: redxtech

title: All manual links in help text should include version number
body:
For example, the `--config` help text includes the link https://deno.land/manual/getting_started/configuration_file but ought to specify the current version: https://deno.land/manual@v1.20.5/getting_started/configuration_file

hint use `crate::version::deno()`

Examples of where it should be updated:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/a4eee007ef28f918db9165c52a19bc30bd65bad3/cli/flags.rs#L457

author: ghost

title: tsc --generateTrace output
body:
I have written a deno library that appears to have a decently slow type compilation stage. I would like to be able to use microsoft's analysis tool https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-analyze-trace to determine where my compilation bottlenecks occur.

Currently, running `tsc --generateTrace .` at the root of my project fails with exit code `1`. The first reason it fails, is that it requires a tsconfig.json file to be present. Thus far, I havent had a need to write one since deno's defaults have been good enough this far. Once a tsconfig.json file is created though, there is a problem I do not believe I can solve userland.
```
lib/mod.ts:2:21 - error TS2691: An import path cannot end with a '.ts' extension. Consider importing 'https://deno.land/std@0.91.0/fs/mod' instead.
```

not to mention some other errors that I do not know how to solve:
```
lib/mod.ts:67:31 - error TS2304: Cannot find name 'Deno'.
```


I am proposing that deno add a new flag `--trace-types=<trace.json>` under `deno run`, `deno test` that maps the `tsc --generateTrace` output. Alternatively, another top level command like `deno analyze` or `deno trace` could work, but I dont know if this deserves its own top level command.

title: `deno upgrade` overwrites the old executable path, regardless of filetype (e.g. symlink)
body:
First: thank you to all `deno` maintainers for the excellent runtime! What a fantastic project, delightful to work with, appreciate everything you all do.

I had a relatively custom setup on my machine, due to some specific permissions configuration that my corporate overlords set up on my work laptop. Essentially the `deno` available on my `$PATH` was a symlink that pointed to the `deno` I installed using the `curl install script | bash` method described on deno.land, with the addition of using the [`DENO_INSTALL` environment variable](https://deno.land/x/install/install.sh#L28) to customize the location of where the binary is placed.

After installing `deno` using the above method, if I, at a later date when a new `deno` version is released, run `deno upgrade`, it looks like the [upgrade will overwrite the location of the existing `deno` entry available on the `$PATH`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1c37ac33526dc45ad0b3f83ca8294dbb55548096/cli/tools/upgrade.rs#L129-L137) - regardless of if that `deno` is a symlink or not.

It would be swell if the `deno upgrade` command followed symlinks in order to determine what the "old path" is for the `deno` executable. I am a total Rust noob so not sure where to start, but if someone more knowledgeable than I can offer tips / guidance, I can try my hand at writing up a pull request for this.

Cheers!

author: Industrial

title: Add support for 'Trust on First Use' for TLS certificates
body: Currently, connecting to hosts with certificates that are not signed (but otherwise valid) will throw an error, and require the `--unsafely-treat-insecure-origin-as-secure`. I think there should be an option, in `Deno.connectTls()` function, called `tofu` or `trustOnFirstUse`. It will be a boolean that is by default `false`. If `true`, the request will be made, and the certificate will still be checked, except for the signed by CA requirement. It should then be cached in some sort of long-term storage, where any subsequent connections to this domain will require that specific certificate that was received on first connection. If there is a mismatch, an error is thrown.

author: kt3k

title: lock: Allow explicit file-by-file opt out of `--cached-only`
body:
Using the `--cached-only` option with a lock file can be argued to be the preferable approach for any high-security enterprise application project. It offers strictly better protection against aberrations in dependency locking. However, using a pure strict approach may still be too inflexible, especially for CI builds.

I suggest that lock files could be optionally edited to replace a given file's hash with `"*"` to indicate that the given file need not be checked for integrity. The benefits of this approach would be:
1. Project's internal file dependencies could be explicitly opted out of integrity checking, allowing said internal dependencies to be fetched from eg. latest Jenkins build output or head of Git branch.
2. Compared to using a lock file without `--cached-only` with removing said internal dependencies from the lock file, an accidental extra removal of a line from the lock file would not suddenly allow an external dependency to be imported without integrity checking.

This is not a huge feature, to be fair but it is also likely quite easy to implement and should be pretty free of upkeep as long as the current lock file format is intended to stay.

body:
**Description**
When running `deno test --coverage` some files are not included in the resulting JSON files.
As a developer I also want to see completely uncovered files in my codecov report.

**Reproduction**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/dhardtke/prandium.git
cd prandium
deno test --coverage=./cov --unstable --allow-all
grep -r "index.controller.ts" cov
```

`index.controller.ts` is not tested (yet) and does not appear in the JSON files and the resulting lcov report.

**Possible Workaround**
A workaround might be to explicitly provide the list of all files to `deno test`, regardless of whether they include tests or not.

**Side Note**
What's odd is that I had a couple of executions of `deno test --coverage` where it did generate JSON files for the `index.controller.ts` but I am no longer able to reproduce this behavior.

title: Interest in Adding Support for "navigator.sendBeacon"?
body:
I was following up on trying to use OpenTelemetry-JS in Deno (after my earlier efforts [here](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-js/issues/2293#issuecomment-1030750431)) this time trying to export trace data out of Deno and to an OTEL collector.

The main issue I ran into this time was that the [browser version of the exporter](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-js/blob/main/experimental/packages/exporter-trace-otlp-http/src/platform/browser/OTLPExporterBrowserBase.ts#L80) relies on either navigator.sendBeacon or XmlHttpRequest to do the exporting, neither of which are currently available in Deno.

I was able to put together this [small userland polyfill](https://deno.land/x/sendbeacon_polyfill@0.0.1) of navigator.sendBeacon based on [this comment from MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/fetch#keepalive) about the "keepalive" option for fetch, which seemed to do the trick as far as my OTEL-JS experiment goes. However I'm not sure if its description there as a "drop-in replacement for navigator.sendBeacon" also applies in Deno.

I remember seeing elsewhere XmlHttpRequest won't be supported in core, but I was curious if the same would apply to navigator.sendBeacon? I'm not quite sure what the direction is around navigator APIs, as I see there seem to only be 2 supported at the moment (?) so I figured it couldn't hurt to ask.
author: Grunet

title: Should "deno test" capture user output by default?
body:
With https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/244926e83c7d3cae3c3ae3fc14e996e3066da43e `deno test` is able to discern that output comes the user code and thus potentially capture it.

In https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14262#issuecomment-1100753401 @lucacasonato suggests that we should capture the output by default (similar to Rust) and disable this capturing if `--nocapture` flag is present. When @dsherret and I worked on the above commit, we did consider it, but both of us prefer the output is not captured by default, so we just left it as is.

This warrants some discussion and getting feedback from community.

Then, add `import { createRequire } from "https://deno.land/std@0.133.0/node/module.ts";`

Doing one of the following makes a child worker stall:

2. `export const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);`

author: pouyakary

title: Idle program detection doesn't work if a web worker is involved
body:
If you have a program like:
```ts
// temp2.ts
await new Promise(() => {});
```
You get from `deno run temp2.ts`:
```
error: Module evaluation is still pending but there are no pending ops or dynamic imports. This situation is often caused by unresolved promises.
```
But when run in a worker:
```ts
// temp.ts
new Worker(new URL("temp2.ts", import.meta.url).href, { type: "module" });
```
`deno run temp.ts` hangs forever.

Of course we want web workers to persist for their event loops since they can get messages from their parents, but if _all_ active runtimes are idle we should be able to exit with a similar message.

```
running 0 tests from ./testing/chai_example.ts

running 0 tests from ./testing/mock_examples/internals_injection.ts
```

title: [unstable] DatagramConn "missing field `transport`"
body:
[originally posted in Deno Discord server]

I'm studying and fixing some code where I'm having a huge issue with the DatagramConn API. [Source](https://github.com/harmonyland/harmony_voice/blob/main/src/udp.ts)

A `DatagramConn` is initialized through `listenDatagram().` [Source](https://github.com/harmonyland/harmony_voice/blob/c088e694ac3342c9aaa25b07d6040b14428295a1/src/udp.ts#L44)
```ts
this.socket = Deno.listenDatagram({
  hostname: "0.0.0.0",
  port: 0,
  transport: "udp",
});
```

Later, `socket.send()` is called, with all the right arguments again. [Source](https://github.com/harmonyland/harmony_voice/blob/c088e694ac3342c9aaa25b07d6040b14428295a1/src/udp.ts#L201)
```ts
return this.socket!.send(
  buffer,
  this.conn.ws?.addr!,
);
```

This should all work, I've checked it by the [documentation](https://doc.deno.land/deno/unstable/~/Deno.DatagramConn) and it looks fine. However, it spits this out:

```
TypeError: Error parsing args at position 1: missing field `transport`
    at Object.opAsync (deno:core/01_core.js:152:35)
    at opSend (deno:ext/net/01_net.js:61:17)
    at Datagram.send (deno:ext/net/01_net.js:321:14)
    at VoiceUDP.sendVoice (file:///home/skybl/code/js/harmony_voice/src/udp.ts:201:25)
    at frame1 (file:///home/skybl/code/js/harmony_voice/src/player.ts:79:38)
```

I'm pretty confused as to why this is happening and I have no idea how to work around this. 

It is also worth knowing that this library was most likely only tested on Windows, judging by the DOS line-ending on all the files. I'm using Linux. Might this be a compatibility or platform support issue?

title: [FR] signal to catch exit via deno run --watch
body:
During development in a docker container network, the deno server is started with deno run --watch. If project files are saved when deno is servicing a request, db transaction etc. there is no opportunity to clean up and if a source file is changed the process is killed instantly and restarted.

I have tried the following:

```js
globalThis.addEventListener("unload", () => { console.log('restart event') })
```
and also
```js
Deno.addSignalListener("SIGUSR1", () => {   // tried all allowable signals
  console.log("received end signal")
})
```
But I can never capture deno going down. This is quite problematic during development/testing.

### Suggested solution

An early suggestions for watch implementation [mentioned here](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/2401#issue-447589621) suggested the possibility to intercept SIGUSR1; that would seem a good solution, with the signal configurable. During watch, allow a configurable grace period (default 2s) before deno restarts, along with a console message to indicate 'Waiting for clean shutdown...'

title: FFI performance improvements (JIT compiler)
body:
- [x] Value (de)serialization is slow. It has to go through JS -> `serde_json::Value` -> Rust, a faster path would be to convert to/fro a `serde_v8::Value` instead. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14663
- [x] Each call has to go through the (already very thin) op layer. A possible solution is to make v8 callbacks during registration. This will get rid of the overhead of OpState. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14945
- [x] JIT compiler for FFI calls. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15006
author: littledivy

title: Support reverse DNS API
body:
Feature request to support address to name translation ("reverse DNS"), a.k.a. wrapper around https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getnameinfo.3.html (e.g. using https://docs.rs/dns-lookup/latest/dns_lookup/#getnameinfo) or equivalent, so that users of Deno can translate an IP and port into a hostname and service.

E.g. 

```ts
interface NameInfoRecord {
  hostname: string;
  service: string;
}

function Deno.resolveNameInfo(ipAddr: string, port: number): Promise<NameInfoRecord>;
```

_API shape and names just as example - suspect we can devise better alternatives! Answers on a postcard 🙃_

```ts
const { hostname, service } = await Deno.resolveNameInfo('127.0.0.1', 22);

console.log(hostname, service); // "localhost" "ssh"
```

---

This would provide a sibling to the existing [`Deno.resolveDns()`](https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/Deno.resolveDns) API which offers DNS resolution currently.

This would increase parity with existing Node APIs:

- https://nodejs.org/api/dns.html#dnslookupserviceaddress-port-callback
- https://nodejs.org/api/dns.html#dnspromiseslookupserviceaddress-port

And is required to support the `dns` and `dns/promises` modules in the Deno std library compat layer.

---

**Alternatives considered**

1) PTR + service lookup

Could use `PTR` records with existing `Deno.resolveDns()` but more limited in capability (don’t get the port to service mapping without additional work) and requires the user or any wrapper to handle IPv4 vs IPv6 address construction (incl. hex for IPv6) - overheads / features that could be provided by core if use `getnameinfo`.

E.g.

```ts
const ipv4Results = await Deno.resolveDns("4.4.8.8.in-addr.arpa", "PTR");

console.log(ipv4Results); // [ "dns.google." ]
```

To then get the service information could write a wrapper in TS to either interrogate the services db direct (e.g. parse /etc/services), or spawn execution of `getservbyport`.

2) Overload `Deno.resolveDns()`

Introduce some faux record type, or just an overloaded interface where ip + port args internally mapped to getnameinfo like behaviour.

Complicates an otherwise stable and clear interface so leaning away from this kind of idea.

author: Sembiance

title: lsp: rework workspace folders
body:
Not all editors / LSP clients support per-project configuration, not all even support *any* configuration. So it becomes non-trivial to make it work with some editors.

For example I've had to figure out a magic incantation to add to [Helix's languages.toml](https://github.com/helix-editor/helix/blob/166818359030cd1649327160c9020d943a8de244/languages.toml):

```toml
config = { deno = { enable = true, lint = true } }
```

by looking at debug messages to see what the server doesn't like.

_Originally posted by @unrelentingtech in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13228#issuecomment-1114361060_

The initial implementation of workspace folders was naïve on my part and in doing the `enableFolders` feature I realised a much cleaner and better way to do it.  We need to rework this feature and respect the client capability around workspace folders, so clients don't have to implement such things.

author: oscarotero

title: expose rusqlite natively under Deno global
body:
So far I have been watching node compatibility features, wasm features, and ffi features all to serve one purpose for myself, to expose a a reliable, fast sqlite interface in deno. There are several good sqlite implementations that exist in deno userland thus far:
- https://deno.land/x/sqlite (a wasm compilation)
- https://deno.land/x/sqlite3 (an ffi binding)
- https://github.com/denodrivers/async-sqlite3 (ffi bindings using rusqlite)


These projects are usable, but they all have their own pitfalls. The wasm libraries are portable, but suffer a reduction in speed, and their memory usage is capped at 4GB. The FFI libraries are generally faster, but still cannot match native bindings, and have an added difficulty of requiring an external dependency. Developers of FFI libraries also have the headache of dealing with end users using different versions of sqlite underneath their js interface.

Here is a small benchmark report given by the `async-sqlite3` library:
```deno_sqlite3
  insert 10_000 rows in bench.db: 1114.5801ms
  query 10_000 rows in bench.db: 30.3004ms

x/sqlite3
  insert 10_000 rows in bench.db: 1306.1578ms
  query 10_000 rows in bench.db: 163.2021ms

node-sqlite3
  insert 10_000 rows in bench.db: 1165.0645ms
  query 10_000 rows in bench.db: 8.1728ms

better-sqlite3
  insert 10_000 rows in bench.db: 592.1117ms
  query 10_000 rows in bench.db: 11.3483ms

x/sqlite
  insert 10_000 rows in bench.db: 28672.3261ms
  query 10_000 rows in bench.db: 16.8747ms
```


Wherever I see a javascript interface to sqlite, better-sqlite3 always seems to win out. It uses NAN (Native Abstractions for Node.js). Deno already includes a rusqlite implementation inside it for things like `localStorage`, so exposing the library to users would not require pulling more dependencies into deno, just exposing more of the underlying systems. I feel (but dont know for certain) that if we had native rust v8 bindings to a library inside deno core, we could achieve some very impressive speeds, and avoid some of the traps that come with wasm compilations.

The interface could be very simple, just exposing prepared statements, database open & close and query execution. That alone would allow deno developers to write their own database abstractions with a solid foundation. The existing sqlite library authors do not need to go away if this is implemented,. Of course, an STD implementation of a sqlite database would be fantastic, but I think starting with the building blocks here makes the most sense.


## drawbacks to deno exposing rusqlite

So far as I can tell, here are the arguments against this:
1. This is another interface to maintain, and `localStorage` has its own special considerations, so making rusqlite exposed generically would be difficult
2. being able to pick and choose your version of sqlite is actually a feature, not a bug. Tying sqlite version to the deno version limits the flexibility of sqlite

I think these are valid points, but they certainly wouldnt be true for me, but Ill let others speak to their own concerns.

title: Keep numbers in scientific notation format after bundling
body:
When bundling a JS module Deno will format number written in scientific forms in their full form.

```
// for example:
Algebra(2, 0, 1, ()=>!(1e0 + 1e1 + 1e2))
// results in:
Algebra(2, 0, 1, ()=>!(1 + 10 + 100))
```
Now that is perfectly fine was it not that the Algebra[1] function here is a JS to JS transpiler that interpret numbers in scientific form, not as scalar values, but as a tensor products where `1e0` is a vectors, `1e12` a bivectors and `1e012` a trivectors.

Admittedly this is sort of a neat thing todo and allows one to write JS for scientific computation and 3D development[2] that looks very close to the math on paper as is quick to experiment with.

My proposal would be to keep numbers in their original form.

[1] https://github.com/enkimute/ganja.js
[2] https://enkimute.github.io/ganja.js/examples/coffeeshop.html#pga3d_points_and_lines

author: idranme

title: steps_output_within is flaky
body:
The test output capturing at the moment is flaky by design unfortunately. Essentially, the JS code is logging to stdout and stderr, which is piped via pipes to two threads that do blocking reads on the output pipes of stdout and stderr. This test output data is then sent across a channel to the test reporter.

What can occurr (especially on the CI), is the data is written to a stdout or stderror pipe, but the threads reading do not read and send the output message in time before the other thread sends the "test complete" notification message.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/runs/6277042335?check_suite_focus=true

To fix this, I believe we'll need a way to unblock the stdout and stderr reader threads and have them to signal back that they don't have any more data to read. I haven't figured out a way to wake up these reader threads though... initially I thought sending zero bytes over would work, but it seems like on Windows doing `WriteFile` on the anonymous pipe write handle with zero bytes does not wake up the read end even when flushing (I'm using all low level win api for testing purposes).

title: Bench: setup and cleanup
body:
If you're writing benchmarks which use some kind of resource (e.g. benching writes to a Sqlite database), there's no way to cleanup that resource after all benchmarks have run.

- The naive approach of simply doing what you need to cleanup after calling `Deno.bench` doesn't work.
- Listening for `window.unload` doesn't accommodate anything async.

title: core: Modules should be manually instantiated
body:
Working on https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14485 we hit a snag that modules are not
instantiated as soon as possible which prevents us from making progress on the issue.

Currently we only instantiate "top level" module and rely on V8 to instantiate all children
modules, instead the code should be refactored to introduce "link" phase, where child modules are instantiated
manually as soon as their child modules are loaded.

## Repro

I'm using [`abc`](https://github.com/zhmushan/abc), trying to get a fallback working for 404 pages. Here is the code in question:

```ts
const showNotFound: HandlerFunc = async() => {
  return await handlebars.render("index", {
    content: await handlebars.render("page/not-found", {}),
    description: "A blog about the DNS, Handshake, and the internet at large.",
    head: `<link rel="stylesheet" href="/not-found.css"/>`
  });
};

app.pre((next: HandlerFunc) => (context: Context) => {
  try {
    console.log("hey");
    console.log(context);
    return next(context);
  } catch(error) {
    console.error(">>> error");
    console.error(error);
  } finally {
    return showNotFound(context);
  }
});
```

I saw the code in [this issue](https://github.com/zhmushan/abc/issues/134#issuecomment-886880849) and obviously, it doesn't work (at least, not in the way I'm using it).
author: NetOpWibby

title: Map u64s and i64s from the FFI to bigints instead of numbers
body:
First I just wanna say that using the FFI overall has been awesome, it's really really great to have this for Deno and I hope it stays around!

Right now FFI functions that return u64s or i64s get coerced to javascript numbers, losing precision for values greater than `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` or less than `Number.MIN_SAFE_INTEGER`.

In the other direction, it's not possible to pass a number

- greater than `2 ^ 32 - 1` (!!) as a `u64` parameter
- greater than `2 ^ 32 - 1` or less than `-2 ^ 31` (!!) as a `i64` parameter

I'm testing this with just

```c
// example.c
#include <stdint.h>

int64_t max_i64() { return 9223372036854775807; }

uint64_t max_u64() { return 18446744073709551615U; }

uint64_t identity_u64(uint64_t i) { return i; }

int64_t identity_i64(int64_t i) { return i; }
```

```
$ gcc -dynamiclib -o example.dylib example.c
```

```ts
const example = Deno.dlopen("example.dylib", {
	max_i64: { parameters: [], result: "i64" },
	max_u64: { parameters: [], result: "u64" },
	identity_u64: { parameters: ["u64"], result: "u64" },
	identity_i64: { parameters: ["i64"], result: "i64" },
})

console.log(example.symbols.max_i64()) // 9223372036854776000
console.log(example.symbols.max_u64()) // 18446744073709552000
console.log(example.symbols.identity_u64(4294967295)) // 4294967295
console.log(example.symbols.identity_u64(4294967296)) // Uncaught TypeError: Expected FFI argument to be an unsigned integer, but got Number(4294967296.0)
console.log(example.symbols.identity_i64(-2147483648)) // -2147483648
console.log(example.symbols.identity_i64(-2147483649)) // Uncaught TypeError: Expected FFI argument to be a signed integer, but got Number(-2147483649.0)
```

There are a few ways to improve the situation here:

- accept entire range of integers representable as javascript numbers, rounding when necessary
- return javascript numbers when possible without rounding, return bigints for values outside the safe integer range, accept bigints in addition to numbers
- require/always use bigints for all `u64` and `i64` values

They all have drawbacks but in my opinion the last option (always use bigints) is the cleanest and most consistent. Having an FFI that silently rounds is just not that useful.
author: joeltg

title: TypeError when importing from URLs in the repl
body:
When importing from URLs in the repl, the following error is thrown:
```Uncaught TypeError: Relative import path "<path>" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ from "<location>"```

Example:
```js
> import {readerFromStreamReader, readAll} from "https://deno.land/std/streams/conversion.ts";
Uncaught TypeError: Relative import path "https://deno.land/std/streams/conversion.ts" not prefixed with / or ./
or ../ from "file:///home/qmelz/$deno$repl.ts"
```
Same thing also happens with dynamic imports.

title: [tests] add option to disable internal logs of "running x tests from ..."
body:
Hello 👋 

If possible, I would like to request an option to disable the logs of `deno test` that show how many tests are running from each file

**Problem:**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23035000/167307323-0bd28b7a-5ccb-49c8-92f2-8ac52fc2003e.png)

**Use Case:**

I have hundreds of unit tests that need to run and each can take about a second. That adds up a lot. I was looking for ways to run them in parallel to make it quicker and i found the best way is to have separate files and run with --jobs flag.

_A test on a smaller scale with only about 14 tests:_

1 file with --jobs = 9s total
1 file without --jobs = 9s total

Separate files with --jobs = 6s total **WINNER**
Separate files without --jobs = 12s total

However, now that I have so many flags, it also ends up spamming the "running x tests from y file"

Thank You
author: Skillz4Killz

title: `deno test --jobs` flag should require equals when passing a value
body: The `--jobs` flag optionally doesn't take a number value to default to the number of available cpus. So it should require an equal sign. Currently `deno test --jobs tests/` doesn't work because `tests/` is parsed as the number of threads. Unfortunately this is a breaking change, so this was a bad oversight.

title: `Deno.consoleSize(Deno.stdout.rid)` doesn't work with `deno test --unstable`
body:
### Version

Deno 1.21.2

### Reproduction

Run the below code with `deno run --unstable main.js` and it prints with an object with `column` and `row`
```js
// main.js
console.log(Deno.consoleSize(Deno.stdout.rid))
```

Run the below code with `deno test --unstable test.js` and it throws an error `Error: Inappropriate ioctl for device (os error 25)`
```js
// test.js
Deno.test('test', () => {
  console.log(Deno.consoleSize(Deno.stdout.rid))
})
```

I am on Intel based macOS Monterey 12.3.1.

title: regression: `confirm` sometimes doesn't show prompt before receiving input on Windows
body: In #14435, everything was changed to use the stdio pipes found in the resources table. This seems to have caused a regression where the output of `op_print` doesn't flush the data on Windows even though there's an explicit flush call.

title: Generated TypeScript definitions not compatible with Deno
body:
I generate TypeScript definitions using `tsc --emitDeclarationOnly` and reference using triple forward slash.

_index.js_:

```js
/// <reference types="./index.d.ts" />
export * from './dist/es/index.js';
```
_index.d.ts_:

```js
import { __VERSION__ as VERSION } from "./version";
export { VERSION };
```

I import with:

```js
import { VERSION } from './index.js';
```

When I run this code with:

```
deno run --reload index.ts
````

I get the following error:

```
error: TS2305 [ERROR]: Module '"deno:///missing_dependency.d.ts"' has no exported member '__VERSION__'.
import { __VERSION__ as VERSION } from "./version";
```

If I manually edit the generated `index.d.ts` file and give the import an extension, denos runs without erroring.

```js
import { __VERSION__ as VERSION } from "./version.d.ts";
export { VERSION };
```

I can't find an option in `tsc` to generate definitions with extensions. Is this a bug in deno or should I raise the concern with TypeScript? I would expect Deno to be able to resolve sibling `d.ts` files without requiring a known extension.
author: tbranyen

title: `deno test` doesn't use the specified `compilerOptions.lib`
body:
Tiny example repo:

https://github.com/RobertAKARobin/deno-test-err

It consists of:

```ts
// index.ts
export const div = document.createElement('div');
```

```ts
// index.test.ts
import { div } from './index.ts';
console.log(div);
```

```ts
// deno.jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "lib": [
      "deno.ns",
      "dom"
    ]
  }
}
```

# Steps to reproduce

1. Run `deno bundle index.ts`. Observe it compiles and completes successfully.
2. Run `deno test index.test.ts`

# Expected result

`deno test` completes successfully, just like `deno bundle`.

# Actual result

`deno test` fails with a fatal error:

```
test result: ok. 0 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (13ms)

error: Uncaught ReferenceError: document is not defined
export const div = document.createElement('div');
                   ^
    at file:///Users/robertthomas/Programming/web/deno-test-err/index.ts:1:20
```

`deno test` doesn't "know" about `document`, which indicates it is not respecting that the configuration file's `compilerOptions.lib` includes `"dom"`.

If I add the `--compat` option...:

```
$ deno test --allow-all --config deno.jsonc --compat  --unstable --no-check index.test.ts 
```

...then the error changes:

```
test result: ok. 0 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (214ms)

error: Uncaught (in promise) SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement outside a module
  const [f, err] = core.evalContext(wrapper, filename);
                        ^
    at wrapSafe (https://deno.land/std@0.138.0/node/module.ts:1315:25)
    at Module._compile (https://deno.land/std@0.138.0/node/module.ts:251:29)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (https://deno.land/std@0.138.0/node/module.ts:1334:10)
    at Module.load (https://deno.land/std@0.138.0/node/module.ts:239:34)
    at Function._load (https://deno.land/std@0.138.0/node/module.ts:558:14)
    at loadCjsModule ([deno:cli/compat/mod.rs:120:30]:3:22)
```

How can I write unit tests that include the DOM API?

title: Option to resolve import_map without actually bundling
body:
I'm currently working on making [fido2-lib](https://github.com/webauthn-open-source/fido2-lib) work in Deno. I'm doing this by making sure the original "node code" only uses node AND deno compatible dependencies, then adding each dependency to an import map. 

It works great, but the bundle is huge (1.8MB), and there is really no meaning in including all dependencies in the bundle. The only thing i need is to translate module name to url, and optionally bundle everything to a single file.

In this (and many other cases) just resolvning the imports to the actual urls would be enough, something like

```deno bundle --import-map=import_map.json --resolve-only```

Which would translate

```js
import { A, B } from "modulename";
```
to

```js
import { A, B } from "https://provider/modulename";
```

author: symful

title: Leading slashes in paths on Windows
body:
Running the following will log the strings described in the comments: 
```ts
console.log(
  import.meta.url, // "file:///D:/test/testing.ts"
  new URL(import.meta.url).pathname, // "/D:/test/testing.ts"
);
```
This is spec-compliant behaviour and not a bug. 

However, this causes some cross-platform issues with code like: 
```ts
console.log(await Deno.readTextFile(new URL(import.meta.url).pathname)); // path has the leading slash
```
(reading the file itself as text). Obviously, this is a contrived example, but I would expect it to work. And [it _does_ work on Mac](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/726004202878533682/975002689584902164/Screenshot_2022-05-14_at_13.52.22.png). A more reasonable example might look like this: 
```ts
import { dirname, join } from "https://deno.land/std@0.139.0/path/win32.ts";

const path = join(dirname(new URL(import.meta.url).pathname), "units.txt"); // path has the leading slash
console.log(await Deno.readTextFile(path));
```
I would expect this to work on all platforms, but on Windows, both of the above examples result in `error: Uncaught (in promise) NotFound: The system cannot find the file specified. (os error 2)`.

---

Since leaving the leading slash in is spec-compliant behaviour for URL.pathname, I propose that Deno ought to ignore the leading slash in file paths to ensure that code is portable.

author: code-ape

title: Ability to ignore IP-address certificate errors
body:
## Problem

[rustls 0.20.5](https://crates.io/crates/rustls/0.20.5) has been recently released and have landed [this PR](https://github.com/rustls/rustls/pull/1032).

It feels that `--unsafely-ignore-certificate-errors` option could just force Deno to ignore verification of IP-addresses as stated in rustls PR. This means that [this issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/7660) could be handled at least by unsafe CLI option.

However, bumping rustls is not a complete solution, since after doing that I got `Unknown ServerName variant` error. This is most likely because rustls added `IpAddress` to [ServerName](https://docs.rs/rustls/0.20.5/rustls/enum.ServerName.html) enum while [this part](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c496639d5dc190b107222bc30462d67ddb86c223/ext/tls/lib.rs#L53) of Deno code is not ready for that.

## Potential Solution

Bump rustls to v0.20.5, then update `ext/tls/lib.rs` to recognise IP-addresses.

title: window prototype chain should be frozen
body: The global-immutable-prototype.any WPT currently fails because Deno does not freeze the window prototype chain. This makes it difficult to [add webidl brand checking to `EventTarget`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14637) because `window` should be an `EventTarget`. This also makes it more difficult to [harden certain APIs against tampering](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14648).

title: Migrate usage on V8's globalThis.console
body:
Since `globalThis.console` is not an ECMAScript defined builtin, V8's globally installed `console` implementation is been moved to the context's extra binding object: https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:v8/src/init/bootstrapper.cc;l=4429?q=bootstrapper.cc

Deno is depending on this too: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/runtime/js/99_main.js#L542. We need to migrate to that one before the globally installed console object is removed in V8.

title: `Deno.permissions.request()` prompts even if `--no-prompt` set
body:
Calling `Deno.permissions.request()` will prompt the user for the permission if not already granted even when `--no-prompt` or `NO_PROMPT` is set, which seems wrong.  In those cases, it should just deny un-granted permissions.

Ref: denoland/deno_emit#3 where deno_cache is using `Deno.permissions.request()` to ensure it has sufficient permissions to determine the `DENO_DIR`.

title: Provide debug logging with the deno CLI
body:
[additional context](https://twitter.com/biwanczuk/status/1527636222377590785)

When debugging Deno programs it would be helpful to have greater visibility into what is happening under the covers. 
For our use-case the programs we run are typically invoked by code in [this repo](https://github.com/slackapi/deno-slack-runtime).
It would be helpful to have log output for the different stages of the execution along with timestamps similar to Terraform's [debugging](https://www.terraform.io/internals/debugging) capability.  

For example

```console
$ deno_log=DEBUG deno run <some program>
2022/05/20 15:04:16 [INFO] Deno version: 1.22.0
2022/05/20 15:04:16 [INFO] v8 version: 10.0.139.6
2022/05/20 15:04:16 [DEBUG] executing main module 
2022/05/20 15:04:16 [DEBUG] worker loading event
2022/05/20 15:04:16 [DEBUG] running worker event loop
2022/05/20 15:04:18 [DEBUG] event loop existed
```

author: bartlomieju

title: deno lint complains variable never reassigned when declared and later assigned
body:
The linter appears to uselessly flag a variable as never reassigned and should be `const`, in a context where a variable is declared but not assigned. So the warning has no value and recommendation is incorrect. Dumb example:

```ts
  let req : string
  req = 'hello'
```
Deno lint warns on the first line:
`'req' is never reassigned. Use 'const' instead deno-lint(prefer-const)`

`req` isn't assigned a second time in the function. If I assign it a second time the warning goes away.

I understand what's going on here - the declaration (1st line) doesn't assign req a value, whereas the second line does, so that's the first assignment. However, in the above (declaration only) context it makes no sense for `req` to be const as it would have to be assigned at declaration time, which isn't always desirable.

_Edit: having read through the linter docs it appears as though the above is expected behaviour, when a single assignment happens at the same scope. The linter doesn't complain when initial assignment happens at another scope, the typical use-case for declaration without assignment. Closing._
author: qgates

    declare interface Crypto {
      readonly subtle: SubtleCrypto;
      getRandomValues<
        T extends
          | Int8Array
          | Int16Array
          | Int32Array
          | Uint8Array
          | Uint16Array
          | Uint32Array
          | Uint8ClampedArray
          | Float32Array
          | Float64Array
          | DataView
          | null,
      >(
        array: T,
      ): T;
      randomUUID(): string;
    }
author: FergoTheGreat

title: Excessive garbage collection
body:
I have just noticed a very odd thing. Node.js seems to be about 4 times faster than Deno or Chrome at running some arithmetic-heavy JavaScript (in my case, generating pseudo-random bytes using the [ISAAC PRNG](http://burtleburtle.net/bob/rand/isaacafa.html)). Upon profiling, I discovered that Node.js seems to run without stopping for garbage collection:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/310464/169953459-b8109927-d8ba-4e18-9430-89d637c07947.png)

whereas Deno is stopping for garbage collection every few milliseconds:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/310464/169953649-d6b607be-7302-4431-84c6-ae3fdf14c991.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/310464/169953681-b324163b-d840-4e82-a397-36e26c691425.png)

I thought maybe Node.js had a larger heap limit than Deno by default, so I attempted to increase Deno's heap size and thereby avoid triggering GC

    deno run --v8-flags="--max-old-space-size=4096,--max-semi-space-size=2048" isaac_benchmark.js

but to no avail. Here are some version numbers:

MacBook Pro (13-inch, Mid 2012)
2.9 GHz Dual-Core Intel Core i7
16 GB 1600 MHz DDR3
MacOS Big Sur 11.4

Node.js v17.9.0
v8 9.6.180.15-node.16

deno 1.22.0 (release, x86_64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.0.139.17
typescript 4.6.2

Here is a link to the benchmark: https://gist.github.com/diachedelic/4917abc492457d5f417f1a6ba63703f3.

I am very curious as to why Node.js has such different performance characteristics to Deno when they both use V8.

Thank you all for such a great runtime!
author: diachedelic

title: feat(fmt): infer config from .editorconfig
body:
Hi!

Deno could rely on [`.editorconfig`](https://editorconfig.org/) to determinate indent_style (space or tabs), indent_size, and max_line_length when they are not set in the deno's config file.

This is a [feature supported by Prettier](https://prettier.io/docs/en/configuration.html#editorconfig). Prettier supports also end_of_line and tab_width.

Note that an editorconfig file enables to change the configuration for different set of files.
author: Conaclos

title: Add memory and object-count report for `deno bench`
body:
Since deno can run with `--v8-flags=--expose-gc`, it would be nice if `deno bench` also report memory usage.
This feature is very handy when refactoring some code using Weak* object.

The flow would be something like
1. run bench code
2. measure speed
3. gc()
4. measure memory and count allocated object

Not sure if counting the allocated object is possible but Chrome browser has `performance.memory` API and the devtool has it [in their Heap Snapshot report](https://chromium-review.googlesource.com/c/chromium/src/+/1109438/)

#### Default console report

| benchmark | time (avg)       | memory (avg) | object count (avg)
|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| new A        | 10 ± 2 ns/iter  | 32 ± 8 B/iter    | 4 ± 2 JSObject/iter |

Some Inspiration: [`go test -bench`](https://go.googlesource.com/proposal/+/master/design/14313-benchmark-format.md)

title: `deno check` appears to require `@jsxImportSource` pragma with `"jsx": "react-jsx"` in configuration
body:
I've tested this on v1.21.2 and v1.22.0, both on macOS. Minimal example. Create these files an empty directory: ``` $ cat compile.jsonc { "compilerOptions": { "allowJs": true, "lib": ["DOM", "ES2020"], "strict": true, "jsx": "react-jsx", "jsxImportSource": "foo" } } $ cat im.json { "imports": { "foo/jsx-runtime": "./jsx-runtime.ts" } } $ cat test.tsx /** @jsxImportSource foo */ export const makeParagraph = () =>

A paragraph!

; $ cat test2.tsx export const makeParagraph = () =>

A paragraph!

; $ cat jsx-runtime.ts export namespace JSX { export type IntrinsicElements = { [key: string]: unknown }; } ``` This call succeeds: ``` $ deno check test.tsx -c compile.jsonc --importmap=im.json ``` But this one doesn't: ``` $ deno check test2.tsx -c compile.jsonc --importmap=im.json Check file:///Users/cscheid/Desktop/daily-log/2022/05/25/tsx-deno/test2.tsx error: TS7026 [ERROR]: JSX element implicitly has type 'any' because no interface 'JSX.IntrinsicElements' exists. export const makeParagraph = () =>

A paragraph!

; ~~~ at file:///Users/cscheid/Desktop/daily-log/2022/05/25/tsx-deno/test2.tsx:1:36 TS7026 [ERROR]: JSX element implicitly has type 'any' because no interface 'JSX.IntrinsicElements' exists. export const makeParagraph = () =>

A paragraph!

; ~~~~ at file:///Users/cscheid/Desktop/daily-log/2022/05/25/tsx-deno/test2.tsx:1:51 Found 2 errors. ``` It seems that `jsxImportSource` is not getting honored by some part of the code, even though `deno check` itself fails if I don't add `jsImportSource` to `compile.jsonc`. Strangely, if I use a "bad" value in either of those _two_ fields (say, replacing `foo` with `bar` in either the .jsonc or the pragma), `deno check` fails.

If you attempt to execute one of these files you get a "module not found" error, as it tries to load an import for a file the cache renamed. Example output (note the double extension) And here is the `lambda.ts.js` transpiled file, notice the imports for `.ts` files

title: Dynamic import blocked after create a worker
body:
Here's an example:

main.ts
```typescript
new Worker(new URL('./worker.ts', import.meta.url), { type: 'module' })
await import('./sub1.ts')
console.log('ok')
```
worker.ts
```typescript
console.log('worker')
```
sub1.ts
```typescript
await import('./sub2.ts')
console.log('sub1')
```
sub2.ts
```typescript
console.log('sub2')
```

Run `main.ts` using `deno run --allow-read main.ts`, it will print
```
sub2
worker
sub1
```
but never print `ok`.

Deno version `1.22.0`

title: `deno types` includes a reference to `deno.net` lib, which is already included at line 6531
body:
As the title says, running `deno types` will generate a file that makes a reference to `deno.net` library, at the very top. Problem is, that file won't exist outside of Deno, and raises an error. However, the error can be completely fixed by just commenting out/removing the reference, as it is redundant anyway. The `deno.net` file is already included in the generated file "bundle" at line 6531, so I don't see why it would need to reference it whilst exporting anyway.

Is it possible for this reference to be stripped out when exporting? It's not really a big problem since it could just be commented out, but it would be nicer if the types from `deno types` would be working without any modifications.
author: andre4ik3

title: `deno compile --target`: add support for URL targets
body:
Currently `deno compile --target` only supports the officially built architectures available on https://dl.deno.land/. For example `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu` isn't available in the officially maintained builds, but there are community maintained unofficial builds of it.

The suggestion is that a URL target such as `deno compile --target "https://deno.toaxl.com/v1.21.3/deno-aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip"` would download the given file and treat it as a release zip, unzipping and creating a standalone executable.

title: deno tasks combined with import maps (feat request)
body:
Hi.
To run Lume in a project I have this `import_map.json` file:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "lume/": "https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.9.1/"
  }
}
```

And this `deno.json` file:

```json
{
  "importMap": "import_map.json",
  "tasks": {
    "build": "deno run -A https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.9.1/ci.ts"
  }
}
```

So everytime I want to upgrade the version of Lume I have to edit both files. It would be great if the `build` task could use the import map file to resolve the lume version. I can think of several ways to do this, but not sure what's the best:

## deno run

`deno run` has a broken support for import maps, because it doesn't work for bare imports, only url-like imports. I've opened this issue some time ago #14066

The idea is that `deno run -A lume/ci.ts` could use the actual import map file to resolve to the full url.

## deno eval

The problem of the previous idea is it doesn't comply with the specs, that say that import maps cannot be used for root modules https://github.com/WICG/import-maps#remapping-doesnt-work-for-script.
But the specs say that import maps could work with inlined scripts (`<script type="module">...</script>`), that's is the equivalent of `deno eval`. So the idea is to be able to do the following:

```
deno eval -A "import 'lume/ci.ts';"
```

This doesn't work actually in Deno.

## variable substitution

Another idea is to provide a way to substitute variables similar to GitHub Actions. For example:

```
deno run -A ${{ importMap["lume/"] }}ci.ts
```

`importMap` variable should contain the entries of the import map being used.

title: Deno panic when running lots of child processes
body:
Dear Deno maintainer, I got the following request via my terminal;

```
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.22.1
Args: ["deno", "run", "--import-map", "../import-map.json", "--allow-all", "../executables/sandbox.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'internal error: entered unreachable code', runtime/errors.rs:61:19
stack backtrace:
   0:        0x104e93788 - <std::sys_common::backtrace::_print::DisplayBacktrace as core::fmt::Display>::fmt::h10cf06316d33e2a9
   1:        0x104780804 - core::fmt::write::h1faf18c959c3a8df
   2:        0x104e8e520 - std::io::Write::write_fmt::h86ab231360bc97d2
   3:        0x104e983d0 - std::panicking::default_hook::{{closure}}::h36e628ffaf3cd44f
   4:        0x104e9809c - std::panicking::default_hook::h3ee1564a7544e58f
   5:        0x1046bb538 - deno::setup_panic_hook::{{closure}}::h28567ebc70677265
   6:        0x104e98ec0 - std::panicking::rust_panic_with_hook::h191339fbd2fe2360
   7:        0x104e98c14 - std::panicking::begin_panic_handler::{{closure}}::h91c230befd9929e3
   8:        0x104e98bac - std::sys_common::backtrace::__rust_end_short_backtrace::haaaeebb1d37476b3
   9:        0x104e98b78 - _rust_begin_unwind
  10:        0x10477ebf8 - core::panicking::panic_fmt::h4fe1013b011ef602
  11:        0x1047804d4 - core::panicking::panic::he60bb304466ccbaf
  12:        0x1049d3bb0 - deno_runtime::errors::get_io_error_class::he1030a14b8b0d726
  13:        0x1049d33ec - deno_runtime::errors::get_error_class_name::h182febce679b3e92
  14:        0x1044c4100 - deno::errors::get_error_class_name::hfabafe9efa9657bd
  15:        0x104822aec - deno_core::ops::OpError::new::h9d14d9669cdb3905
  16:        0x104aacc80 - <extern "C" fn(A0) .> R as v8::support::CFnFrom<F>>::mapping::c_fn::h3081f66b6dcbd808
```

This happens consistently when I add a line to my code that runs an order of magnitude _more_ child processes. I'm trying to use Deno to 1) download a lot of bare Git repositories, then 2) retrieve a list of tags for each of them, then 3) run `git log -1` on each of those tags to discover which commit hash they belong to, then 4) get a list of all commits with all info, and combine it with the results from step 3. Adding steps 2 and 3 will cause a panic, presumably because there are now _a lot_ more child processes. Without steps 2 and 3 the script runs beautifully.

Running more of these child processes serially, instead of in parallel, will avoid the panic.

Unfortunately I cannot easily share my code, but at your request I could put in a few hours to make public minimal repro case. Love your work in Deno, please let me know if/how I could help :)

title: Disabling type checking for vendored dependencies or directories
body:
I have third party dependencies that work with the default `--no-check=remote` option. However once I vendor them and updating `deno.json` to use the `vendor/import_map.json`, I start getting errors type errors. It appears the only workaround for having my dependencies vendored is to run `deno run` and `deno test` with the `--no-check` flag to disable all checking. I'd like to have checking for my other local files when I run `deno test`. According to the most recent release [blog post](https://deno.com/blog/v1.22#default-type-checking-behavior-has-been-updated), it looks like it's planned to keep local type checking for `deno test` and to remove it for `deno run`.

In our `deno.json` file we can configure `deno lint` and `deno fmt` to exclude files and directories. It would be nice if we could do the same with deno's type checking so that we can disable it for the vendor directory generated by `deno vendor`. That would allow me to use `deno vendor` without giving up type checking when I run my tests.

Below is an example of one of the type errors I get related to my vendored dependencies. I don't get the type error when not using vendored dependencies.

```
error: TS2339 [ERROR]: Property 'spawn' does not exist on type 'typeof Deno'. 'Deno.spawn' is an unstable API. Did you forget to run with the '--unstable' flag?
  const { status, stderr } = await Deno.spawn(Deno.execPath(), {
```

title: Add more platforms to `Deno.build.os`
body:
Deno runs on FreeBSD [with the help of some patches](https://cgit.freebsd.org/ports/tree/www/deno)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40470127/172024739-27dd1fc8-d9c4-4419-83ac-1828d95695cb.png)

On that build `Deno.build.os` returns `freebsd` but type checking will fail given `Deno.build.os`'s definiton only mentions windows, darwin, and linux.

While deno is officially supported on windows, darwin, and linux, it would be best to add all feasible platforms. [Node includes even more](https://nodejs.org/api/process.html#process_process_platform)

It would be preferable to add all of these platforms at once, as this would be a breaking change

body:
Using the following `deno.jsonc`:
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "lib": [
      "deno.worker"
    ]
  },
  "tasks": {
    "test": "deno test --quiet --shuffle --reload=file://. --import-map=import_map.json -A --lock=lock.json --coverage=coverage/data",
    "coverage": "deno task --quiet test && deno coverage --exclude=\"internal|test\" --unstable --lcov --output=coverage/profile.lcov coverage/data",
  }
}
```

And running `deno task coverage`, throws the following:
```
error: Error parsing script 'coverage'.

Caused by:
    Expected command following boolean operator.
    
    Unexpected character.
      "internal|test" --unstable --lcov --output=coverage/profile.
      ~
```

It seems like it detects the pipe operator (`|`) regardless of the existence of surrounding quotation marks (marking the pipe as a parameter for the command).

title: Suggestion: `deno check` and `deno compile`
body:
I have a suggestion that might be a little bit out-of-scope for Deno, but I hope you may be interested in this :)

As you might be aware, Node.js has recently stabilized their ESM support. TypeScript now has a `node16` resolver to target it, but it’s a bit of a mess due to how users are expected to use *output* extensions instead of source extensions. See: https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/49083

But then I realized Deno might be in a unique position to help out here. You have already integrated TSC in a way that suppresses errors about the `.ts` extensions.

If you could implement a `deno check` command that does nothing but run the integrated TSC on a project, we would have a `tsc --no-emit` alternative that actually works with `.ts` extensions.

*And* you have already integrated SWC as well. So you could offer a high-performance `deno compile`  command to replace the emit functionality of `tsc` as well. At this point you might understand I’m effectively asking your help to implement the `.ts` extension rewriting that the TypeScript developers themselves are refusing to implement 😅

But I do believe this could be advantageous to Deno as well. It would help library developers who wish to target both Node and Deno with a single codebase. The functionality could also work as a “gateway drug” to Node users who might find Deno offers a more complete toolchain around TypeScript, even if they still have to rely on Node for actually running their code. And from there it’s a smaller step for them to migrate to the runtime as well.

If you think this functionality does not belong in the Deno CLI itself, do you think it could be feasible to use Deno’s integrations to build this as a stand-alone tool on top of Deno?
author: arendjr

title: Suggestion: Allow `--compat` to be used with `check` and `bundle` subcommands

I would like to extend its use so it can also be passed to the `check` and `bundle` commands. This would allow people targeting Node.js to use Deno as a `tsc` replacement that is compatible with the ESM resolution as implemented by Deno.

author: Theryston

title: Remove type checking for unanalysable dynamic imports
body:
For dynamic imports with non-static-string specifiers like `await import("./" + "foo.ts")`, we do a separate [type check at runtime](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/3d2394954c61a434bd6227ad3e0c5dea38361b6a/cli/proc_state.rs#L519) with the imported module as the root. Users write these when importing a directory of modules or something. I propose to no longer type check these, for the following reasons:
1. As of 1.23.0, the primary workflow is checking ahead of time with `deno check` or your editor, and not checking during `deno run`. That already aligns with the proposed behaviour. The only way then to type check these truly dynamic imports is `deno run --check`, which is an awkward discrepancy. So I think we shouldn't do it then either.
2. When do you really ever want to see type checking errors part way through execution of your program? Users should instead have a `dynamic_imports.ts` (maybe auto-generated by scanning a directory) which tracks such imports and is checked statically in CI: `deno check main.ts dynamic_imports.ts`.
3. The way we check these dynamically discovered graph segments is not even correct. For example if the static graph includes global augmentations, the type check for the dynamic module is not aware of those unless it for some reason imports back into the augmenting module. This sort of incremental addition to a type-checked graph is not a thing in tsc-world. We don't have the prior art or tools to do such a thing correctly.

Note that points 1 and 2 apply to web workers as well, but let's leave those out of scope for now. Eventually I think we should do the same thing for them and introduce `deno check --worker`.

author: KnorpelSenf

title: lsp: detect import map changes automatically
body:
It seems import map changes require restarting the LSP (e.g. via "Deno: Restart Language Server" in [vscode_deno](https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno)) in order to take effect, which does not align with the experience of using TypeScript with Node.js (vanilla or even with [PnP](https://yarnpkg.com/features/pnp)), which detect package changes in a few seconds.

This may be eased if editor extensions instruct the LSP restart if changes are made to import maps, but does not fix the fundamental issue. The LSP is expected to detect these changes automatically.

title: Performance degradation in deno 1.11.0
body:
I just noticed that deno 1.11.0 had a huge performance degradation. And that problem remains in 1.23.0.

Steps to reproduce:

```
$ git clone https://github.com/vslinko/deno-csv.git
$ cd deno-csv
$ gco benchmark-test
$ cd benchmarks
$ deno upgrade --version 1.10.3
$ make test-deno-csv-CSVReader
test-deno-csv-CSVReader
Read 500001 lines for 8.745 seconds
$ deno upgrade --version 1.11.0
$ make test-deno-csv-CSVReader
test-deno-csv-CSVReader
Read 500001 lines for 57.650 seconds
```

I think the cause might be in [the array destructurization in TextDecoder#decode](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/10805/files#diff-b3a759f63320020b99af869bf4847169acad9b8e8436373e8693976c29cc4a32R4237-R4245).
author: vslinko

title: Cannot update the vendored modules when import map is in output directory
body:
As of Deno v1.23.0, the vendor command modifies the configuration file, to the import-map to `vendor/import_map.json`. Deno will then complain if the user tries to update the vendored modules.

```console
$ deno vendor ./_config.js -f
error: Specifying an import map file (vendor/import_map.json) in the deno vendor output directory is not supported. Please specify no import map or one located outside this directory.
```

This isn't a problem in v1.22.3, since the `deno.json` config file isn't modified.

**Notes**
- Yes, I tested this on both v1.23.0 and v1.22.3
- I have a hunch that this comes from https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14836

it would be nice to have a feature to get files from deno cache in plain text.

The more i use deno, the more my code becomes hybrid. Meaning i use a lot of classes 1:1 on the server and in the browser. Right now i have to cache them myself in plain text and send them with the http requests, meaning i have a second cache doing completely the same as the deno cache. It would be nice to have a function like Deno.fromCache() that gets the same file from the cache that import would use, just without executing it (maybe even puts it there if its not present yet).

Regards

title: Proposed changes to `Deno.spawn()` API
body:
- `SpawnOutput` should extend `ChildStatus` instead of composing it, discussed favourably on Discord a while ago.
- `SpawnOutput::stdout`, `SpawnOutput::stderr`, `Child::stdin`, `Child::stdout` and `Child::stderr` shouldn't be optional, instead make them getters that throw at runtime if that stream wasn't set to `"piped"`. Remove the complicated `<T extends SpawnOptions = SpawnOptions>` which we currently need to give proper type hints for the availability of these fields. Their typings for these would get increasingly complex if it became dependent on more options (e.g. `SpawnOptions::pty` which if set should make the stdio streams unavailable), so we should make them always present.

author: EnigmaOne1

title: lsp: warning messages may not be accurate due to caching
body:
### Problem
If you don't specify the version of a dependency, deno lsp will warn you that you're *implicitly using the **latest** version* and it shows a version number, the problem is that the shown version is not really the latest version, its the latest version on the local system cache.

The actual latest version is 0.5.0 in this next example:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22427111/174908280-60d44e00-6cab-4ffe-81c8-e4d9329db828.png)

### Why this matters

The message is misleading and the user will rightfully think that hes using the latest version, this is not desirable and among other things it can lead to having vulnerable outdated dependencies.

### Suggestions

- Improve warning message which I think is located here https://github.com/denoland/dotland/blob/2b6850e057eabc85567f17e37c05327be90f1ccf/routes/x/module.tsx#L543 to something like `latest version in cahce`

- Add a new codelens action `update to latest version` which ignores the cache and check the version remotely

title: deno compile produces slower ARM binaries than x86 binaries on Apple M1
body:
```
$ deno --version
deno 1.22.0 (release, x86_64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.0.139.17
typescript 4.6.2
```

Installed via Homebrew.

On an M1, the binary produced with `--target aarch64-apple-darwin` is consistently slower than the one produced by `--target x86_64-apple-darwin`.

On first run, `--target x86_64-apple-darwin` took `2.726` seconds as measured by the `time` command. On subsequent runs it took `0.097` seconds.

I missed the first run of `--target aarch64-apple-darwin`, but subsequent commands run consistently at about `0.31` seconds. This is more than 3x slower.

This is very surprising as one would expect the x86 binary to consistently be slower than the ARM binary.
author: taoeffect

title: Incomplete SubtleCrypto.deriveKey typescript declaration
body:
SubtleCrypto.deriveKey in [lib.deno_crypto.d.ts](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/crypto/lib.deno_crypto.d.ts#L277) is incomplete,
[MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SubtleCrypto/deriveKey) define the following type for the algorithm parameter:
- [EcdhKeyDeriveParams](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/EcdhKeyDeriveParams)
- [HkdfParams](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HkdfParams)
- [Pbkdf2Params](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Pbkdf2Params)

lib.deno_crypto.d.ts declare only `AlgorithmIdentifier | HkdfParams | Pbkdf2Params`.

EcdhKeyDeriveParams is missing in type declaration but is well implemented.

title: Support `$DENO_PROXY` for deno registry mirror
body:
As far as I know, **deno** supports `export HTTPS_PROXY ` and we could use it to redirect all DENO's network traffic to DENO registry mirror.

Since the DENO mirror server doesn't have proxied server's certificate, it can't provide ability to cache packages.

I think it would be better to provide a variable `$DENO_PROXY` like `$GOPROXY` to support registry mirror.

title: Preloading modules
body:
I want to be able to start up Deno scripts fast even for deep dependency trees and/or an empty Deno cache.

I was originally thinking server push but today I learned that [HTTP/2 Push is dead](https://evertpot.com/http-2-push-is-dead/) (thank you @lucacasonato).

This could be done via `Link: rel=preload` response headers so that Deno is able to be informed of transitive dependencies/modules long before its even fetched a module that imports them so that the cache can be populated faster and lower overall perceived latency, etc.

See also https://github.com/denoland/dotland/issues/2205.
author: mfulton26

title: lsp: test ignore/only code actions
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

It's fiddly to convert `Deno.test(name, fn)` to `Deno.test({ name, fn, ignore: true })` or `Deno.test({ name, fn, only: true })`. Compare this to jest where it's as simple as changing `test()` to `test.skip()` or `test.only()`.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add a code actions to toggle ingnore and only on tests.

## Examples

### Ingoring/focusing basic test definition

This is pretty straightforward

```js
Deno.test("name", () => {})
// trigger "ignore test"
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, ignore: true)
```

```js
Deno.test("name", () => {})
// trigger "only test"
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, only: true)
```

### Ingoring/focusing object test definition

If the property exists set it to true, otherwise add the property on the end as true.

```js
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {})
// trigger "ignore test"
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, ignore: true)
```

```js
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, only: false)
// trigger "only test"
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, only: true)
```

### Unignoring/unfocussing object test definition

This is a little complex as there are a few valid ways to do this depending on the code

#### When the test definition only has `name`, `fn,` and (optionally) `ignore`/`only` properties

We have three options. Given the following code

```js
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, ignore: true)
```

Triggering "unignore test" could give either:

```ts
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, ignore: false)
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {})
Deno.test("name", () => {})
```

#### When the test definition has additional properties

Given the following code

```js
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, ignore: true, sanitizeResources: true)
```

Triggering unignore could give either:

```ts
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, ignore: false, sanitizeResources: true)
Deno.test({ name: "name", fn: () => {}, sanitizeResources: true)
```

#### Which is best?

In general I think it's best to modify the code as little as possible. For this reason I think we should opt for the top option in both cases. Preserve the definition object and set the appropriate option to `false`.
