title: Tab completion fails for deno.land/x/polkadot with `throw_on_side_effect: Some(true)`
body:
Hi, I'm building a online repl playground called [Subshell](https://subshell.xyz) for the [deno.land/x/polkadot](https://deno.land/x/polkadot) library using deno, but found some issue with tab completion functionality in deno repl, and added a small patch to fix that.

However I'm not sure if there are better ways to do it. 

Steps to reproduce:

First save the following snippet to `tini.ts`:
```
import { ApiPromise, WsProvider } from 'https://deno.land/x/polkadot@0.0.1/api/mod.ts';

async function initApi(){
  const PROVIDER = Deno.env.get("PROVIDER") ?? "wss://rpc.polkadot.io";
  const provider = new WsProvider( PROVIDER );
  return await ApiPromise.create({ provider });
}

console.log("api is initializing. Please hold on...");

const api = await initApi();
```

Then start a repl using tini.ts as eval-file. Pressing tab(s) on the following input doesn't show any completion:

```
$ deno repl --eval-file=tini.ts
api is initializing. Please hold on...
Deno 1.23.1
exit using ctrl+d or close()
> api.tx.
```

(The equivalent code in Node.js will give such output:)
```
(node)
> api.tx.
api.tx.__proto__                   api.tx.hasOwnProperty              api.tx.isPrototypeOf               api.tx.propertyIsEnumerable
api.tx.toLocaleString              api.tx.valueOf

api.tx.apply                       api.tx.arguments                   api.tx.bind                        api.tx.call
api.tx.caller                      api.tx.constructor                 api.tx.toString

api.tx.auctions                    api.tx.authorship                  api.tx.babe                        api.tx.balances
api.tx.bounties                    api.tx.childBounties               api.tx.claims                      api.tx.configuration
api.tx.council                     api.tx.crowdloan                   api.tx.democracy                   api.tx.dmp
api.tx.electionProviderMultiPhase  api.tx.grandpa                     api.tx.hrmp                        api.tx.identity
api.tx.imOnline                    api.tx.indices                     api.tx.initializer                 api.tx.length
api.tx.multisig                    api.tx.name                        api.tx.paraInclusion               api.tx.paraInherent
api.tx.paras                       api.tx.parasDisputes               api.tx.parasShared                 api.tx.phragmenElection
api.tx.preimage                    api.tx.proxy                       api.tx.registrar                   api.tx.scheduler
api.tx.session                     api.tx.slots                       api.tx.staking                     api.tx.system
api.tx.technicalCommittee          api.tx.technicalMembership         api.tx.timestamp                   api.tx.tips
api.tx.treasury                    api.tx.ump                         api.tx.utility                     api.tx.vesting
api.tx.voterList                   api.tx.xcmPallet
```

And after some trial and error, I have located the relevant bit in deno's source tree that causes the problem. 

By changing this line from

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/8d82ba729937baf83011354242cabc3d50c13dc2/cli/tools/repl/editor.rs#L132

to

```
          throw_on_side_effect: None,
```

tab completion will work again:
```
$ subshell repl --eval-file=tini.ts
api is initializing. Please hold on...
Subshell 0.1.2
exit using ctrl+d or close()
> api.tx.
system                      imOnline                    proxy                       paras                       length                      hasOwnProperty
scheduler                   democracy                   multisig                    initializer                 name                        __lookupGetter__
preimage                    council                     bounties                    dmp                         arguments                   __lookupSetter__
babe                        technicalCommittee          childBounties               ump                         caller                      isPrototypeOf
timestamp                   phragmenElection            tips                        hrmp                        constructor                 propertyIsEnumerable
indices                     technicalMembership         electionProviderMultiPhase  parasDisputes               apply                       valueOf
balances                    treasury                    voterList                   registrar                   bind                        toLocaleString
authorship                  claims                      configuration               slots                       call
staking                     vesting                     parasShared                 auctions                    toString
session                     utility                     paraInclusion               crowdloan                   __defineGetter__
grandpa                     identity                    paraInherent                xcmPallet                   __defineSetter__
> api.tx.
```

I'm wondering if we can safely disable `throw_on_side_effect` in deno by default, so I don't have to maintain a separate fork. Or should there be a command line switch allowing people to disable this flag?
author: btwiuse

title: 🙏🏻 `Deno.inspect()` should conform to NodeJS quoting behavior (or allow customization)
body:
`Deno.inspect()` varies from the NodeJS implementation by giving double quotes priority for formatting strings. The only note about this is that "we prefer double quotes as the default" without further explanation.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/eadf943e594a5ebf95ddf7e007cfb7d2dcff130e/ext/console/02_console.js#L698-L719

This choice is unfortunate as it varies from NodeJS, making simple output comparisons for testing impossible.

It's also known to be a much worse reading environment for accessibility, adding unneeded visual clutter.

Changing this back to the NodeJS behavior is simple and, IMO, desirable. Alternatively, adding an option such as a `quotePriority` array to `Deno.InspectOptions` would be an alternative which preserves current behavior but allows reversion to the NodeJS behavior.

Thanks for the consideration.

title: No apparent use case for `deno task --no-config`

```
error: No config file found
```

If there is a config file with a non-default name, then using just `--config` works without any need for `--no-config` as well.

```shell
deno run --allow-net --allow-env --allow-write --allow-read --config tsconfig.json --unstable https://deno.land/x/oak_nest@v1.11.0/example/main.ts
```
This will normal serve
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19886374/175888249-91279b3a-b29c-484a-8b55-5b97c9b686db.png)


But when I bundle this file to js:
```shell
deno bundle https://deno.land/x/oak_nest@v1.11.0/example/main.ts  main.js
```
Then run this js:
```shell
deno run --allow-net --allow-env --allow-write --allow-read --config tsconfig.json --unstable main.js
```
It called error:
```
error: Uncaught SyntaxError: Identifier 'mod' has already been declared
const mod = {
      ^
    at <anonymous> (file:///C:/wk/deno/deno-oak-nest/main.js:2037:7)
```
I know that packaging and merging is a complex thing, but I hope deal with the same name orders of parameters such as rollup or webpack.

author: JLCarveth

title: deno_emit fails depending on whether or not a breakpoint is set.
body:
Not sure if this is a Deno issue, or a deno_emit issue, but it seems like debugging shouldn't affect the code like this so I'm assuming this is an issue with Deno.

**To reproduce:**

- create `emit.js`
```js
import {emit} from "https://deno.land/x/emit@0.3.0/mod.ts";

const result = await emit(new URL("https://deno.land/std@0.145.0/path/mod.ts"));
console.log(result);
```

- Run `deno run -A --unstable --inspect-brk emit.js`
- Connect a debugger (I'm using vscode since Chrome DevTools can't place breakpoints on files that haven't been run yet)
- place a breakpoint at the `console.log` line.
- Resume the debugger.

**Actual result**
`Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: WebAssembly.compile(): Argument 0 must be a buffer source`

**Expected result**
The code runs without any errors.

Some things to note:
- removing the breakpoint seems to make the code run, but when the process is done it keeps hanging rather than exiting automatically.
- removing `--inspect-brk` make the code run like normal.
- Enabling 'break on caught/uncaught exceptions' doesn't actually pause the debugger when the promise rejects, though I think I've seen this happen before with promises so this might be intended.
author: jespertheend

title: deno_runtime::ExitCode should derive Default
body:
(Discussion here)[https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/778060818046386176/991780844559667310].

For users who embed deno, there is no way to init `deno_runtime::ops::os::init`.
https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14814  added an `ExitCode` struct as a required input arg, ExitCode's data is private an cannot be set by external packages.

`ExitCode` should derive `Default`
author: curtisallen

title: Unsound unsafe usages
body:
[clippy::mut_from_ref](https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/#mut_from_ref)

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/core/ops_metrics.rs#L56-L62

It is not safe to create `&mut [u8]` which is pointing to uninitialized memory. 
See also <https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines/issues/71>

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/ext/net/ops_tls.rs#L378-L382

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/serde_v8/de.rs#L679-L692

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/serde_v8/de.rs#L708-L720

[clippy::uninit_vec](https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/#uninit_vec)

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/serde_v8/magic/bytestring.rs#L52-L66

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/serde_v8/magic/u16string.rs#L35-L49

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/snapshots/lib.rs#L30-L53

What is this?

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/serde_v8/magic/value.rs#L30-L48

Related (maybe): 
+ https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12341
+ https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12653
+ https://github.com/denoland/deno_core/issues/129

author: dsherret

title: the ~25 megabyte zipped executable
body:
See https://deno.land/x/manual@v1.20.3/introduction.md, https://deno.land/manual

> Given a URL to a Deno program, it is runnable with nothing more than [the ~25 megabyte zipped executable](https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases).

I downloaded [v1.23.2](https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v1.23.2). The executable is 91.2MB. 

`node` v19.0.0-nightly20220630350a6a8d59 executable is 82.2MB.

The documentation claims ~25MB executable.

Where do I find or how to build the ~25MB `deno` executable?

title: Deno.run fails to resolve .cmd executable on Windows even though .cmd is in PATHEXT
body:
This is weird:

```shellsession
> deno
Deno 1.23.2
exit using ctrl+d or close()
> Deno.run({ cmd: ["npm"] })
Uncaught NotFound: program not found
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:170:12)
    at opRun (deno:runtime/js/40_process.js:28:17)
    at Object.run (deno:runtime/js/40_process.js:111:17)
    at <anonymous>:2:6
> console.log(Deno.env.get("PATHEXT"))
.COM;.EXE;.BAT;.CMD;.VBS;.VBE;.JS;.JSE;.WSF;.WSH;.MSC;.RB;.RBW;.CPL
undefined
> Deno.run({ cmd: ["npm.cmd"] })
Process { rid: 3, pid: 21176 }
```
author: dsherret

title: Dynamic local import always tries to get fetched from URL when running from deno.land package install
body:
Hello,

I have this scenario:
`a.ts` file creates a `b.ts` file and dynamically imports it later inside `a.ts`.

Locally it works as expected.

However, once the package is pulled through deno.land (`deno install --name readme-manager --reload --allow-all https://deno.land/x/readme_manager@v1.4.2/mod.ts` and run locally I get an error telling me that the `b.ts` file it is tried to resolve is a URL.

```bash
Download https://deno.land/home/user/.config/readme-manager/mod.ts
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Module not found "https://deno.land/home/user/.config/readme-manager/mod.ts".
    at async mod (https://deno.land/x/readme_manager@v1.4.2/mod.ts:122:31)
    at async https://deno.land/x/readme_manager@v1.4.2/mod.ts:164:1
```

Is this intended or a bug and is there anything I can do to make this work 

note: I tried with absolute and relative paths to see if it was to do with that but same outcome with both pathing formats.
author: jukefr

title: Following orders and reporting crash.
body:
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: linux x86_64

author: nitrologic

title: Respect Microsoft Policy for locations of configuration and data files
body:
[From Microsoft Desktop App Requirements](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-gb/windows/win32/win_cert/certification-requirements-for-windows-desktop-apps) (Edited)
> 10.3 Your app data, which must be shared among users on the computer, should be stored within ProgramData 
> 10.4 Your app's data that is exclusive to a specific user and that is not to be shared with other users of the computer, must be stored in `Users\<username>\AppData `
> 10.5 Your app must never write directly to the "Windows" directory and or subdirectories

I am not asking that `.deno/` directory becomes optionally movable to a different location. I am asking that the default location should comply with Windows Policies so that the user experience is optimal. It should be somewhere in `%appdata%`

Windows is not Linux. The home directory is no place for binary apps or any apps at all. Neither is something as audacious as the xdg_spec the appropriate lens through which to store configuration data in Windows. The home directory should just contain my documents, desktop, downloads, favourites and so on. It doesn't need to contain `.bin/`, `.deno/`, `.config/` or the sort. It looks **odd** when you see such files lingering around. Those should be where they are supposed to be, without having to configure a `DENO_PATH` or whatnot. Of course, I am not amused.

If they were so common as to warrant keeping them in the home directory, I would've added them to quick access if I so needed. If every app did the same as Deno does right now, our home directories would be filled with dotfiles and dotdirectories according to everyone's opinionated tastes, instead of following an established organizational structure.

Kind regards.
p.s. Same style as in https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/111446
author: wmstack

title: watcher: When deno is running as a daemon process with watching enabled, some ANSI escape sequences are printed even though stdout/stderr are not a TTY
body: (deno 1.23.1) Here's a screenshot from a log viewer (deno is running as a system daemon/background process, not in a terminal). Watching can also be useful in atypical situations like this. I think [printing](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2769d602506af1953312b28580506fca3fcbe030/cli/file_watcher.rs#L121) of [the ANSI escape sequence](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2769d602506af1953312b28580506fca3fcbe030/cli/file_watcher.rs#L25) should be automatically gated using `atty::is`, similar to how an [`is_tty` boolean is already being used elsewhere in the codebase](https://github.com/denoland/deno/search?q=is_tty).

author: yilmazdurmaz

title: What determines which fmt features are configurable?
body:
Two classic bikeshed debates have been dealt with in very different ways in Deno fmt:

1. "Tabs vs. spaces" – give the people a config boolean to end the bikeshedding
2. "Semicolons vs. ASI" – just pick a side, and lock issues if people try to discuss it

These seem like philosophically opposite approaches to addressing the problem of bikeshedding. Is there any reason why it's this way round?
author: callumlocke

title: Deno has panicked. deno test | jq .
body:
I inadvertently tried to pipe test results to `jq` and deno panicked.

Repeatable test case is as follows:
``` bash
mkdir -p /tmp/deno-panic
cd /tmp/deno-panic
export RUST_BACKTRACE=1
cat <<EOF > test.js
console.log("hello")
EOF
deno test | jq . 2>/dev/null

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos x86_64
Version: 1.23.2
Args: ["deno", "test"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'failed printing to stdout: Broken pipe (os error 32)', library/std/src/io/stdio.rs:1017:9
stack backtrace:
   0: _rust_begin_unwind
   1: core::panicking::panic_fmt
   2: std::io::stdio::_print
   3: <deno::tools::test::PrettyTestReporter as deno::tools::test::TestReporter>::report_plan
   4: tokio::runtime::task::harness::poll_future
   5: tokio::runtime::task::raw::poll
   6: deno_runtime::tokio_util::run_basic
   7: deno::main
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
```

Same setup but piping to `cat` does not panic.
``` bash
deno test | cat
hello
running 0 tests from ./test.js

ok | 0 passed | 0 failed (14ms)
```

I'm testing with jq 1.6 installed on a mac with homebrew.

One last comment on the command I'm testing with...
```
deno test | jq . 2>/dev/null
```
I'm suppressing jq's STDERR 'cos it complains about not being able to parse what it gets from deno (not surprised by that). Deno panics whether or not I include the `2>/dev/null`, but wanted to keep this report focused on the output from deno.

Not sure what would be different in having `jq` on the other end of that pipe instead of `cat`.

title: is it possible to grant access for only one-time use when running without flags
body:
I have v1.23.1 installed.

When running a script without access flags, it prompts me to allow/deny access but does so only once and grants access to any part of the script afterward.

I would like to grant access only for that line that needs an operation and prompted again when another line (whether separate or called by that last line) wants the same access. It may be exhausting but this may come useful when debugging the script with outside libraries. And when everything is seemingly fine, I could then use access flags to run the script.

By the way, current prompting only says it needs access but does not show where that happens. It would be nice to see the path and line number encountered.

title: Panic caused by missing h in @jsxFrag pragma
body:
**Reproduction Steps**

1. Create a new Fresh project
2. Add the below code at `routes/posts/[id].tsx`
3. Run

```tsx
/** @jsx h */
/** @jsxFrag */
import { h } from "preact";
import { Handlers, PageProps } from "$fresh/server.ts";
import { Marked, Parsed } from "markdown";

type Data = { markup: Parsed };

export const handler: Handlers<Data> = {
  async GET(req, ctx) {
    const markdown = await Deno.readTextFile(`posts/${ctx.params.id}.md`)
    const markup = Marked.parse(markdown);
    const resp = await ctx.render({ markup });
    return resp;
  },
};

export default function Greet(props: PageProps<Data>) {
  return (
    <>
      <div
        dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
          __html: props.data.markup.content,
        }}
      />
    </>
  );
}

```

Note that `/** @jsxFrag */` should be `/** @jsxFrag h */` and this typo causes a panic.

author: Siilwyn

title: Is deno ecosystem production ready now?
body:
Although I am big fan of `deno`'s future, which can 
- use typescript out of box 
- without `nodejs`'s annoying not chainable loader, or wait compiling, leading to many configurations

I am still hesitating to switch from `node` to `deno` now.

Emm, here are some questions...
- ~~Is `deno` **ecosystem** production ready now?~~
> This depends on what your requirements are for production software
- ~~Which companies and apps are using `deno`?~~
https://github.com/denoland/deno/wiki#companies-interested-in-deno
- Is there a workflow, self hostable, one can publish fully typescript package, and use it in browser, and also in node for compactibility?
- ~~How does registries guarantee to future-reproducible build?~~ 
https://deno.land/manual/linking_to_external_code/integrity_checking
- Is there a `git`-ish registry like [cdnjs/cdnjs](https://github.com/cdnjs/cdnjs)?
- Is there a cheat sheet, cover famous `nodejs` library's deno equivalents, or high lighted no equivalent, like koa to oak, listed in style like [rosetta](https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Pacman/Rosetta)?
- Is there a Hot-Module-Replacement guide?
- ~~Is there a way to alter partial dependency code, without effect other projects?~~
I think there is a `deno vendor` download dependencies to local dir, which can path library locally instead of global modification.

Thanks.

### Additional
I think it would be nice to have some kind of deno server standard
- show dependents in `deno info X`
- how many times N were package X is downloaded from central in recent Y time
- how many times N were package X is referenced from cache in recent Y time

author: ghost

title: TypeScript/JavaScript code fixes should have higher priority than linting code fixes
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Quick fix actions (`CTRL+.` keyboard shortcut) are ordered in inefficient way which force me to do unnecessary keypresses.

**Screenshot**

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/47059999/177151435-57a4cdee-2758-4813-9e34-464bc2f8275f.png)

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I see two possible solutions for that:
 - "Update import" action place should be before lint actions as it's used more often.
 - Quick fix actions order could be configured in `settings.json`

title: `deno compile` not respect `deno.jsonc`'s `.importMap`
body:
### What
`deno compile` not respect `deno.jsonc`'s `.importMap`

### Reproduce
```sh
$ make test
```

### Actual
```txt
$ ./dist/reproduce
error: Relative import path "colorette" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ from "file:///path/to/reproduce.ts"
```

### Expected
```txt
$ ./dist/reproduce
hello world
````

author: loynoir

title: import.meta.resolve second argument
body:
Chrome is now moving towards shipping `import.meta.resolve` as discussed in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/7296.

The current form takes a single argument to resolve.

Node.js implements the ability to pass an optional second argument to `import.meta.resolve` for a custom parentURL to resolve against, allowing `import.meta.resolve` to act as a generic resolver in various contexts, which may be useful as a generic way to expose the global resolver.

I wanted to post this as a tracking bug for Deno to implement this along with https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/7296. Further discussion welcome.
author: guybedford

title: deno cache invalid after git clone
body:
I am using deno v1.23.2.

In order to run offline and have everything preloaded (also to be protected from vanishing dependencies) I am adding a project-local deno cache to version control.

In the CI Pipeline I am running:
```sh
DENO_DIR=.deno_cache deno test --allow-net --cached-only --check --lock=lock.json --no-prompt --unstable
```

Sadly my pipeline fails with `error: The source code is invalid, as it does not match the expected hash in the lock file.`.

Steps to reproduce:
```sh
DENO_DIR=.deno_cache deno cache --lock=lock.json --lock-write --unstable deps.ts
git add -A
git commit -m "my commit"
git push
``` 
```sh
mkdir new_dir
cd new_dir
git clone ...
DENO_DIR=.deno_cache deno test --allow-net --cached-only --check --lock=lock.json --no-prompt --unstable
```

Because I found this to also happen locally, it cannot be the difference between Ubuntu and Alpine Linux.\
A `deno cache --reload` does fix the problem, but this goes against doing things offline.

I also have a `.gitattributes` file containing `* text=auto eol=lf`.

And I compared the cache before and after doing a reload, and it was exactly the same (apart from the metadata files).

title: Runtime references deno-specific flag in error log
body:
When using `deno_runtime` and calling `execute_main_module`, the function will return an error if it is lacking permissions to access external IO, when printing this error it will look something like this:

```
Error: Requires write access to "./duplicates.txt", run again with the --allow-write flag
```

The issue with this is that the `--allow-write` flag is specific to Deno's CLI and thus might confuse users when `deno_runtime` is embedded in another application.

I've tried to work around this by using [`get_custom_error_class`](https://docs.rs/deno_core/0.142.0/deno_core/error/fn.get_custom_error_class.html) and checking if it is `Some("PermissionDenied")`, which it should be according to [the source code](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/runtime/permissions.rs#L71-L78). However this always returns `None`, despite the error being created through `custom_error`, so I'm not sure that function is working properly?

https://esm.sh/react-icons@4.4.0/fa?alias=react:preact/compat&deps=react.

```json
{
```

title: thread 'main' has overflowed its stack when file has over 250 nested if statements
body:
Hi.  I'm trying to get my library to support deno.

For reasons I don't understand, when I import my library in deno, it overflows the stack.  It's fine in the browser, though, or in node if you use dynamic import and change the extension.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/77482/177857375-e1fb0037-81d1-41a0-b3ea-81f55e0dbc00.png)

Also happens with direct binding (that is, no `* as`.)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/77482/177857761-fcbe29eb-471d-43b3-85aa-2613f8941107.png)

It's immediate and there are no apparent diagnostics.

I recognize that I might sound a little extreme when I say this, but I'm nervously concerned there might be a portability bug in deno.  If not, alternately, there might be a resource ceiling on the stack that is significantly lower than in browsers and node.  There are a number of other people claiming this is happening, and every single one of them is a Windows user.  See #13196, #11356, #9752, and #5848.

Particularly interestingly, #9752 appears to treat this as a bug that was fixed upstream seven months ago.

On the other hand, there's a ***solid*** chance that I'm doing it wrong and making some stupid mistake I just don't understand.

You can see a reproduction, if you're interested, by pulling the branch `ReattemptDenoSupport` from https://github.com/StoneCypher/jssm/tree/ReattemptDenoSupport and running `npm install && npm run build`, then opening `deno` and issuing `import * as jssm from "./dist/deno/jssm.deno-esm.js"`.

Sorry, I know it's not cool to put `npm` instructions in here.  I'm trying to fix it to be on `deno.land` and I'll update this as soon as I get what's going on.  It's hard to know if I'm using `deno.land` correctly when I haven't got my library working as expected.

How does someone go about diagnosing something like this?

author: panva

title: Deno should try to trigger GC when the OS is low on memory
body:
V8 provides the [`v8::Isolate::low_memory_notification`](https://docs.rs/v8/latest/v8/struct.Isolate.html#method.low_memory_notification) method, which is meant to let V8 know when the OS is running low on memory, so it can cause an aggressive, synchronous garbage collection. Currently Deno never calls this method, which can result in the OS killing the process when it's out of memory.

The question is when/how to know to call it.

- Node.js seems to call into this method [when an allocation in C++ fails](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/4d5ff25a813fd18939c9f76b17e36291e3ea15c3/src/util-inl.h#L354), before trying again. In Rust this is not easy to do, because failed allocations will panic by default; APIs like [`Box::try_new`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/boxed/struct.Box.html#method.try_new) can help, but they're still experimental, and we can't change every dependency to use them.
- Chrome [has a memory pressure monitor](https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:components/memory_pressure/) that seems to use OS signals to know the pressure level. It's not yet clear to me how (or whether) this signal ends up notifying V8, but I'm assuming that it does.
author: andreubotella

title: BroadcastChannel postMessage makes unnecessary copies of the serialized buffer
body:
The `postMessage` method (in workers, `MessagePort` and `BroadcastChannel`) first serializes the passed objects into an `ArrayBuffer` (using `Deno.core.serialize`), and then an op is called which sends it across the underlying communications channel. However, for all of these, the op which sends the buffer with the serialized data takes it as a `ZeroCopyBuf`, which (as its name indicates) doesn't create a copy of the data before calling the op, but these operations do copy the data in order to send it as an owned `Vec<u8>`. This ultimately results in such buffers having copies in the V8 heaps for both isolates.

However, since #14102, serde_v8 has the `DetachedBuffer` type, which takes ownership of an `ArrayBuffer` by detaching it from the original isolate, and which can be "serialized" to create a new `ArrayBuffer` in a different isolate with the same backing memory – that is, it allows transferring the buffer in the structured clone sense. We should use this for workers and `MessagePort`.

For `BroadcastChannel` it's more complicated because, for any other instances of `BroadcastChannel` with the same name in the current isolate, a task will be queued which deserializes the buffer, and then the buffer is sent to other isolates. So the buffer can't be detached until all such tasks have run. One solution for this might be to deserialize for each instance before queuing the corresponding taks.

body:
# Proposal
Support running valid TypeScript even with ".js" extension, just ignore the extension.  With upcoming Type Annotations proposal for JavaScript ".ts" files might now be indistinguishable with ".js" files. 

Also noticed that running a file without an extension or without an extension for that matter they are treated as JavaScript files. I understand that there might be performance reasons for this since there will be transpile step when enabling TypeScript by default. By just ignoring types as in the proposal there might be a performance gain.

title: Remove vary header from response
body:
I'm using a gateway and need my requests to be cached by the gateway, but std http is adding a `vary` header to the response which I need not to be there for the request to be cached.

On caching, from the Envoy docs: [envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_filters/cache_filter](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_filters/cache_filter)

> HTTP Cache respects Cache-Control directive from the upstream host. For example, if HTTP response returns status code 200 with Cache-Control: max-age=60 and no vary header, it will be cached.

Is there a way to remove `vary` header from the response?
author: aabedraba

title: Event loop middlewares returning true cause a live loop
body:
If a `deno_runtime` user wants to use the event loop middleware feature to keep Deno from exiting while waiting for eg. a message to come from a spawned thread, they must return `true` from the event loop middleware.

This will cause the Deno runtime to wake up the isolate to check for new tasks, which causes the event loop middlewares to be immediately re-polled. As a result, any event loop middleware returning `true` will essentially put Deno into a live loop with the process taking as much CPU processing power as it can get.

Additionally, this is somewhat at odds with the presumably intended use case of event loop middleware: If a middleware causes JS to be run on the isolate and that JS creates microtasks, then one might expect that the middleware should then return true (or some more explicit enum value) to signal that the isolate should be woken up and microtasks performed. What instead happens is that Deno's automatic microtasks policy will run any created microtasks as soon as the JS function run by the middleware returns, effectively making them just synchronous calls.

As such, event loop middleware are currently not a good solution to keeping Deno alive while some external task is being performed. One solution might be to enable event loop middleware to return a delay value, which would sleep the Deno process for the delay (of course presuming no one else returns a smaller delay, or returns an eager polling command) and re-poll event loop middlewares after the delay (possibly waking up the isolate as well).

Another solution might be to give ops easier access to a clonable Waker that could be used to manually wake up the isolate when or after performing external tasks. This would be more in line with the JS Promise / callback system, whereas the previous would be more like a ticking interval.

Whether or not either of these solutions is implemented, the microtasks policy also causes some confusion to these. A Waker waking up the isolate just for the purpose of creating a Promise that, once resolved on the next tick, will perform heavier calculations would unexpectedly find itself blocked by the heavier calculations as those would be performed synchronously when the Promise creating JS code returned. Similarly, an event loop middleware causing JS to be run on the isolate would find that any microtasks created by that JS would already be run as part of the call into the isolate, and explicitly telling Deno to execute microtasks eagerly after the middleware returned would be unnecessary.

body:
Steps to reproduce:
1. Check out the SheetJS Deno repo from https://github.com/SheetJS/nodeno/
2. Change the `read.ts` file to use `Deno.bench()` around the `XLSX.read()` call.
3. Try to run the bench using `deno bench`

Expected result:
1. Bench will work, as run did (well, there're some issues here with V8 crashing but lets not worry about the small stuff)

Actual result:
1. Bench fails with the following error:
```
deno bench --unstable --allow-read=./ --check --allow-env=NODE_DEBUG read.ts large_strings.xlsx.xml
error: The module's source code could not be parsed: Unexpected token `?`. Expected this, import, async, function, [ for array literal, { for object literal, @ for decorator, function, class, null, true, false, number, bigint, string, regexp, ` for template literal, (, or an identifier at file:///home/user/build/nodeno/large_strings.xlsx.xml:1:2
```

The `large_strings.xlsx.xml` file indeed starts like this:
```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
```
so the line 1, colon 2 for token "?" is very much correct.

It seems that bench is trying to read the `readFileSync` source file as JS.

Switching to `await Deno.readFile()` does not fix the issue.
author: aapoalas

title: Performance issues with importing 25K line file
body: Setup: ```bash $ echo -n > blank.mjs $ curl -LO https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.18.9/package/xlsx.mjs $ echo "import * as XLSX from './xlsx.mjs'; console.log(XLSX.version);" >> import.mjs ``` Baseline is comparable to node / bun: ``` $ hyperfine -w 3 'deno run blank.mjs' 'bun blank.mjs' 'node blank.mjs' ``` With the import, deno is unexpectedly slow: ``` $ hyperfine -w 3 'deno run import.mjs' 'bun import.mjs' 'node import.mjs' ```

title: Deno bundle doesn't rename declared identifiers since v1.23.1
body:
Since v1.23.1, I get the following error when I run the bundled file:

```
error: Uncaught SyntaxError: Identifier 'createCommonjsModule' has already been declared
function createCommonjsModule(fn, basedir, module) {
^
    at <anonymous> (file:///home/yxqiu/workspace/test_deno/test_deno.bundle.js:248:1)
```

I compare the bundled file produced by v1.23.0 and v1.23.1:

- in v1.23.0, multiple `createCommonjsModule` functions will be renamed to `createCommonjsModule1`, `createCommonjsModule2`, `createCommonjsModule3` and so on.
-  in v1.23.1, all `createCommonjsModule` functions will still be `createCommonjsModule` after bundled.

I guess the PR "fix: upgrade swc via deno_ast 0.16 (https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14930)" in v1.23.1 introduces this issue since it is the most related PR in v1.23.1.

author: slim-hmidi

title: Unexpected test failure without stack details
body:
I ran into a fairly specific bug where a Deno test will fail unexpectedly, then reproduce no details in its error message.

I wasn't able to reproduce the bug in a "simpler" way than I caused it, however, my test file & code can be found at [this](https://github.com/lydo-lang/lydo-bootstrap/blob/d2b417ac2c96243460bf1cbd18ad7148dc0bde85/tests/lexer.test.ts) commit. In this file, you'll notice two tests: `Lexer tokens` & `Lexer syntax err`. When running `deno test -A`, both of these pass as expected. However, when rearranging the file such that `Lexer syntax err` comes first and `Lexer tokens` comes second, the latter test fails without any stack details:
```
running 2 tests from file:///Users/hpaulson/Development/Lydo/lydo-bootstrap/tests/lexer.test.ts
test Lexer syntax err ... ok (4ms)
test Lexer tokens ... FAILED (3ms)

failures:

Lexer tokens


failures:

        Lexer tokens

test result: FAILED. 1 passed; 1 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (24ms)

error: Test failed
```

Of course, these tests should be absolutely independent of each other, and there shouldn't be a reason why switching their order would cause one to fail. In addition, I think this bug has something to do with the call to `lexer(src)` in the `Lexer tokens` test; When replacing this call with the actual array of tokens (which we expect to receive as an output from the function), the test passes as expected. Even if the function call isn't providing back the expected output (which likely isn't the case considering it works fine when the order of tests is swapped), the test should still send back some details about why it failed; Here it simply says it failed with no additional information. 

If there's any additional reproduction info I could provide that may be helpful, please let me know!

title: `deno vendor` causes an "Relative import path "path" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../" error but `deno run` does not
body:
If you create a file `test.js`:
```js
import * as parser from "https://esm.sh/typescript-parser@2.6.1?pin=v87";
```

And then run it with `deno run -A test.js`, it runs just fine. And even with `deno run -A --check test.js` it doesn't complain.
But if you use `deno vendor test.js` you'll get the following error:
```
Relative import path "path" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../
```

I suppose this could also be an issue with esm.sh, but it's hard to tell where things are going wrong because Deno doesn't actually tell which file/request is causing the error.

Fwiw, typescript-parser seems old and unmaintained, so if this issue is specific to typescript-parser I guess it won't be worth fixing. But I reckon this issue might pop up for other imports as well.

title: Deno namespace inconsistency

I propose to rename them to PascalCase and deprecate the old ones as they can be removed in v2.

title: `Deno.Command` kill ability permission requirement
body:
I ran a subprocess using Deno from within a process that had already been granted a specific run permission for Deno (`--allow-run=deno`), and then tried to kill the child process. I tried two ways: by providing an `AbortSignal` in the options, and by using [`Deno.Child#kill`](https://doc.deno.land/deno/unstable/~/Deno.Child#kill). I was met with this permission prompt both times:

```
⚠️  ️Deno requests run access to all. Run again with --allow-run to bypass this prompt.
   Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)]
```

I denied it (because I don't want to run the script with unlimited run permissions — I already granted the process name in advance), and an exception was thrown:

```
⚠️  ️Deno requests run access to all. Run again with --allow-run to bypass this prompt.
   Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)]  n
error: Uncaught (in promise) PermissionDenied: Requires run access to all, run again with the --allow-run flag
  ac.abort();
     ^
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:170:12)
    at Child.kill (deno:runtime/js/40_spawn.js:172:12)
    at onAbort (deno:runtime/js/40_spawn.js:128:34)
    at AbortSignal.[[[signalAbort]]] (deno:ext/web/03_abort_signal.js:80:11)
    at AbortController.abort (deno:ext/web/03_abort_signal.js:159:32)
    at file:///...
```

I haven't followed the stack trace into the built-ins and core yet, but I was quite surprised to see this. Is it a requirement to grant blanket run permission to **all** processes in order to kill **any** child process using the `spawn`/`spawnChild` API? If so, why? And can this be changed before stabilization?

author: jespertheend

title: Inspector doesn't break on rejected top level await
body:
**To reproduce**
- Create `test.js`:
```js
await (async () => {
	await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 1));
	throw new Error("oh no");
})()
```
- run `deno run --inspect-brk test.js`
- attach a debugger and enable 'break on uncaught exceptions'
- resume the debugger

I tested this with both the builtin debugger from vscode as well as the debugger from `chrome://inspect`

Note that if you remove the first `await` the debugger pauses as expected, removing the `setTimeout` also solves the issue.

A workaround is to also enable 'pause on caught' exceptions, but this often makes the debugger break on exceptions internal to Deno, which is not ideal.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2737650/179400842-f5b2c7c5-3fc4-44cc-8c7f-6dc716d789eb.mp4

author: elycheikhsmail

title: Provide better experience for using lodash in TypeScript
body:
In lots of TypeScript interface .d.ts files around the internet, you see this sort of pattern:

```typescript
import _ = require("../index");
```

Unfortunately, Deno is unable to read these because it requires file extensions on imported modules (it expects to see `../index.d.ts`). This means that lots of typings are unusable in Deno, while perfectly good in most other engines.

Because the typings for lodash do this, I had an incredibly hard time finding a way to include lodash with correct typings in Deno. Since lodash is such a popular library, I was shocked to see how difficult this was.

One might expect this to work:
```typescript
// @deno-types="https://esm.sh/@types/lodash@4.14.182/index.d.ts?target=deno"
import lodash from "https://esm.sh/lodash@4.17.21/lodash.js?target=deno";
```

But it will simply confuse the engine and give an error:
```
error: Import 'https://esm.sh/@types/lodash@4.14.182/index' failed: 500 Internal Server Error
    at https://esm.sh/@types/lodash@4.14.182/common/number.d.ts:1:20
```

Using lodash without typings severely limits its usefulness, because the compiler won't understand things like the `isNil` type guard.

## Failed strategies

* Use https://deno.land/x/lodash
  * Works, but no typings
  * Note: This option is displayed prominently on the deno.land/x homepage, but it provides no guidance on how to use it with Deno at all
* Use https://deno.land/x/deno_lodash
  * Works, but no typings
* Use `@deno-types` from esm.sh (with or without `?target=deno` options): https://esm.sh/@types/lodash/index.d.ts
  * Error reading types
* Use `@deno-types` from skypack (without options): https://cdn.skypack.dev/@types/lodash/index.d.ts
  * Error reading types

## Successful strategies

* Use `@deno-types` from skypack (with `?dts` options):
```typescript
// @deno-types="https://cdn.skypack.dev/@types/lodash?dts"
import lodash from "https://cdn.skypack.dev/lodash-es";
```
* Use https://deno.land/x/deno_ts_lodash
  * Note: This repo appears to be unmaintained and hasn't been touched in over a year
author: stabai

title: `deno task` with no args should run first task
body: A useful feature of `make` is that it builds the first target when you don't specify any. `deno task` should do the same. This should typically be the `start`/`serve` task. Currently `deno task` lists available tasks, I think we should move that to `deno task -l`.

author: kitsonk

title: `task --cwd` differs from `cd` when directory contains deno.json
body:
I was thinking `deno task --cwd=<DIR>` (#14820) would behave like `make -C DIR`. That is, change to DIR before doing anything. One use is that it's a convenient way to stack independent recipes where you have one deno.json in the root and then submodules with their own deno.json. Deno's task runner currently only ever uses the root deno.json.

Consider the following:

```bash
cat > deno.json <<eof
{
  "tasks": {
    // Go into a subdirectory and ask it to build itself.
    "cd:build": "cd submodule && deno task cd:build",
    // Go into a subdirectory and then run this command there.
    "cwd:build": "deno task --cwd submodule cwd:build"
  }
}
eof

mkdir submodule
cat > submodule/deno.json <<eof
{
  "tasks": {
    "cd:build": "echo cd:build submodule",
    "cwd:build": "echo cwd:build submodule"
  }
}
eof
```

Now run the `cd` version which works as expected:

```
$ deno task cd:build
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
Task cd:build cd submodule && deno task cd:build
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
Task cd:build echo cd:build submodule
cd:build submodule
```

But the `--cwd` version tries to use the root config unexpectedly:

```
$ deno task cwd:build
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
Task cwd:build deno task --cwd submodule cwd:build
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
Task cwd:build deno task --cwd submodule cwd:build
Warning deno task is unstable and may drastically change in the future
error: No such file or directory (os error 2)
```

Very, very excited to have this task runner <3

title: `deno coverage` - store source text and emit in output directory?

title: "deno bench" should run empty function to measure baseline before running user benches
body:
Hello,

I've a behavior in Deno.bench that I cannot explain : 
```ts
Deno.bench("noop", () => {});
Deno.bench("noop", () => {});
Deno.bench("noop", () => {});
```

```shell
❯ deno bench --unstable noop.ts
cpu: Apple M1
runtime: deno 1.23.3 (aarch64-apple-darwin)

benchmark      time (avg)             (min … max)       p75       p99      p995
------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------
noop       490.06 ps/iter   (466.6 ps … 47.63 ns)  479.2 ps  516.7 ps  529.1 ps
noop         4.29 ns/iter    (4.12 ns … 32.86 ns)   4.22 ns   4.67 ns   5.38 ns
noop         4.22 ns/iter    (4.12 ns … 33.54 ns)   4.23 ns   4.58 ns   4.79 ns
```

Easy to say that second and third results are close and are explained by CPU activity, but why the first is ~10x faster ?
Maybe something like "reset" job between them, that are not executed for the first, but, in this case, is it possible to execute this "reset" before the first job too, to be consistent ? Or just don't monitor them ?

Best regards,
Gaël
author: pand-app

title: Deno runs out of file descriptors when put under heavy load
body:
This issue is the follow up to #15124.

We are currently developing a server benchmark for various languages and runtimes. While developing our stress-tester we discovered a bug / possible improvement in Deno 1.24.0.

When putting heavy load on Deno (steps to reproduce follow) it throws:
```
error: Uncaught (in promise) Error: Too many open files (os error 24)
  for await (const conn of listener) {
                   ^
    at async Listener.next (deno:ext/net/01_net.js:214:16)
    at async start (file:///Users/user/drafts/server-language-benchmark/Deno/src/server.ts:37:20)
    at async file:///Users/user/drafts/server-language-benchmark/Deno/mod.ts:3:1
```

## Analysis
I'm not very familiar with the Deno codebase, but I tried to pinpoint the error. When printing the Resource Ids [(which probably somewhat map to the file descriptor number on Unix (like) systems)](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/72199303d899b8ddf2ff46ed11bd513ed9cc47e2/core/resources.rs#L94) I observed that they only increase during the test run. This indicates that the resource id numbers are not reused, which, if they in fact directly map to file descriptor numbers, **means that dead connections might not be closed fast enough**. I'm pretty sure that these connections have been shut down, since the client only maintains a maximum of 64 concurrent connections on my system, dropping old ones.

The issue might be somewhere hidden in the TCP stack, although I'm definitely not certain. Note that we did some unholy things with TCP in order to put such a high load on the server (https://github.com/umgefahren/server-language-client/blob/bc5eaf085f399cb76a4aa1e796696c5fd274c360/src/worker.rs#L101). I.e. we flushed, set the lingering time to 1ms and then immediately shut down the connection. 

## Instructions to reproduce

The Deno server code we ran can be found here: https://github.com/umgefahren/server-language-benchmark/tree/main/Deno

The code of the client can be found here: https://github.com/umgefahren/server-language-client

Steps to reproduce the issue with the client:

Build the client (in release mode) cargo build --release
Generate data with ./target/release/server-language-client generate 10000000
Run the benchmark (after starting the deno server) ./target/release/server-language-client benchmark 1m

title: deno server memory is larger than nodejs
body:
My team has been using Deno for more than a year. But I found that the memory consumption in docker container is relatively large, which is more than twice that of nodejs with the same function. 

This is frustrating, but strangely, if I use Deno inside the program `Deno.memoryUsage()` is even better than nodejs' process which used `process.memoryUsage()`. The value is smaller, but it is another case in docker stats.

[Here](https://github.com/jiawei397/deno_node_docker) is the test sample of `docker` I used, I use the origin nodejs http and origin Deno http test, and also test koa and oak.

The console logs:
```systemverilog
deno_1   | heapTotal 3.53MB，heapUsed 3.07MB，rss 3.70MB，external:0.00MB
oakjs_1  | heapTotal 5.42MB，heapUsed 3.95MB，rss 4.74MB，external:0.00MB
oak_1    | heapTotal 6.78MB，heapUsed 5.23MB，rss 6.27MB，external:0.00MB
koa_1    | heapTotal 6.93MB，heapUsed 5.50MB，rss 24.12MB，external:0.78MB
node_1   | heapTotal 4.92MB，heapUsed 4.22MB，rss 18.33MB，external:0.52MB
```
But docker stats：
```systemverilog
        CONTAINER           CPU %               MEM USAGE / LIMIT       MEM %               NET I/O             BLOCK I/O           PIDS
deno	c55528ee5171        0.00%               9.355 MiB / 3.858 GiB   0.24%               2.27 kB / 656 B     0 B / 0 B           5
oakjs   a1074c23261b        0.00%               18.3 MiB / 3.858 GiB    0.46%               1.88 kB / 656 B     0 B / 0 B           5
oak 	efafc26ffd90        0.00%               24.75 MiB / 3.858 GiB   0.63%               1.88 kB / 656 B     0 B / 0 B           5
koa   	78b030ee5801        0.00%               9.227 MiB / 3.858 GiB   0.23%               1.97 kB / 656 B     0 B / 0 B           7
node	ae1d64819c6f        0.00%               6.715 MiB / 3.858 GiB   0.17%               3.1 kB / 656 B      0 B / 0 B           7
```
The oak bundled js seems to be better than oak which started on typescript.

The following are my two actual Deno projects, which is also the reason why I did this test.
The console logs:
```systemverilog
project1: heapTotal 50.32MB，heapUsed 47.62MB，rss 49.13MB，external:22.68MB
project2: heapTotal 9.14MB， heapUsed 8.04MB， rss 8.63MB， external:1.16MB
```
docker stats:
```systemverilog
          CONTAINER           CPU %               MEM USAGE / LIMIT       MEM %               NET I/O             BLOCK I/O           PIDS
project1  43144c7cdcb4        0.03%               272.2 MiB / 3.858 GiB   6.89%               51.1 kB / 21.9 kB   0 B / 0 B           5
```

The above tests were conducted in the docker environment of Linux,  nodejs image is **node:16-alpine**  and deno image is **denoland/deno:alpine-1.23.2**：
```systemverilog

Operating System: CentOS Linux 7 (Core)
OSType: linux
Architecture: x86_64
Number of Docker Hooks: 3
CPUs: 4
```
It's very comfortable to develop with Deno. I personally like it better than nodejs, but in the actual operation process, the memory occupation is embarrassing. I hope to solve this problem. I believe that the bottom layer is the deno of Rust, In this regard, it should be the advantage, not the opposite.

title: [ext/ffi] non-deterministic `getCString` on `JSON.stringify()`
body:
- Deno: `1.24.0` (and `1.23.4`)
- Arch: `aarch64-apple-darwin`

I am trying to:
- stringify some JSON
- encode into a Uint8Array
- Get the pointer to the value
- Create a view of the pointer
- Read the string from the pointer

Code example:

```ts
const foo = { a: "b" }

const serialized = new TextEncoder().encode(JSON.stringify(foo))

const ptr = Deno.UnsafePointer.of(serialized)
const view = new Deno.UnsafePointerView(ptr)

console.log(serialized)
console.log(view.getCString())
```

Sometimes this results in the correct output:

```console
tmp λ deno run --allow-ffi --unstable mod.ts
Uint8Array(9) [
  123, 34, 97, 34,
   58, 34, 98, 34,
  125
]
{"a":"b"}
```

However, sometimes it results in this output:

```console
tmp λ deno run --allow-ffi --unstable mod.ts
Uint8Array(9) [
  123, 34, 97, 34,
   58, 34, 98, 34,
  125
]
Error 'invalid utf-8 sequence of 1 bytes from index 10' contains boxed error of unknown type:
  Utf8Error { valid_up_to: 10, error_len: Some(1) }
error: Uncaught Error: invalid utf-8 sequence of 1 bytes from index 10
console.log(view.getCString())
                 ^
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:170:12)
    at UnsafePointerView.getCString (deno:ext/ffi/00_ffi.js:103:19)
    at file:///tmp/mod.ts:9:18
```

This happened more to me with Deno version `1.23.3` and quite a bit less on `1.24.0`, it is still occuring however.

On Deno `1.23.3` this also sometimes happened:

```console
tmp λ /opt/homebrew/bin/deno run --allow-ffi --unstable mod.ts
Uint8Array(9) [
  123, 34, 97, 34,
   58, 34, 98, 34,
  125
]
{"a":"b"}+ // notice the +, can be any char, here
```

~~but this seems to be resolved in 1.24.0.~~ 
(Tested within my actual project it did occur, random chars at the end, just does not really occur in the example project on `1.24.0`)

Is something wrong in my code, is the detection for end-of-string wrong in Deno or something else?

edit:

The issue seems to be related only to `JSON.stringify` and not when calling `encode()` on a normal string.
author: berendsliedrecht

title: `-q` option position is inconsistent for the `task` subcommand
body:
There is a tiny consistency bug in the CLI's `-q` option position for the `task` subcommand specifically. The Deno CLI itself (`deno [OPTIONS] [SUBCOMMAND]`) supports `-q` for most (all?) subcommands except `task`.

Consider the following:

```
$ echo '{"tasks": {"echo": "echo"}}' > deno.json
$ deno -q task echo
Task not found: task
Available tasks:
- echo
    echo
```

Instead, the `-q` option must appear _after_ the subcommand:

```
$ deno task -q echo

```

This is inconsistent with most other subcommands and the the CLI help documentation. Eg, `fmt` works as expected:

```
$ deno -q fmt
```

The expected supported format is always `deno [OPTIONS] [SUBCOMMAND]`. `deno -q task`, in this case.
author: niedzielski

title: Feature (ext/ffi): Support non-uniform function pointer sizes
body:
As pointed out by @Phosra in [this comment](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15173#discussion_r922778747), it is not guaranteed that function pointers are always size-wise interchangeable with data pointers.

Currently the FFI layer presumes both function and data pointers to always be `usize` sized. This presumption also goes deep, the Rust libffi crate does not offer a separate type for function pointers and as such there might be a need to manually convert function pointers into appropriate integer sizes.

In any case, this probably needs a bit of work to support.
author: aapoalas

title: `JsRuntime::create_realm` should be a static method
body:
The `JsRuntime::create_realm` method, introduced, in denoland/deno#14019, made it possible to create V8 contexts that had the same set of extensions and ops loaded, and it has since been used to write tests for `deno_core`'s realm support. However, this method takes a `&mut self`, so it can only be called from code embedding the Deno runtime, as opposed to from the runtime itself.

Given that the whole intention of having `deno_core` support realms was to make it possible to implement the upcoming `ShadowRealm` TC39 proposal (see denoland/deno_core#911), it must be possible to create such a realm from a callback passed to V8.

Making this method static would require making `JsRuntime::init_extension_js` and `JsRuntime::init_cbs` static as well, plus probably moving the `extension` field from `JsRuntime` to `JsRuntimeState`.

author: GJZwiers

title: Require permissions for time zone and other time-related metadata
body:
Currently, there is a popular npm library that displays a protest message if it detects that it is being executed in a time zone that overlaps with Russia. This has caused many downstream projects to move away from said library.  (As it turns out, many non-Russian humans happen to live in Russian-overlapping-time zones.)

One high-level observation that we can make about this story is that it would be nice to live in a world where our npm dependencies can't hot-patch in features that spy on our geographic location. (In the exact same way that our npm dependencies should not be able to hot-patch in features that arbitrarily use the file-system or arbitrarily access the network.)

My first thought upon hearing this story was:
"If we lived in a world where everyone was using Deno instead of Node/npm, then this would be a non-issue, due to the awesome permissions model."

And my second thought upon hearing the story was to actually look into the specifics. It turns out that by default, Deno allows retrieval of the time zone without any specifically required permissions, which left me surprised and disappointed.

Has this topic been previously discussed? I searched through the GitHub issues, but I didn't find anything relevant.

I think that everyone can probably agree that "time zone" should be squarely placed in a separate category than "personally identifiable information". Yet, with the previous story in mind, it still seems desirable for Deno to be able to protect the former.
author: Zamiell

title: fmt: also sort import and export specifiers
body:
Currently, Deno only sorts the imported and exported names from import and export specifiers. The goal is to also sort the import and export specifiers in a certain sort function.

Usually, I sort those specifiers in Node in this way,
```ts
import 'style.css' // might be side effects module if JS
import fs from 'fs' // default import
import * as path from 'path' // namespace import
import { ref } from 'vue' // normal named import

// typed imports
import type TypeC from 'abc'
import type * as TypeC from 'abc'
import type { TypeC } from 'abc'
```
Exports are also sorted in that way as well.

For Deno, I am thinking to sort like,
```ts
import 'style.css' // might be side effects module if JS
import fs from 'fs' // default import
import * as path from 'path' // namespace import
import { ref } from 'vue' // normal named import

// url imports go into one section
import fs from 'https://deno.land/std/node/fs.ts'
/// ... the rest of imports

// typed imports
import type TypeC from 'abc'
import type * as TypeC from 'abc'
import type { TypeC } from 'abc'

// type imports from url here
import type { Colors } from 'https://deno.land/std/colors.ts'
```

I have checked that `dprint` can sort the import and export specifiers. But, Deno hasn't enabled them. I am not familiar with the code base, so I will let the core team decide the preferred way of sorting.

Some unresolved questions are how should `deno fmt` handle side effect imports? Should they be left as it is and let the users sort (since they will become one section after other imports are sorted)?

Thanks!

title: This condition will always return 'false' since the types 'string | null' and 'number' have no overlap.deno-ts(2367)
body:
Typescript says there is no overlap here, but that is wrong. `nationID` is a number and getItem will return a string or null. If it returns a string then `'4' == 4` would be true in javascript while `'4' === 4` would be false. Deno shouldn't be complaining about me trying to do the below.
```js
localStorage.getItem('id') == nationID
```

author: iugo

title: Add option for recursive directory permissions
body:
Right now, the permission system seems very strongly based on always interactively querying for permissions, forcing users to agree to the specific resource that is accessed.

This approach is somewhat problematic when dealing with systems where the user is expected to execute the same module very frequently, causing them to be constantly re-prompted for these permissions. This becomes especially annoying when a lot of files are accessed, which would likely just drive people to use the permission system in a less granular way (just enable all read/write).

I think most people would expect that a request to access `./` for example would give them access to the files inside that directory, however right now that isn't the case. So my proposal is to allow this to work so permissions can be prompted for directories, allowing all children recursively. This would likely help with excessive permission requests and make requesting permissions ahead of time easier.
author: cd-work

title: compat(node/https): ClientRequest.setTimeout is not a function
body:
**Describe the bug**

ClientRequest.setTimeout in node/https erroring and saying that it is not a function, which is incompatible with node's https module and will break projects which use the compat mode.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Create a file with contents
```js
const https = require('https');
const request = https.request({method: 'GET', host: 'discord.com', path: 'https://discord.com/api/v10/gateway', headers: {"User-Agent": "DiscordBot"}});
request.setTimeout(10000);
request.end();
request.on('response', response => {
  response.on('data', data=>console.log(data.toString()));
  response.on('end', ()=>console.log('ok'));
});
```
2. Run it using the Deno compat mode(it uses the std modules for node defaults)
```js
>deno run -A --compat --unstable httpsTest.js
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: request.setTimeout is not a function
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///D:/usb/compatTest/httpsTest.js:3:9)
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///D:/usb/compatTest/httpsTest.js:9:4)
    at Module._compile (https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/node/module.ts:260:36)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/node/module.ts:1332:10)
    at Module.load (https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/node/module.ts:239:34)
    at Function._load (https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/node/module.ts:558:14)
    at loadCjsModule ([deno:cli\compat\mod.rs:119:30]:3:22)
```

**Expected behavior**

```js
>node httpsTest.js
{"url": "wss://gateway.discord.gg"}
ok
```
The code should return this, as on node.

**Environment**

- OS: Windows 10
- deno version: 1.24.0
- std version: 0.149.0

```bash
deno install -A --import-map=https://deno.land/x/wallpal@1.0.0/import_map.json https://deno.land/x/wallpal@1.0.0/wallpal.ts
```


This project has an import map that looks like this:
```json
{
  "imports": {
    "std/fs": "https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/fs/mod.ts",
    "std/io": "https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/io/mod.ts",
```

and imports that look like

It seems that deno is attempting to resolve these imports as if they are local to the wallpal module. These import maps work fine locally, but they fail with `deno install`, so I am inclined to believe there is a bug.

title: lsp: completions in jsx props, the value is set to `"$1"` or `{$1}`
body:
**Describe the bug**

When autocompleting props in jsx the prop is set to have the value;
- for props which accept functions `{$1}` 
- and for props which accept strings `"$1"`.

Note: This consistently seems to occur for props which accept a function or string value, but not for props which accept a boolean value.

**To Reproduce**

1. Create a `tsx` file (extension ends in `.tsx`
2. Either import React -- or set a custom jsx factory and import that
4. Write some jsx
5. In the jsx start writing a property which accepts a string or function value and use the autocomplete.

**Expected behaviour**

The prop is set to have the value;
- for props which accept functions `{}` 
- and for props which accept strings `""`

**Current behaviour**

The prop is set to have the value;
- for props which accept functions `{$1}` 
- and for props which accept strings `"$1"`

![jsx-props-autocomplete-repro](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/848525/182016002-d7a3faeb-dc0f-4d01-bec7-b0ef64718205.gif)

author: outloudvi

title: Have a unique pause reason for `--inspect-brk`
body:
This weekend I was looking at making Deno nicer to use in the VS Code debugger.

One initial challenge is that we'd want to run Deno programs with `--inspect-brk`. However, this then causes the debugger to start paused in an arbitrary location. We do have an option for `continueOnAttach`, which works for Node, but not for Deno. This is because Node has a unique pause `reason`, ["Break on start"](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-js-debug/blob/95d772b31e87ed4069e0d9242f424925792e96e9/src/adapter/threads.ts#L787-L789), that we use to determine when the pause is a result of `--inspect-brk`. 

It would be helpful if Deno similarly had a unique `reason` as a result of `--inspect-brk`. Currently, Deno simply calls `break_on_next_statement`

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2703996dea73c496d79fcedf165886a1659622d1/core/inspector.rs#L317-L320

which pauses with a hardcoded `debugCommand` reason.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2703996dea73c496d79fcedf165886a1659622d1/core/inspector.rs#L571

Perhaps there could be a `break_on_next_statement_with_reason` method to give us a unique reason?

title: TZ environment variable not respected in PowerShell
body:
Setting the `TZ` variable on Windows PowerShell does not change the default timezone of Deno

### With Deno
```ts
// test.ts
console.log(Deno.env.get(`TZ`))
console.log(new Date().toString())
```
```ps
PS > $env:TZ='UTC'; deno run --allow-env test.ts; $env:TZ='';
UTC
Mon Aug 01 2022 01:57:00 GMT+0800 (Philippine Standard Time)
```

title: Imports issue with paths that include `#`
body: It appears some URL escaping is done even if the given file path is not in URL form (`#` along with a handful of other characters are valid across Linux/Mac/Windows filepaths) To Reproduce: - create a folder with `#` in the name - try to import a file from that folder (both import statements and import calls) Current Workaround: - escape the `#` with `%23` just like it would be in a URL import - more generically `await import(encodeURIComponent(THE_PATH).replace(/%2F/g,'/'))`

We should better determine which root to auto detect a configuration file, by enumerating the workspace folders checking their enablement and seeing if there is a config file, until one is found.

author: renhiyama

title: "Deno.upgradeHttp" API needs to be rewritten for Node compat
body:
Required for https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/2061

Node emits "upgrade" event with "request", "socket" and "head" arguments, when a request is received that has `Connection: upgrade` header. `Deno.upgradeHttp` was meant to be a solution to polyfill that Node API, however the semantics of this API are strictly related to how upgrades work in `hyper`. `Deno.upgradeHttp` resolves a promise only after a response is sent back (again, mandated by `hyper`), but in case of Node's API it is callers responsibility in write the response manually. 

Because of that we are unable to polyfill Node's server API which is a (most likely) the last blocker for Vite compatibility https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/2055 We need to change `Deno.upgradeHttp` in such a way that it will verify that passed request is actually an upgrade request and yield the request back, along with the underlying connection and first packet of data (could be empty).

CC @littledivy

title: Add a flag for allowing requests to fail during `deno vendor`.
body:
#15391 makes Deno error when a dynamic import fails to load. This causes no files to get vendored.
However, there might be cases where vendoring modules is still desirable, even when some files fail to load.
Would it be possible to add a flag to `deno vendor` to continue vendoring even though a request fails?

My current use case is that I'm trying to download https://esm.sh/rollup@2.75.7?pin=v87&target=es2022 to disk, so that it can be loaded from a local server during development. At the moment this fails because one of its dynamic imports responds with a 500 status code, but  the vendored module still load fine in a browser even though some files are missing.

Admittedly this might be a bit out of scope. I suppose `deno vendor` wasn't really intended for this use case, and maybe esm.sh is at fault for responding with a 500 status code.
But I reckon there might be other instances where an invalid status code is returned that are not exclusive to this use case.

title: deno inspect with docker container - port forwarding
body:
I am trying to inspect a deno app that is ran inside a docker container with docker-compose.

docker-compose configuration is as follows:

```
services:
  api_bo:
    image: denoland/deno:debian-1.23.4
    volumes:
      - type: bind
        source: .
        target: /usr/src
    ports:
      - 9229:9229
      - 6005:3000
    command: bash -c "cd /usr/src/packages/api_bo && deno task inspect"
    depends_on:
      - mongo_db
    environment:
      - MONGO_URL=mongodb://mongo_db:27017/academy_db
      - DB_NAME=academy_db
      - PORT=3000
```

deno.json is as follows:

```
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "allowJs": false,
    "strict": true
  },
  "lint": {
    "files": {
      "include": ["src/"],
      "exclude": ["src/types.ts"]
    },
    "rules": {
      "tags": ["recommended"],
      "include": [
        "ban-untagged-todo",
        "no-explicit-any",
        "no-implicit-any",
        "explicit-function-return-type"
      ],
      "exclude": ["no-debugger", "no-console"]
    }
  },
  "tasks": {
    "start": "deno run -A --watch src/app.ts",
    "inspect": "deno run -A --inspect src/app.ts"
  },
  "importMap": "../../import_map.json"
}
```
Chrome does not detect the running process.

When running out of docker with deno run, it works just fine.

It seems that deno only listens to connections from `0.0.0.0` and thus it cannot be inspected using docker port forwarding.

author: jonasfrey

title: WebSocket: Ping and Pong
body:
Hi, Would it be possible to include the methods `.ping()` and `.pong()` on Deno's Web Socket implementation? It's noted that while browsers do not support sending these control frames, they can be a server side requirement if deploying WebSocket servers behind load balanced environments. 

Infrastructure such as AWS's Application / Network Load Balancer (as well as many other load balancers) will automatically terminate idle connections. Applications can mitigate LB termination by periodically sending `ping()` control frames down to each connected client. Because `ping` frames are sent out of band from the sockets messaging channel, server applications can keep their connection keep alive logic and application protocol logic separate (with the added benefit of browser Web Sockets not seeing `ping` control frames at all)

Without this functionality, it does raise quite a few challenges porting existing application protocols to run on Deno. This is due to applications often omitting ping control frames in the applications protocol itself (as it's generally understood that these control frames are already provided as part of the RFC6455 specification)

### RFC

https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6455#section-5.5.2
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6455#section-5.5.3

### Rust

https://docs.rs/ws/latest/ws/struct.Frame.html#method.ping
https://docs.rs/ws/latest/ws/struct.Frame.html#method.pong

### Node

https://github.com/websockets/ws/blob/master/lib/sender.js#L209
https://github.com/websockets/ws/blob/master/lib/sender.js#L252

Would it be possible to include these methods?

title: `deno test` errors when using project root for absolute imports
body:
Steps to reproduce:

1. Create file `a_test.ts` containing

```ts
import { assertEquals } from "https://deno.land/std@0.151.0/testing/asserts.ts";

Deno.test("test1", () => {
  assertEquals(1, 1);
});
```

2. Create file `imports.json` containing

```json
{
    "imports": {}
}
```

3. Run `deno test --import-map=imports.json`. Observe that the test passes:

```
> deno test --import-map=imports.json
running 1 test from ./a_test.ts
test1 ... ok (7ms)

ok | 1 passed | 0 failed (46ms)
```

5. Update `imports.json` to use [project root for absolute imports](https://deno.land/manual/linking_to_external_code/import_maps#:~:text=To%20use%20your%20project%20root%20for%20absolute%20imports):

```json
{
    "imports": {
        "/": "./"
    }
}
```

6. Run `deno test --import-map=imports.json` again. Observe error:

```
> deno test --import-map=imports.json

ok | 0 passed | 0 failed (27ms)

error: Module not found "file:///C:/path/to/repo/a_test.ts".
```

Version information:

```
> deno --version
deno 1.24.2 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 10.4.132.20
typescript 4.7.4
```
author: mentoc3000

title: `deno_core::resolve_import` is CWD dependant
body: `deno_core::resolve_import("", "<unknown>")` causes an `std::fs::cwd` call to be made. This is highly unexpected and possibly a hazard, considering that `deno_core` is otherwise completely oblivious to the underlying module loading system. As far as I am aware this cwd can not leak into the isolate, so the hazard is restricted to only Rust embedders. Regardless, `resolve_import` should not treat `<unknown>` as a special base URL that results in a `std::fs::cwd` call.
