author: masx200

title: Proposal: deprecate `deno bundle`, add `deno pack`
body:
## Context

The _intention_ of `deno bundle` is to generate a single file redistributable of code for Deno which makes it easy run a Deno workload without having to fetch dependencies, transpile TypeScript, or use `deno compile`.

There has been confusion in the community that `deno bundle` is a general purpose bundler, and people are then disappointed that it only does the thing it was designed to do.

When `deno bundle` was introduced, it used the built in TypeScript compiler (`tsc`) to emit a set of AMD modules where a minimalist AMD loader was prepended to the file to allow the modules to be loaded.  This worked effectively, until top level await became a thing and AMD stating that they wouldn't ever be able to support that as part of the standard.  This lead to a migration to SystemJS and again a minimalistic loader.  During that period we adopted swc to start doing the emit of TypeScript in some situations and swc created bundler.

We migrated to the swc bundler and had loads of issues where we work collaboratively with swc to resolve, but still bundling with swc has been a source of several issues, often hard to chase down, and hard to fix upstream, as well as a source of breaking changes in the Rust API which has caused needs for us to refactor.  Using swc to transform and emit has not been as problematic, and been easy for us to stay current with releases.

That being said, merging ES modules into a single context, but trying to determine what symbols need to be renamed and changed while not affecting runtime behaviour is a daunting task, one that is really hard.  So it is arguable that such an approach to "bundling" isn't such a good idea.  The advantages of AMD and SystemJS is that the preserve the module scope.  Specifically SystemJS was designed to be able to move an ES module into SystemJS and have its runtime behaviour preserved.

## Proposal

We would deprecate `deno bundle` in the next minor release of Deno and remove it in the release after. We would introduce `deno pack` in the same minor release.

`deno pack` would use swc to emit SystemJS modules into a single file, prepended with a minimalistic SystemJS loader, and support both inline and external source maps.  While this doesn't provide features like tree shaking, it provides a far more reliable way to do what is intended, generate a single file JavaScript file with no external dependencies.

We can investigate code minification at some point in the future to help reduce the size of the emitted file.

### Alternatives considered

- Just keep `deno compile` - there are limitations to this, mainly that you have to not only redistribute the code, but you also have to redistribute the binary as the author plus also decide all my target architectures. It also precludes downstream derivative work as well, where people would _pack_ one or more of their libraries and the composite them in a final script.
- Generate external `eszip` bundles, which is the format that `deno compile` uses and what Deno Deploy uses. `eszip` was never designed to be "exposed" and always for internal consumption.  It is not loadable by any runtime directly, and would require a lot of work to try to integrate an external file format seemlessly.  Emitting somethings that is a pure ES module/file is desirable in this situation.
- Just make people use `deno vendor` - This is potentially fine for end users but doesn't make it easy to redistribute workloads. `deno vendor` solves a different set of problems.
- Integrate `esbuild` directly into Deno. This is probably overkill for a lot of people, as not everyone uses a general purpose web bundler, plus the need to provide configuration and options.  `esbuild` supports Deno as a runtime, and we should just continue to encourage people who need a general purpose web bundle/build tool to use `esbuild` or other solutions.

title: bug: `--watch --reload` will reload on every file change
body:
Running `--reload` with `--watch` will reload on every file change, which is probably not desirable.
Example `./file.ts`:
```ts
#!/usr/bin/env -S deno run --allow-net --watch --reload
import { serve } from "https://deno.land/std/http/mod.ts"

serve(() => new Response("hi"), { port: 8080 });
```
Edit the file or just save it and deno will reload all dependencies

author: kidonng

title: lsp: better handling of non-actionable error messages
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The Deno extension shows a lot of error notifications, more than any of the extensions I'm currently using. Some examples include:
- When a test fails. This is fine if an error occurs that causes the tests to not run properly, but a notification is also shown when the testing ui already shows the failing tests.
- https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/issues/591 (though now fixed)
- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15400
- https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/issues/609
- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/25999

This leads to frustration because these notifications don't work like toasts, so you have to manually dismiss them.
This also takes attention away from notifications that actually do matter.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

The solution here is probably less trivial than I expect it to be. Ideally there would be different settings for different kind of notifications. So the user can choose which notifications to subscribe to.
But if this is not feasible, a single option to suppress all errors would be helpful as well I think.

author: GJZwiers

title: Provide `deno check` as runtime API
body:
[`Deno.emit`](https://doc.deno.land/deno/unstable@v1.21.3/~/Deno.emit) has [come](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/8799) and [gone](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14463), and plenty of [suggestions can be found pointing toward using `esbuild`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues?q=is%3Aissue+esbuild) to _transpile_ TypeScript source code at runtime, but there is now a lack of a runtime API for type-checking.

Previously, with `Deno.emit`, [type-related diagnostics](https://doc.deno.land/deno/unstable@v1.21.3/~/Deno.Diagnostic) were available for determining if transpiled source code had type-related problems. Now, there is no way to know if the transpiled source is unsound from a type system perspective.

It would be very useful for Deno to provide runtime API access to its type-checking functionality and diagnostics. It's not possible to use `tsc` or tools in its ecosystem for this purpose (primarily because Deno's import specifier syntax is incompatible).

I believe this should be provided directly from the runtime on the `Deno` namespace (instead of as an external module like [`https://github.com/denoland/deno_emit`](https://github.com/denoland/deno_emit)) because the modified version of the compiler that is needed for this task is already so tightly-integrated. Deno saliently advertises TypeScript integration as one of its core features ([home page](https://deno.land/), [introduction in the manual](https://deno.land/manual@v1.25.0/introduction), etc.). I find it unexpected for such a platform not to provide TypeScript runtime integration.

Related:
  - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/1739
  - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/10731
  - Query for potentially-related open issues: [`check typescript api`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+check+typescript+api)
author: jsejcksn

title: `Deno.stdin.close()` should end `Deno.stdin.read()` promises
body:
The following snippet illustrates what I am referring to:
```ts
const buffer = new Uint8Array(1024)
const promise = Deno.stdin.read(buffer)

setTimeout(() => { Deno.stdin.close() })
await promise
```

this snippet will hang indefinitely even though the stdin resource has been closed. For the most part, this does not matter, but when used along with `setRaw`, then it becomes an issue:
```ts
const buffer = new Uint8Array(1024)
Deno.setRaw(Deno.stdin.rid, true)
const promise = Deno.stdin.read(buffer)

setTimeout(() => {
  Deno.setRaw(Deno.stdin.rid, false)
  Deno.stdin.close()
})
await promise
```
this will give the following error:
```
error: Uncaught (in promise) Busy: Resource is unavailable because it is in use by a promise
  Deno.setRaw(reader.rid, false);
       ^
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:170:12)
    at Object.setRaw (deno:runtime/js/40_tty.js:21:10)
```

and attempting to setRaw after closing stdin gives the following error:
```
error: Uncaught (in promise) BadResource: Bad resource ID
  Deno.setRaw(reader.rid, false);
       ^
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:170:12)
    at Object.setRaw (deno:runtime/js/40_tty.js:21:10)
```

so effectively there isnt a great way to close stdin here besides waiting for user input and _then_ closing the reader
author: andykais

title: Ways to get the Deno configuration
body:
I would like and have use cases for a function in the `Deno` namespace that gets the current configuration file.

I thought of 3 forms this could come in (suggest any other ideas you have here):
- `Deno.config` would return an object representation of the current configuration file.
- `Deno.configFile` would return the file path to the current configuration file.
- `Deno.beforeArgs`, `Deno.fullArgs` and `Deno.command` would all return info of how the code was run (see comment below with the explanation).

The last could be used in more situations than to find the configuration file, so I think we should add both.

title: lsp: cannot disable Deno auto import or auto discovery
body:
**Describe the bug**

When I type text into a `ts` file in a project where the Deno extension is enabled, I get autocomplete suggestions for symbols that have not been imported. When I choose one of them, a import is added at the top of my module even though I have the following settings all set to `false`:

```jsonc
{
  "deno.suggest.autoImports": false,
  "deno.suggest.imports.autoDiscover": false,
  "typescript.suggest.autoImports": false,
  "javascript.suggest.autoImports": false,
}
```

**To Reproduce**

1. Set the settings mentioned above.
2. Reference an un-imported symbol in a `ts` file.
3. Observe that a new import for the symbol is added to the top of the file, after you trigger autocomplete the symbol.

**Expected behavior**

No imports should automatically be added to my files please. I also would not like suggestions for symbols that aren't already imported or global.

**Versions**

vscode: 1.69.2 deno: 1.24.3 extension: 3.13.1

author: bakjos

title: Auto-closing async iterables
body:
I see that there's been some discussion related to consistently implementing `return()` on Deno provided implementations of `AsyncIterable`s in the past in #10577 and #14086. From what I can tell the latest thinking was to not remove the deprecation of it in `FsWatcher` so that `FsWatcher` would be consistent with other Deno provided async iterables.

Current Deno provided async iterables are from:
- `Deno.readDir()`
- `Deno.watchFs()` (`Deno.FsWatcher`)
- `Deno.serveHttp()` (`Deno.HttpConn`)
- `Deno.listenDatagram` (`Deno.DatagramConn`, unstable)
- `Deno.listen()` (`Deno.Listener`)
- `Deno.listenTls()` (`Deno.TlsListener`)
- have I missed any?

While looking at all these all (except for perhaps `Deno.readDir()`) would benefit from implementing `return()` to support auto-closing after iterating over the resource.

Instead of removing `return()` from `FsWatcher` to make it consistent with the other async iterables I suggest adding `return()` to the others.

title: flash: passing invalid as a response results in non-catchable errors and hangs
author: kitsonk

title: Deno.serve() - unable to determine what request is associated with an error...
body:
The `onError()` method that can be passed requires a `Response` or `Promise<Response>` return, but lacks the context of what request it is associated with (nor is it clear under what conditions `onError` would be called).  This means it is impossible to return a meaningful response or even log anything meaningful, because you can't associate it with any particular context.

I know we were following the `std/http/serve()` but it actually makes it really difficult to provide a resilient server implementation, where with `Deno.serveHttp()` you can handle each request and response independently, and handle any rejections/exceptions with `.respondWith()`.

With oak, I am having to basically make the solution less robust in handling exceptions to cater to flash.
author: kitsonk

title: Don't cache localhost imports (or give an option to turn it off)
body:
The reason Deno caches remote imports, in my understanding, is (a) so that subsequent runs will be faster, and (b) so that previously imported modules can be imported even offline. Caching can be done without causing problems because we assume the content delivered by the server is (mostly) not going to keep changing on us.

None of this is true when the "remote" is localhost. The content isn't actually remote, so the speedup is probably negligible. Localhost works fine offline. And, most importantly, the assumption that the content being served is not changing is often wrong (i.e. in an active development context, which is what localhost is normally used for).

There's plenty of precedent for treating localhost differently, for example, Chromium treats localhost as a secure context even over http.

I propose that the default behavior be for Deno to *not* cache imports from localhost. Possibly it would be worth adding a flag to force even localhost files to be cached as well, though I'm stumped to come up with a legitimate use case for that.

title: `Deno.serve` hangs on Windows
author: ayame113

title: `Deno.serve` issues
body:
Quick list of things I think we need to address with the new `--unstable` `Deno.serve` API.

- [ ] Rename the API. `Deno.serve` does not clearly communicate the relation to HTTP. Strawman: `Deno.listenAndServeHttp`. Anyone else have other ideas?
- [x] Change signature to `(handler: (Request, ConnInfo) => Response, opts?: ListenAndServeHttpOptions) => Promise<void>` (move handler out of options bag).
- [ ] Rename `cert` and `key` options to `certFile` and `keyFile`.
- [ ] Add `certData` and `keyData` options that don't require reading these files from disk.
- [ ] Errors returned from `server.serve()` should not be swallowed with a `.catch(console.error);`.
- [ ] <s>Errors occuring in `server.serve` should clean up server resources</s>
- [ ] <s>Each server should have it's own date interval, otherwise each invocation to the API creates a different number of resources, which is confusing to users when using the test resource sanitizer.</s>
- [x] #15574
- [x] #15572
- [ ] <s>Remove all of the unwraps when handing values from JS.</s>
- [ ] <s>Flash does not handle socket write failures at all. If a write fails, or only writes partial data, we completely ignore this and just go onto the next write... </s>
- [ ] <s>The safety comments very often don't explain why something is safe. They just say "it's safe".</s>
- [ ] <s>Flash can not handle arbitrary methods.</s>
- [x] #15573
author: lucacasonato

title: `deno cache` fails with JSON imports
body:
**test.ts**

```ts
import asd from "./test.json" assert { type: "json" };
```

**test.json**

```json
{
  "foo": {
    "bar": 1
  }
}
```

```
$ deno --version
deno 1.24.3+ecf3b51 (canary, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.5.218.2
typescript 4.7.4
$ deno cache test.ts
error: Expected ';', '}' or <eof> at file:///mnt/artemis/Projects/github.com/denoland/fresh/test.json:2:8
```

It seems to treat the file as JS instead of as JSON.
author: lucacasonato

title: `jsxImportSource` resolution in config files
author: lucacasonato

title: Enable "--experimental-async-stack-tagging-api" in V8 configuration
body: Trying to upgrade to V8 10.6 in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15547 caused a problem with "unresolved external reference" originating in "createTask" function that is installed by V8. I disabled it using "--noexperimental-async-stack-tagging-api". Since that flag is on by default starting with V8 10.6, we should look into enabling it too. I think a solution would be to disable it only during snapshotting.
author: bartlomieju

title: serde_v8 fast bytes::Bytes conversion panics with bytes 1.2.0+
body:
It seems that newer versions of the `bytes` crate breaks the assumptions made by the transmutes and the layout of the `RawBytes` struct.

To repro:
- Explicitly set `bytes = "=1.2.0"` or `bytes = "=1.2.1"` in serde_v8 Cargo.toml
- Run the serde_v8 tests (`cargo test -p serde_v8`)

The OS will kill the process with `(signal: 4, SIGILL: illegal instruction)`.

Original implementation PR: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14412

cc @AaronO

body: Use recursive setTimeout instead of a setInterval to prevent timer drift (setInterval can drift).
author: littledivy

title: suggestion: `Deno.permissions.querySync`
body:
`Deno.permissions.query` is currently async. A sync version would be useful in contexts where top-level await would otherwise break node. Refs:
* denoland/deno_std#2446
* denoland/deno_std#1495
* denoland/deno_std#1396 (possibly)

In denoland/deno_std#1495 in particular, @bartlomieju mentions that a sync version of this API might not be a good idea. Is this still the case? If so, is there a decent workaround for the above-mentioned refs?
author: iuioiua

title: Add a lint step that ensures Rust crates in the repo don't have circular dependencies
body:
Using https://github.com/denoland/automation we should add another lint step to `tools/lint.js` that ensures that Rust crates have no circular dependencies. One dependency snuck and caused a problem in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15585.

Potentially we should also have a lint that verifies that dependencies in `Cargo.toml` files always have `version` specified.

CC @dsherret

title: Deno 1.25 breaks sqlite3 ffi
body:
```sqlite-ffi.ts
import { Database } from "https://deno.land/x/sqlite3@0.4.4/mod.ts";

const db = new Database("test.db");

console.log("ok");
```

run with Deno 1.25.0 `deno run -A --unstable sqlite-ffi.ts`

```
error: Uncaught TypeError: Invalid FFI pointer type, expected null, integer or BigInt

(base) 
```

run with Deno 1.24.3, 'ok' is printed.

author: curtislarson

title: deno_ffi crate source is missing tinycc files
body:
There seems to be an error with the publishing process, a submodule wasn't included and that makes deno 1.25.0 impossible to install from crates.io (tested on Linux).

The deno_ffi build script breaks because tinycc isn't included in the crate source tarball.

```
CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/cargo-installhXwaLD cargo install -j1 deno
    Updating crates.io index
  Installing deno v1.25.0
   Compiling deno_ffi v0.53.0
error: failed to run custom build command for `deno_ffi v0.53.0`

Caused by:
  process didn't exit successfully: `/tmp/cargo-installhXwaLD/release/build/deno_ffi-4402470cd9da2f6a/build-script-build` (exit status: 101)
  --- stderr
  [/home/g2p/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/deno_ffi-0.53.0/build.rs:29] &tcc_src = "/home/g2p/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/deno_ffi-0.53.0/tinycc"
  thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Os { code: 2, kind: NotFound, message: "No such file or directory" }', /home/g2p/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/deno_ffi-0.53.0/build.rs:34:37
  ```
  
Confirmed that https://crates.io/api/v1/crates/deno_ffi/0.53.0/download doesn't contain a tinycc folder.
author: g2p

title: Add Vite as an integration test for npm support
body: Vite was a great test subject for npm support and we managed to fix numerous edge cases in the last two days thanks to it. Now that we're able to run Vite in Deno we should add an integration test that uses Vite to make sure we have no regressions.

title: npm: error parsing version requirement for dependency
body:
## Steps to Reproduce
```
$ deno --version
deno 1.25.0 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.6.194.5
typescript 4.7.4

$ cat main.ts
import { scaleLinear } from "npm:d3-scale@4.0.2";

$ deno run --unstable main.ts
error: npm package: d3-scale@4.0.2

Caused by:
    0: error parsing version requirement for dependency: d3-time@2.1.1 - 3
    1: expected comma after patch version number, found '-'
```

For reference https://github.com/d3/d3-scale/blob/main/package.json#L35-L40

title: npm URL throws: `Extracted directory of npm tarball was not in output directory.`
author: GJZwiers

title: Incomplete error cause for long cause chains
body:
```text
deno 1.25.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.6.194.5
typescript 4.7.4
```

If the `Error.cause` chain exceeds 2 `Error`s and the last `Error` is caused by a previous `Error`, deno doesn't output the cause of the last `Error`.

`deno run` this code

```typescript
const foo = new Error("foo");
const bar = new Error("bar", { cause: foo });
const baz = new Error("baz", { cause: bar });
foo.cause = bar;
throw baz;
```

Output

```
error: Uncaught Error: baz
const baz = new Error("baz", { cause: bar });
            ^
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:3:13
Caused by: Error: bar
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:2:13
Caused by: Error: foo
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:1:13
```

Note that deno doesn't tell us the cause of `foo`

Expected (Added 2 lines to the end)

```text
error: Uncaught Error: baz
const baz = new Error("baz", { cause: bar });
            ^
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:3:13
Caused by: Error: bar
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:2:13
Caused by: Error: foo
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:1:13
Caused by: Error: bar
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:2:13
```

Same issue if there are 4 `Error`s. But not if there are 2 `Error`s.

For comparison, if there are 2 `Error`s

```typescript
const foo = new Error("foo");
const bar = new Error("bar", { cause: foo });
foo.cause = bar;
throw bar;
```

Output matches expectation

```text
error: Uncaught Error: bar
const bar = new Error("bar", { cause: foo });
            ^
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:2:13
Caused by: Error: foo
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:1:13
Caused by: Error: bar
    at file:///home/lukas/projects/deno/cli/tests/testdata/error_cause_recursive_non_topmost.ts:2:13
```

Found while working on #13413  
In the broken example: `JsError::cause` of `foo` is `None` in `format_js_error_inner()`.
In the working example: `JsError::cause` of `foo` is `bar` in `format_js_error_inner()`. `bar`'s cause is `None`.

Node handles the example as expected (And with `<ref>` and `[Circular]`)

title: `npm:node-fetch@2` doesn't work because of missing `global.Promise`
body:
The following script fails with `Uncaught Error: native promise missing, set fetch.Promise to your favorite alternative`

```ts
import fetch from "npm:node-fetch@2.6.7";
await fetch("https://example.com");
```

It seems caused by missing `global.Promise` object. If I modify the line below (in downloaded node-fetch source):

```js
fetch.Promise = global.Promise;
```
to
```js
fetch.Promise = globalThis.Promise;
```
then it works.

author: littledivy

title: `deno vendor` as cache override
body:
Proposed new design for vendoring in Deno:
- Support `{ "vendor": true }` option in `deno.json`. (Maybe a `--vendor` flag as well for completeness.)
- If it is set, use `./vendor` in place of `$DENO_DIR/deps`.
- If it is set, use readable cache paths for URLs instead of encoding to hashes. `./vendor` should look the same as with `deno vendor`.
- Remove `deno vendor` subcommand.

Advantages:
- Adding `{ "vendor": true }` once to set up a vendor directory is much easier than calling `deno vendor` on every dep bump. It will be updated on normal usage.
- Import maps are non-composable; the import map slot is a valuable resource for users which would no longer be occupied by the vendoring mechanism.
- The vendor directory needs to be known at runtime to capture true dynamic imports.
- Runtime behaviour will be better preserved (see current `import.meta` values from vendored code).
- No subcommand necessary.

```
$ git clone git@github.com:quarto-dev/quarto-cli.git
Cloning into 'quarto-cli'...

error: Expected ';', '}' or <eof> at file:///private/tmp/quarto-cli/src/resources/editor/tools/yaml/tree-sitter-yaml.json:1:8

This error appears in a few different places where we use a dynamic import of type `json` (we have to do it in this way because reasons, if you're curious):

- https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/blob/main/src/core/lib/yaml-intelligence/parsing.ts#L57-L60

author: kitsonk

title: URLPattern breaks on request.url for Unix socket listeners
body:
When using `Deno.listen({ path: "/tmp/file.sock", transport: "unix" })` for HTTP requests, in a request handler, the `request.url` appears as  `http+unix://%2Ftmp%2Ffile%2Esock/url-path`. Unfortunately these Unix-socket URLs won't parse in the _URLPattern_ interface.

While many of the URL router tools I've seen work totally off of paths and creating _URLPattern_ objects from pathnames, I found myself wanting to process URL query parameters, and the simplest way to do this is going back to the full `request.url`. Doing this while also using Unix socket listeners currently requires a touch of search-replace foo. Ideally this would "just work" someday.
author: wwoast

title: It's been a good run boys and gals
body:
I wanna give a big thank you to all of the Deno community, a thank for you the 3 years I have been able to be a part of this fun community! I also want to thank all of the contributors who's made Deno stand out compared to Node and Bun, it's been a good run. Unfortunately it seems that the core team has shifted its focus for Deno towards Node, which in my own opinion is very disappointing. I always thought Deno was special because it challenged one of the most used and liked projects in the world, specifically Node. Almost every single developer has heard of Node, now almost every developer has heard of Deno, they've maybe not thought of using it in big productions or for anything at all but most have heard of it. That's an achievement.

I am announcing my leave from the Deno community due to coming changes to the runtime. I know no one probably cares about this and a lot of people will disagree with my opinions, I hold nothing against that. But I will say this, Deno has been my go to ever since 0.18.0. It's going to be difficult to find something else that will be even half as useful as Deno has been. It's been a hell of a run guys.

Now let me have it, all the "this isn't an airport" memes!

title: Lockfile support for experimental npm specifiers
body:
**Bug:**
Lock files aren't supported when using the new experimental npm modules feature.

**Why post this?**
The release notes below describe the list of specifiers that are supported and those that aren't. Deno cache is not in that list as of writing this ticket.
https://deno.com/blog/v1.25#experimental-npm-support

**Repro Steps**
1. Create a new folder
```bash
mkdir deno-npm-lock-repro
```
2. cd into folder 
```bash
cd deno-npm-lock-repro
````
3. Create a new project 
```bash
deno init
````
4. Create an express app in the main.ts file
```javascript
import express from "npm:express";
const app = express();

app.get("/", function (req, res) {
  res.send("Hello World");
});

app.listen(3000);
console.log("listening on http://localhost:3000/");
```
5. Create a lock file using the deno cache command
```bash
deno cache --reload --lock=lock.json --lock-write --unstable main.ts
```

EXPECTED:
A file is created named lock.json with a list of modules.

ACTUAL:
A file is created named lock.json but it has an empty object.
author: sagea

title: Feature Request: loading ffi shared libs from Uint8Array directly
body:
The idea here is pretty simple, in addition to `Deno.dlopen` accepting a `string` or `URL`, lets also let it accept a `Uint8Array`. The reason for this is so we can start embedding shared libraries in javascript files, so that they can be downloaded at import time. This idea is already widely used with wasm binaries, so in my opinion it isnt a large stretch for ffi libraries either

[edit]
Further background on this idea is that currently we do not have a great way to package ffi shared libs alongside the rest of a deno library. Node solves this with node-gyp, and I believe precompiled binaries in some cases. In this case, we can just piggy-back off of existing javascript module imports.
author: andykais

title: [deno_webgpu] WebGPU GPUSupportedFeatures object doesn't include `has` method
body:
Code:
```
const adapter = await navigator.gpu.requestAdapter();
const device = await adapter?.requestDevice();

console.log(device.features.has('timestamp-query'));
```
Output:
```
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: device.features.has is not a function
console.log(device.features.has('timestamp-query'));
```
Expected output:
True or False

Due to  [WebGPU spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/webgpu/#gpu-supportedfeatures) and webidl spec, `device.features` is a [setlike](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#dfn-set-entries) Object and should includes `has` method, but there is not.

author: eswr

title: depending on npm:ssh2 fails with `Extracted directory...of npm tarball was not in output `
body:
`ssh2` fails to install given a `ssh.ts` file that looks like this:

```ts
import {Client} from 'npm:ssh2';
const conn = new Client()
```
running:

```bash
deno run -A --unstable ssh.ts
```

results in:

```
Download https://registry.npmjs.org/ssh2/-/ssh2-1.11.0.tgz
Download https://registry.npmjs.org/buildcheck/-/buildcheck-0.0.3.tgz
Download https://registry.npmjs.org/cpu-features/-/cpu-features-0.0.4.tgz
error: Failed caching npm package 'ssh2@1.11.0'.

Caused by:
    Extracted directory '/Users/m/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/ssh2' of npm tarball was not in output directory.
```

Happy to provide any additional info that could help the team with this!
author: johnstonmatt

title: Deno v1.25.0 always attempts to create a `npm` directory even when not necessary
body: When running Deno in a Lambda environment, where the filesystem is read-only, I'm seeing Deno fail because it's attempting to create an `npm` directory in the Deno cache directory. In my case, this directory is not being used. Would it be possible to defer this directory creation until if/when the npm directory is going to be written into?

author: lino-levan

title: NPM support did not resolve the node dependencies (knex, better-sqlite3)
body:
## Env
* Deno version:
```
deno 1.25.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.6.194.5
typescript 4.7.4
```

## Phenomenon

Run the code:

```
import knex from 'npm:knex';

const DB = knex({
  client: 'better-sqlite3',
  connection: {
    filename: './localdb.sqlite3'
  }
});

export default DB
```

And throw exception:

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) Error: Cannot find module 'events'
Require stack:
- /home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tarn/3.0.2/dist/Pool.js
- /home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tarn/3.0.2/dist/tarn.js
- /home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/knex/2.3.0/lib/client.js
- /home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/knex/2.3.0/lib/knex-builder/Knex.js
- /home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/knex/2.3.0/lib/index.js
- /home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/knex/2.3.0/knex.js
- /home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/knex/2.3.0/knex.js
    at Function.Module._resolveFilename (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:609:17)
    at Function.Module._load (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:441:29)
    at Module.require (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:652:21)
    at require (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:777:18)
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tarn/3.0.2/dist/Pool.js:6:18)
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tarn/3.0.2/dist/Pool.js:518:4)
    at Module._compile (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:713:36)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:740:12)
    at Module.load (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:630:34)
    at Function.Module._load (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:487:14)
```

Then find the keyword in the `tarn` package:

```
$ grep -r 'events' ~/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tarn/3.0.2
/home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tarn/3.0.2/dist/Pool.d.ts: import { EventEmitter } from 'events';
/home/leroy/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/tarn/3.0.2/dist/Pool.js: const events_1 = require("events");

```

## Expected
Resolve the node dependencies.

// main.ts
import fast, {
  type Context,
} from 'https://deno.land/x/fast@4.0.0-alpha.3/mod.ts'

import {
  getCookies,
} from 'https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/http/cookie.ts'

const app = fast()

app.get('/', async (ctx: Context) => {

  const h = await ctx.request.headers
  const cookie = getCookies(h)
  console.log(cookie)

  return 'no id'
})

await app.serve()

author: dsherret

title: Deno panicks when watching a Koa server using npm compat
author: divmgl

title: [node-compat]: unable to run pre-release versions
body:
On running this (using deno v1.25.1):

```sh
deno run -A --unstable --reload npm:vitepress@1.0.0-alpha.13
```

I'm getting this error:

```txt
Download https://registry.npmjs.org/vitepress
error: Could not find npm package 'vitepress' matching 1.0.0-alpha.13. Try retreiving the latest npm package information by running with --reload
```

On v1.25.0, this step was working fine although there were other errors. I'm not familiar with deno's codebase yet, but it looks this was caused by #15714 ?

Also, running `npm:vitepress` is running 0.x version, so I guess it doesn't respect `latest` tag?

<sup>(PS: there is a typo in retrieving)</sup>
author: brc-dd

title: `deno init -q` does not suppress info logs
body:
Currently there is no difference in output between `deno init` and `deno init --quiet`.

```
deno init --quiet
✅ Project initialized
Run these commands to get started
  cd foo
  deno run main.ts
  deno test
```
author: GJZwiers

title: Add an API for fast HTML escaping
body:
In various benchmarks that @littledivy and @billywhizz did we see that escaping HTML that a huge chunk of time in React SSR examples. 

It's clearly a bottleneck that could be addressed by having a dedicated API for escaping HTML (probably as an op implemented in Rust). 

This should allow us to top various React SSR benchmarks.

title: Cache cjs export and esm global analysis
body:

A bit of work on this: https://github.com/dsherret/deno/tree/node_analysis_cache
author: dsherret

title: Tagged versions in npm specifiers cause a panic
body:
Attempting to run an npm package with a tagged version causes Deno to panic:

```
❯ RUST_BACKTRACE=1 deno run --unstable npm:rome@next

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: linux x86_64
Version: 1.25.1
Args: ["deno", "run", "--unstable", "npm:rome@next"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Invalid version requirement.

Caused by:
    0: Invalid npm specifier version requirement 'next'.
    1: Unexpected character.
         next
         ~', cli/graph_util.rs:86:59
stack backtrace:
   0: rust_begin_unwind
             at /rustc/4b91a6ea7258a947e59c6522cd5898e7c0a6a88f/library/std/src/panicking.rs:584:5
   1: core::panicking::panic_fmt
             at /rustc/4b91a6ea7258a947e59c6522cd5898e7c0a6a88f/library/core/src/panicking.rs:142:14
   2: core::result::unwrap_failed
             at /rustc/4b91a6ea7258a947e59c6522cd5898e7c0a6a88f/library/core/src/result.rs:1805:5
   3: deno::graph_util::GraphData::add_graph
   4: deno::proc_state::ProcState::prepare_module_load::{{closure}}
   5: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
   6: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
   7: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
   8: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
   9: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
  10: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
  11: deno::run_command::{{closure}}
  12: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
  13: deno::main::{{closure}}
  14: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
  15: deno_runtime::tokio_util::run_local
  16: deno::main
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
```

Tagged versions should probably be supported, but at the very least they should gracefully fail instead of panicking.
author: Liamolucko

title: Bug: [Deno 1.25.0 Deno.serve(...)] Deno panicked while using "--watch" after restart after file change
body:
### Description
The issue below happens in these scenarios:
1. Running the server.ts with --watch and modifying the server.ts file triggering a restart.
2. Running the same file twice in separate terminal windows

Both of these cases result in the exact same message.

**I added reproduce steps below the stack trace**

### Error Log W/ RUST_BACKTRACE
```
============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos x86_64
Version: 1.25.0
Args: ["deno", "run", "-A", "--unstable", "--watch", "test-server.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', ext/flash/lib.rs:1316:42
stack backtrace:
   0: _rust_begin_unwind
   1: core::panicking::panic_fmt
   2: core::panicking::panic
   3: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
   4: <extern "C" fn(A0) .> R as v8::support::CFnFrom<F>>::mapping::c_fn
   5: __ZN2v88internal12_GLOBAL__N_119HandleApiCallHelperILb0EEENS0_11MaybeHandleINS0_6ObjectEEEPNS0_7IsolateENS0_6HandleINS0_10HeapObjectEEENS8_INS0_20FunctionTemplateInfoEEENS8_IS4_EEPmi
   6: __ZN2v88internal21Builtin_HandleApiCallEiPmPNS0_7IsolateE
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
```

### Error Log original
```
============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos x86_64
Version: 1.25.0
Args: ["deno", "run", "-A", "--unstable", "--watch", "test-server.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', ext/flash/lib.rs:1316:42
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

### Exact Repro Steps
**Scenario 1: --watch**
1. Create new project folder `mkdir project-test-watch`
2. Navigate to project folder `cd project-test-watch`
3. Create server.ts file
```
echo "
Deno.serve(
  (request) => {
    console.log('url', request.url);
    const url = new URL(request.url);
    console.log('url', url);
    return new Response('hehe');
  },
  { port: 4500 },
);
" > server.ts
```
4. Start server in watch mode `RUST_BACKTRACE=1 deno run -A --unstable --watch server.ts`
5. Modify the server.ts file `echo "\n // update file :)" >> server.ts`
EXPECTED:
Server Restarts fine
ACTUAL:
The error above shows

**Scenario 2: Running file twice**
1. Follow Scenario 1's steps 1-3;
2. Start server `RUST_BACKTRACE=1 deno run -A --unstable server.ts` **--watch is not needed**
3. Open up a another terminal window
4. Run the same deno script to start the server (Make sure the other one is still running) `RUST_BACKTRACE=1 deno run -A --unstable server.ts`
EXPECTED:
Some error akin to `port already in use`
ACTUAL
The Second tab from step 4 outputs the error above.
author: sagea

title: Feature Request: Enable Third Party Subcommands
body:
Hi! I’d love to have the ability to create and install third party subcommands.

I’m imagining functionality like Git or Cargo, where if I have an executable `deno-foo` in my PATH, then running `deno foo —my —flags` will invoke `deno-foo` with `—my —flags` as arguments.

Does something like this already exist within Deno? If not, I’d love to help implement.
author: aunyks

title: [Bug 1.25.1] (Deno.serve) - Only handles one request at a time
author: sagea

title: [Bug 1.25.1] (Deno.serve) panics after responding to curl request with an emoji in the "--data-urlencode" flag
body:
### Description
Deno panics when making a curl request with an amoji in the --data-urlencode. The request **makes it to the server and the response makes it to the client**. (Look at the gif screenshot below.)

Works fine: `curl "http://127.0.0.1:9999/normal"`
Server Blows Up: `curl "http://127.0.0.1:9999/emoji" --data-urlencode "a=🌴"`

### Output (From the example below)
```RUST_BACKTRACE=full deno run -A --unstable serve-test.ts
Server Started... http://127.0.0.1:9999/
⏰ Recieved: http://127.0.0.1:9999/normal
✅ Completed: http://127.0.0.1:9999/normal
⏰ Recieved: http://127.0.0.1:9999/emoji
✅ Completed: http://127.0.0.1:9999/emoji

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Version: 1.25.1
Args: ["deno", "run", "-A", "--unstable", "serve-test.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', ext/flash/lib.rs:715:41

   4:        0x104dd5c24 - std::panicking::default_hook::h18647b59f1a84ee2

   6:        0x104dd6a8b - std::panicking::rust_panic_with_hook::hd9ead35a68ccc55e
   7:        0x104dd68df - std::panicking::begin_panic_handler::{{closure}}::h6fca91c5e1dc2f30
   8:        0x104dd6879 - std::sys_common::backtrace::__rust_end_short_backtrace::h4ff3025d9a0a0490
   9:        0x104dd6835 - _rust_begin_unwind
  10:        0x1061cf893 - core::panicking::panic_fmt::h3d9f795ee387ef8d
  11:        0x1061cf977 - core::panicking::panic::h02eb82771823ab30
  12:        0x10460073c - <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll::h64dca9964049c833

### Example
![emoji-data-urlencode-example](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12538959/188294320-d00357a0-c8bc-45ae-9856-c07dd01682eb.gif)

**Command** `RUST_BACKTRACE=full deno run -A --unstable serve-test.ts`
**SourceCode**
```typescript
Deno.serve(
  (request) => {
    try {
      console.log(`⏰ Recieved: ${request.url}`);
      return new Response('Worked')
    } finally {
      console.log(`✅ Completed: ${request.url}`)
    }
  },
  {
    port: 9999,
    onListen({ hostname, port }) {
      console.log(`Server Started... http://${hostname}:${port}/`);
    }
  } 
)

author: sagea

title: Importing `std::borrow::Borrow` breaks `#[op]`
body:
## To reproduce

1. Clone this repo: https://github.com/shniubobo/deno-op-borrow-bug
2. Run `cargo clippy`. It should exit with no error:
```
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.04s
```
3. Now uncomment the first line of `src/lib.rs`:

```rust
use std::borrow::Borrow;
```

4. Run `cargo clippy` again. It now fails:
```
error[E0282]: type annotations needed for `&Borrowed`
 --> src/lib.rs:5:1
  |
5 | #[op]
  | ^^^^^
  |
  = note: type must be known at this point
  = note: this error originates in the attribute macro `op` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)
help: consider giving `op_state` an explicit type, where the type for type parameter `Borrowed` is specified
  |
5 | #[op]: &Borrowed
  |      +++++++++++

error[E0609]: no field `tracker` on type `&_`
 --> src/lib.rs:5:1
  |
5 | #[op]
  | ^^^^^
  |
  = note: this error originates in the attribute macro `op` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

Some errors have detailed explanations: E0282, E0609.
For more information about an error, try `rustc --explain E0282`.
error: could not compile `deno-op-borrow-bug` due to 2 previous errors
```

## Expected behavior

`#[op]` should work no matter whether `std::borrow::Borrow` is imported or not.

author: sigmaSd

title: [Bug 1.25.1] (Deno has panicked) - Bad url provided to the deno test --reload flag
body:
### Description
Deno panics when the deno test command is run with a bad url in the `--reload` flag


### Failure Cases
**IMPORTANT:** 

> Each of these test cases provides a slightly different output at this location...
> thread 'main' panicked at 'Bad Url: foo', cli/args/flags.rs:3106:7
>                 ⬆️

**Cases:**
1. A bad URL 
`deno test --reload=badurl`
2. Nothing after equal sign 
`deno test --reload=`
3. Single comma after equal sign 
`deno test --reload=,`
4. Trailing comma 
`deno test --reload=http://0.0.0.0:4500/,` 
5. leading comma 
`deno test --reload=,http://0.0.0.0:4500/`
6. Bad URL after good URL 
`deno test --reload=http://0.0.0.0:4500/,badUrl`

### Trace

title: [Bug] Error when running `npm:astro@^1.1.5`
body:
Hi Deno Team, 

I try to run the astro cli without any connection to node to have a 'deno-only' development environment. 

## Problem

Deno Panic when running  
`deno run --unstable --allow-env=NODE_DEBUG npm:astro@^1.1.5 build`

## Simplest Reproduction

The simplest reproduction of the Problem would be:  
`deno run --unstable --allow-env=NODE_DEBUG npm:astro@^1.1.5 help`  
(bc. you don't need any repo setup)

title: FFI stack alignment on Apple silicon
body:
Upstream bug: https://bugs.chromium.org/p/v8/issues/detail?id=13171

V8 aligns everything to 8 byte boundary on arm64 while Apple diverges from that. https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/writing-arm64-code-for-apple-platforms
author: littledivy

title: lsp: make deno scheme easier to parse
body:
Deno scheme sometimes uses `deno:path` this is valid according to the url spec, but for editors it can cause ambiguity because for example `a:b.ts`  is a valid filename in linux

There is a pr to handle this in neovim https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/19967, but it seems to be tricky as it might causes subtle breaking changes.

It would be great if deno changed is usage from `deno:path` to `deno://path` to make it easier to parse and resolve the ambiguity.
author: sigmaSd

title: [Experimental npm support] npm package implicit entry point not resolved correctly (cross-fetch)
body:
Popular **cross-fetch** polyfill NPM package (https://github.com/lquixada/cross-fetch) does not work with with the new npm: specifiers.

### Example of code that results in an error
```typescript
// Import from a package that has cross-fetch as a dependency
import { FetchDocumentLoader } from "npm:jsonld-context-parser";
const loader = new FetchDocumentLoader();
```
### Error message
```
error: Uncaught Error: Cannot find module 'C:\...\deno\npm\registry.npmjs.org\cross-fetch\3.1.5\polyfill\dist\node-ponyfill.js'. Please verify that the package.json has a valid "main" entry
    at tryPackage (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:127:21)
```

### Cause of the error
The cross-fetch polyfill is supposed to be imported as `import 'cross-fetch/polyfill'`. However, the package does not provide `exports` field in the root `package.json`. Instead there is a subfolder named `polyfill` containing a separate `package.json` file (https://github.com/lquixada/cross-fetch/blob/main/polyfill/package.json).

When resolving the module, Deno uses an entrypoint from the root `package.json` file, but also keeps the path to the subfolder in the module name, resulting in final path `cross-fetch/3.1.5/polyfill/dist/node-ponyfill.js` instead of `cross-fetch/3.1.5/dist/node-ponyfill.js`.

### Possible solutions

1. Let Deno resolve `package.json` files from subfolders in npm modules.
2. Upstream fix - adding correct exports entry to `cross-fetch` fixes the issue, but it is probably not happening. There is an open PR from mid-2021: https://github.com/lquixada/cross-fetch/pull/111

The cross-fetch package is quite a big deal as it has 9.6 million weekly downloads on NPM (react has 15.7 million).

### Temporary solution
I have managed to make the package work by modifying its local copy in Deno cache by applying changes contained in the aforementioned PR: https://github.com/lquixada/cross-fetch/pull/111/files

Another possibility would be to remap the module using an import map to e.g. an empty module, but it is probably not yet supported with the new `npm:` specifiers (I wasn't able to make it work).

title: npm classes lose their prototypes
body:
When running the following file `foo.js`:
```js
import {ESLint} from "npm:eslint@8.23.0";

console.log(ESLint.outputFixes);
```
with `deno run --unstable -A foo.js`

title: Stabilize `Deno.setRaw`
body:
I'd like to stabilize the functionality exposed by `Deno.setRaw`. I'd like to make one change prior to stabilization though: `setRaw` should be a method on `Deno.stdin`, not a standalone function that takes `stdin`'s resource id as a parameter.

Proposed API:

```diff,ts
  /** A handle for `stdin`.
   *
   * @category I/O
   */
  export const stdin: Reader & ReaderSync & Closer & {
    readonly rid: number;
    readonly readable: ReadableStream<Uint8Array>;
+   /** **UNSTABLE**: new API, yet to be vetted
+    *
+    * Set TTY to be under raw mode or not. In raw mode, characters are read and
+    * returned as is, without being processed. All special processing of
+    * characters by the terminal is disabled, including echoing input characters.
+    * Reading from a TTY device in raw mode is faster than reading from a TTY
+    * device in canonical mode.
+    *
+    * The `cbreak` option can be used to indicate that characters that correspond
+    * to a signal should still be generated. When disabling raw mode, this option
+    * is ignored. This functionality currently only works on Linux and Mac OS.
+    *
+    * ```ts
+    * Deno.stdin.setRaw(true, { cbreak: true });
+    * ```
+    *
+    * @category I/O
+    */
+   setRaw(mode: boolean, options?: SetRawOptions): void;
  };
```

Proposed timeline:

- Ship the above API change in Deno 1.26
- Stabilize in Deno 1.27

title: lsp: Code autocompletion stops working in WebStorm with Deno (Windows only)
body:
I am using the latest canary build of Deno with WebStorm WS-222.3739.57 and the official Deno plugin on Windows 11. Code autocompletion stops working and the only thing to make it work again is restart the editor. 

I opened a WebStorm issue but their team says that it's a Deno bug. The detailed explanation of the issues and steps how to reproduce it are there:
https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/WEB-57101

Here is a simple project that you need to reproduce the bug:
[webstorm-bug.zip](https://github.com/denoland/deno/files/9506862/webstorm-bug.zip)

Open index.ts, type "state." in function body and autocompletion will break. See the detailed instruction inside index.ts file.
Btw, if you remove the import map, it will work as expected. So it's something wrong with the import map.

The error occurs because of an incorrect Content-Length in a reply received from the deno. There are non ASCII characters in the reply and the length is computed incorrectly.

The issues is very similar to this one that is already fixed but it doesn't fix the problem with WebStorm:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15781

The bug might be reproduced on windows only! Don't try to reproduce it on MacOS/Linux.
author: sergey-tuna

title: WebSocket: configure the maximum message size
body:
I see in the codebase that the WebSocket server has some size limitations for its payloads. More specifically, [the message size is limited to 128 MiB.](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/429759fe8b4207240709c240a8344d12a1e39566/ext/websocket/lib.rs#L97-L98)

It would be great to make this configurable, [maybe via the `UpgradeWebSocketOptions` interface?](https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/Deno.UpgradeWebSocketOptions) We could add a new field `maxMessageSize?: number`

What do you think about that?

title: Add a flag to symlink "node_modules/" directory to CWD
body:
Deno's npm integration doesn't create a local "node_modules/" directory in each project, instead it uses a single global cache just like in case of "http"/"https" imports. This is great for a lot of users who provided feedback on the feature, however it causes some problems when trying to run certain project.

As an example, if we have a frontend project that is build with `vite`, then `vite`'s bundler (`rollup`) expects that all packages will be available in "node_modules/" directory in the project root directory. Because of this fact, currently users need to `npm install` when using `vite` in Deno (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15427#issuecomment-1227199177).

Ideally we'd be able to tell these tools to use Deno's cache, but it's sort of "chicken and egg" problem, maintainers of these tools might be reluctant to add additional codepaths when there isn't much usage of these tools in Deno. On the other hand, even if maintainers were willing to do that, we are missing certain APIs to obtain a path to cache (one can do that, by spawning a subprocess with `deno info` subcommand).

To fix this problem, I propose we add a flag (let's name it "--symlink-npm" for the sake of discussion) that would create a "node_modules/" directory with symlinked packages that reside in the Deno cache (controlled by `DENO_DIR` env var). This would work similar to `pnpm` package manager, which also maintains a single copy of each package on the machine. 

Such flag would be an escape hatch for users who really need to have "node_modules/" directory but wouldn't be a burden for users who don't need (and actually might prefer not to have it).
author: bartlomieju

title: Unable to discover some of npm dependencies
body:
Best seen in an example here: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15427#issuecomment-1227199177

Concretely:
```js
// vite.config.js
import { defineConfig } from 'npm:vite'
import vue from 'npm:@vitejs/plugin-vue'
import "npm:vue@3.2.37/compiler-sfc" // <--- this step is not required when running in Node

// https://vitejs.dev/config/
export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [vue()]
})
```

In the above example `vue` is a peer dependency of `@vitejs/plugin-vue`. However, if we try to run to run a project without that import we get following error:
```
Download https://registry.npmjs.org/@vitejs/plugin-vue/-/plugin-vue-3.1.0.tgz
error when starting dev server:
Error: Failed to resolve vue/compiler-sfc.
@vitejs/plugin-vue requires vue (>=3.2.25) to be present in the dependency tree.
    at resolveCompiler (file:///Users/ib/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/@vitejs/plugin-vue/3.1.0/dist/index.mjs:12:11)
    at Context.buildStart (file:///Users/ib/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/@vitejs/plugin-vue/3.1.0/dist/index.mjs:2587:46)
```

Due to how Deno currently discovers and handles npm dependencies, we are not installing `vue` package that is required to run this program.

Works with a basic example:
```typescript
await Deno.serve({ port: 8000 }, () => new Response());
```

It occurs when making a `POST` request when behind a `next-http-proxy-middleware` as follows:

```typescript
import type { NextApiRequest, NextApiResponse } from "next";
import httpProxyMiddleware from "next-http-proxy-middleware";

const { API_URL } = process.env;
const handler = (req: NextApiRequest, res: NextApiResponse) =>
  httpProxyMiddleware(req, res, {
    target: API_URL,
    pathRewrite: [{ patternStr: "^/api/", replaceStr: "/" }],
  });

export default handler;
export const config = {
  api: { externalResolver: true },
};
```

This is enough to reproduce the crash:
```
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/v1/abc
```

Here is the backtrace:
```
===========================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.25.0
Args: ["deno", "run", "-A", "--unstable", "test.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', ext/flash/lib.rs:846:41
stack backtrace:
   0: _rust_begin_unwind
   1: core::panicking::panic_fmt
   2: core::panicking::panic
   3: <core::future::from_generator::GenFuture<T> as core::future::future::Future>::poll
   4: <extern "C" fn(A0) .> R as v8::support::CFnFrom<F>>::mapping::c_fn
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
```
author: danteissaias

title: Loading npm specifiers in dynamic import that's non-analyzable when other code does not have npm specifiers is somewhat broken
body:
In #15806 we made a few fixes and that surfaced that the code executed in the [`npm_tests.rs#dynamic_import` test](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15806/files#diff-5ea34ccbe091e1aeef61467fedb60a4c6c239c2a6a6f0bf2684d2c3d62ed9cf0R92-R99) wasn't exactly correct, so we disabled this test.

Although, npm specifiers seem to sort of work in dynamic imports that are non-analyzable (when other code does not import from npm specifier), it's actually partially broken. The `ext/node/01_node.js#initialize` function is not called, meaning that the node globals and "native modules" are not initialized. We need to ensure this function is called in this scenario. Bartek was looking into adding a callback to the runtime to help do this, but will investigate it later.

For a test case, re-enable the `dynamic_import` test in npm_tests.rs
author: dsherret

title: Native `Deno.serve()` API for 103 Early Hints / informational responses
author: ayame113

title: [Feature request] Option to only print failing tests
body:
This is a feature request to reduce the verbosity of `deno test` output:

1. Don't print tests that have passed.
2. Don't re-print cached test results.
3. Aggregate successful test.step() output.

Deno's `--quiet` flag could be repurposed or a new option added.

I write a lot of unit tests and many of them are parameterized. I only care about test failures and want to hide all passing test outputs because they interfere with my ability to debug and sometimes even identify real issues. In my tiny project, I don't have _that_ many tests but all the passing test reports totally blows away all other meaningful build and test process outputs. I can redirect Deno's stdout or maybe some kind of magic grep but I'd prefer Deno natively support different logging levels and that `--quiet` was actually quiet (only print warnings and errors, not successes).

Thank you for Deno! <3

## Example

This is some toy text layout engine test code and its output. It's only one file of many. Most of the files include tests that are parameterized with test.step() like this similar to [Jest's test.each()](https://jestjs.io/docs/api#testeachtablename-fn-timeout).

### Test

```ts
import {
  Font,
  FontParser,
  I16,
  I16Box,
  I16XY,
  TextLayout,
  Uint,
} from '../mod.ts';
import { memProp5x5 } from '@/mem';
import { assertEquals } from 'std/testing/asserts.ts';

const font: Font = FontParser.parse(memProp5x5, I16.cast);
Deno.test('layout()', async (test) => {
  for (
    const [caseNumber, [string, width, expected]] of ([
      [
        '',
        I16.max,
        { chars: [], cursor: I16XY.cast(0, 0 * font.lineHeight) },
      ],
      [
        ' ',
        I16.max,
        { chars: [undefined], cursor: I16XY.cast(3, 0 * font.lineHeight) },
      ],
      [
        '\n',
        I16.max,
        { chars: [undefined], cursor: I16XY.cast(0, 1 * font.lineHeight) },
      ],
      [
        'abc def ghi jkl mno',
        I16.max,
        {
          chars: [
            I16Box.cast(0, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(8, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(14, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(18, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(22, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(28, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(32, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(36, 0 * font.lineHeight, 1, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(40, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(44, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(48, 0 * font.lineHeight, 1, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(52, 0 * font.lineHeight, 5, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(58, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(62, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
          ],
          cursor: I16XY.cast(65, 0 * font.lineHeight),
        },
      ],
      [
        'abc def ghi jkl mno',
        10,
        {
          chars: [
            I16Box.cast(0, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(0, 1 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(6, 1 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(0, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 3 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 3 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(8, 3 * font.lineHeight, 1, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 4 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 4 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(8, 4 * font.lineHeight, 1, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 5 * font.lineHeight, 5, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(6, 5 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(0, 6 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
          ],
          cursor: I16XY.cast(3, 6 * font.lineHeight),
        },
      ],
      [
        'abc def ghi jkl mno',
        20,
        {
          chars: [
            I16Box.cast(0, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(8, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 1 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 1 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(8, 1 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(8, 2 * font.lineHeight, 1, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 3 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 3 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),

          cursor: I16XY.cast(13, 4 * font.lineHeight),

      ],

            I16Box.cast(8, 1 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(4, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(8, 2 * font.lineHeight, 1, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(12, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(16, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(20, 2 * font.lineHeight, 1, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 3 * font.lineHeight, 5, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(6, 3 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            I16Box.cast(10, 3 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
          ],
          cursor: I16XY.cast(13, 3 * font.lineHeight),
        },
      ],
      [
        'a  b',
        4,
        {
          chars: [
            I16Box.cast(0, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
          ],
          cursor: I16XY.cast(3, 2 * font.lineHeight),
        },
      ],
      [
        'a  b ',
        4,
        {
          chars: [
            I16Box.cast(0, 0 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
            undefined,
            I16Box.cast(0, 2 * font.lineHeight, 3, font.cellHeight),
            undefined,
          ],
          cursor: I16XY.cast(0, 3 * font.lineHeight),
        },
      ],
    ] as const).entries()
  ) {
    await test.step(`Case ${caseNumber}: string="${string}" width=${width}.`, () =>
      assertEquals(
        TextLayout.layout(font, string, I16.cast(width), I16XY.cast(1, 1)),
        expected as unknown,
      ));
  }
});

Deno.test('layout_word()', async (test) => {

author: iugo

title: perf: #[op] should ban serde_v8 deserialization
body:
serde_v8 is great. we use it for passing arguments and returning structures from Rust... but maybe we use it too much:

- there is an obvious overhead serializing object keys for structs. We end up flattening arguments in an op. eg: #15723 #15776 
- simple enums are represented as strings in JS, bad for performance.
- for extremely critical ops, we want to inline arguments. eg: #15681 #15731 

The `#[op]` macro should not allow serde arguments unless `#[op(serde)]` is used (which is useful sometimes). This will force developers working on Deno to not fall off this performance cliff.

deno run --unstable --allow-env --allow-read npm:mocha@10.0.0 test.js

import assert from "assert";
describe("Array", function () {

    it("should return -1 when the value is not present", function () {
      assert.equal([1, 2, 3].indexOf(4), -1);
    });
  });
});

### package.json
```json
{
  "type": "module"
}

### file permission

drwxr-xr-x   .

-rw-r--r--   package.json

author: jmandel

title: Writing JS strings to resources, Deno.encode and Deno.encodeInto
body:
## Issue

I was doing some investigation into network perf using Deno FFI and stdio perf in general and came up against a performance roadblock which is relevant to anywhere we are writing JS strings to ops resources using send or write syscalls and the like.

If you look at the benchmark script [here](https://github.com/denoland/performance-team/blob/main/benchmarks/io/02-strings/bench.js#L34) you can see the following scenarios benchmarked. All scenarios write to /dev/null using FFI fast call.

## Benchmarks

- **static_buffer**: writing a pre-existing buffer with the string pre-encoded - nothing should be faster than this afaik
- **encode_dynamic_string**: writing a dynamically generated string encoded into a new Uint8Array using ```Deno.core.encode```
- **encode_static_string**: writing a static string encoded into a new Uint8Array using ```Deno.core.encode```
- **encode_into_dynamic_string**: writing a dynamically generated string encoded into an existing Uint8Array using ```Deno.core.ops.op_encoding_encode_into```
- **encode_into_static_string**: writing a static string encoded into an existing Uint8Array using ```Deno.core.ops.op_encoding_encode_into```

## Results
![results](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/159073/190502364-d8cf38c1-6d61-4daa-b642-54f03c37c616.png)

## Flamegraphs

Flamegraphs to compare the three static string scenarios are here which show clearly the overhead involved in both ```Deno.core.encode``` and ```Deno.core.ops.op_encoding_encode_into``` compared to the baseline of writing a pre-encoded buffer and using Deno.writeSync, which seems to be fastest option currently exposed to userland.

```
const tests = {
  static_buffer_writesync: () => Deno.writeSync(rid, payload),
  static_buffer_ffi: () => fs.write(fd, payload, payload.length),
  encode_dynamic_string: () => {
    const payload = encode(`${TEXT}${byteLength(str)}${CRLF2}${str}`)
    fs.write(fd, payload, payload.length)
  },
  encode_static_string: () => {
    const payload = encode(resp)
    fs.write(fd, payload, payload.length)
  },
  encode_into_dynamic_string: () => {
    const { written } = ops.op_encoding_encode_into(`${TEXT}${byteLength(str)}${CRLF2}${str}`, buf)
    fs.write(fd, buf, written)
  },
  encode_into_static_string: () => {
    const { written } = ops.op_encoding_encode_into(resp, buf)
    fs.write(fd, buf, written)
  }
}
```
### static_buffer_writesync
![static_buffer_writesync](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/159073/190502916-5d7aa789-9068-46e3-b371-19d46f32d411.svg)

### static_buffer_ffi
![static_buffer_ffi](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/159073/190502959-37a3d899-6d9e-4b35-9c21-1adc6d5e91c2.svg)

### encode_static_string
![encode_static_string](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/159073/190503003-60d395c8-28ab-4928-948e-a36fae971858.svg)

### encode_into_static_string
![encode_into_static_string](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/159073/190503051-d0f611f9-27f1-49e5-bd7d-9383732ed616.svg)

## Discussion

From the benchmarks we can see:
- calling ```Deno.core.encode``` on the static string in question causes a **~58%** drop in throughput and takes **2.3x** longer
- calling ```Deno.core.ops.op_encoding_encode_into``` causes a **~64%** drop in throughput and takes **3.27x** longer
- JS String concatentation adds **~15%** overhead

From the flamegraphs we can see:
- with a static buffer, the program spends **88%** of time in ```__libc_write```
- with ```Deno.core.encode``` the program spends **35%** in ```__libc_write```
- with ```Deno.core.ops.op_encoding_encode_into``` the program spends **28%** in ```__libc_write```

There are a few of issues I think this raises:
- ```Deno.core.encode``` is expensive because it does a lot of work - it allocates a BackingStore, creates an ArrayBuffer and wraps it in a Uint8Array and returns that to JS. This is per spec so not much we can do about it other than to avoid using it where we don't need to.
-  ```Deno.core.ops.op_encoding_encode_into``` **should** be faster than Deno.core.encode as it is working with a pre-existing buffer and only needs to do a memcpy from the string into the buffer. The overhead here is likely down to it being a native op and being wrapped in serde_v8.
- We need a method for writing a JS string directly to an ops resource rid. I came across the same issue with just-js and had to do something like this. This is significantly quicker than first encoding into a buffer and then writing the buffer as far as i can remember.

```C++
  String::Utf8Value str(isolate, args[1]);
  args.GetReturnValue().Set(Integer::New(isolate, write(fd, 
    *str, str.length())));
```

## Planned Actions

- add an [op-v8] api call ```Deno.core.encode_into (u8, String)``` into ops_builtin_v8.rs which does encode_into without serde_v8.
- add a ```Deno.core.write_utf8 (rid, string)``` api call to [ops_builtin_v8.rs](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/core/ops_builtin_v8.rs) which will take a copy of the utf8 bytes inside the v8 string passed to it and write them to the resource identified by rid, as in the C++ snippet above.

The disadvantage of the second approach is, afaik, it will not be optimised as a fast call because fast call api has no string support yet. But it would be worth knowing if it is faster than the other methods above even without the fast call so I propose doing it as a draft PR and see if it improves perf. It should be pretty easy to implement.

author: curtislarson

title: Feature request: Web Locks API
body:
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Locks_API

> The Web Locks API allows scripts running in one tab or worker to asynchronously acquire a lock, hold it while work is performed, then release it. While held, no other script executing in the same origin can acquire the same lock, which allows a web app running in multiple tabs or workers to coordinate work and the use of resources.

I've been looking for some ways to manage access to a single resource, and this seems like it could fill quite a lot of the gap left by shared workers.

}

Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10.15; rv:104.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/104.0"
]]

portrule = shortport.port_or_service({ 80, 443, 9000 }, { "http", "https" }, "tcp", "open")

local function merge_table(...)
	local all = {}
	for _, s in ipairs(...) do
		for k, v in pairs(s) do
			all[k] = v
		end
	end
	return all
end

action = function(host, port)
	local domain = host.targetname or host.name or host.ip
	local result = { count = 0, success_list = {} }
	local headers = { ["user-agent"] = USER_AGENT }
	local pipes, auth

	auth = stdnse.get_script_args("auth-token")
	if auth ~= nil then
		stdnse.debug("auth-token = %q", auth)
		headers["authorization"] = "Bearer " .. auth
	end

	auth = stdnse.get_script_args("auth-cookie")
	if auth ~= nil then
		stdnse.debug("auth-cookie = %q", auth)
		headers["cookie"] = auth
	end

	for _, path in ipairs(GENERAL_PATHS) do
		-- local uri = base .. path
		local query = url.build_query({ query = INTRO_QUERY })

		-- General GET request
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path .. "?" .. query, {
			header = headers
		}, pipes, "GET")

		-- GET request with base64 encoded
		query = url.build_query({ query = base64.enc(INTRO_QUERY) })
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path .. "?" .. query, {
			header = headers
		}, pipes, "GET")

		-- General POST form-data
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path, {
			header = merge_table(headers, {
				["content-type"] = "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
			}),
			content = url.build_query({ query = INTRO_QUERY }),
		}, pipes, "POST")

		-- General POST json
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path, {
			header = merge_table(headers, {
				["content-type"] = "application/json",
			}),
			content = json.generate({ query = INTRO_QUERY }),
		}, pipes, "POST")
	end

	local responses = http.pipeline_go(domain, port.number, pipes)

	stdnse.debug("count = %d", #responses)

	for i, res in ipairs(responses) do
		stdnse.debug("status = %d", res.status)
		result.count = result.count + 1

		if res.status == 200 then
			table.insert(result.success_list, pipes[i].path)
		end
	end

	return { result, header = headers }
end

author: xbol0

title: serde_v8: StructAccess implements SeqAccess
body:
Is there a reason that `serde_v8` deserializes structs as a sequence of it's values?

That is to say `StructAccess` implements `serde::de::SeqAccess` instead of `serde::de::MapAccess`.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2fc88aba1ac69dd72e0d516de22157f72778b2ef/serde_v8/de.rs#L559

And as a consequence, later interpreting the struct as a sequence:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2fc88aba1ac69dd72e0d516de22157f72778b2ef/serde_v8/de.rs#L373-L381

The implementation discards information about the key for each value, and I'm not sure whether this is an intentional design decision.

I would be happy to implement `serde::de::MapAccess` for `StructAccess` instead if this is the case.
author: Suficio

title: deno compile ignores custom ca-certificates on linux when downloading runtimes
body:
I am using the official `denoland/deno:alpine` docker image to build some cross-platform binaries.

The build must run behind a proxy that performs SSL re-encryption, and requires a custom cert to be trusted. I've added this cert to the docker image like so:

```Dockerfile
FROM denoland/deno:alpine-1.25.2

ADD .certs/dummy .certs/*.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/
RUN chmod 644 /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/*.crt \
    && cat /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/*.crt \
    >> /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
    
...
```

I'm trying to run something like:

```sh
deno --unstable compile --cert="/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt" -o build/mod-x86_64-apple-darwin \
        --target x86_64-apple-darwin \
        mod.ts
```

Without the `--cert` arg, the build fails much earlier when downloading imports. With the `--cert` arg, it successfully downloads and caches the imports, but fails to download the runtime binary.

```
Checking https://dl.deno.land/release/v1.25.2/deno-x86_64-apple-darwin.zip
Sending fatal alert BadCertificate
error: error sending request for url (https://dl.deno.land/release/v1.25.2/deno-x86_64-apple-darwin.zip): error trying to connect: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer

Caused by:
    0: error trying to connect: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer
    1: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer
```

I've also tried using the following in my dockerfile, but got the same result as I did with `--cert`
```Dockerfile
ENV DENO_TLS_CA_STORE="system"
```

My suspicion is that the `--cert` arg as well as the `DENO_TLS_CA_STORE` env var both take effect for import resolution, but not for the download of the actual binary runtime when everything is bundled.
